topics.title: Basic Electronics and Electrical Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT864 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Electronics and Electrical Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Basic Electronics and Electrical Components" in Mechatronics?

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for **Mechatronics (PEAGT864)**, the topic *Basic Electronics and Electrical Components* refers to the foundational passive and active electronic devices that act as the **"nervous system"** of any mechatronic system. Mechatronics, by definition, is the synergistic integration of Mechanical + Electronic + Computer + Control engineering. Before any sensor signal can be processed, before any actuator can be driven, and before any microcontroller can issue commands, the underlying signals must pass through resistors, capacitors, inductors, diodes, transistors, and op-amps.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Basic Electronics and Electrical Components constitute the discrete and integrated devices used to control, condition, switch, amplify, or convert electrical signals. They form the **hardware bridge** between physical phenomena (sensors) and computational intelligence (controllers), and between controllers and physical motion (actuators).

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of This Topic

The topic is conventionally divided into three pedagogical pillars:

1. **Passive Components** — Resistors, Capacitors, Inductors (do not amplify; only store or dissipate energy).
2. **Active Components** — Diodes, BJTs, MOSFETs, Op-Amps (require external power; can amplify).
3. **Interfacing & Protection Devices** — Relays, Switches, Breadboards, Fuses, Optocouplers (for signal routing, isolation, and protection).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Plumbing & Plumbing Valves View

Imagine your mechatronic system as a **water network**:

| Electronics Element | Plumbing Analogy | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Resistor** | Narrow pipe section | Restricts flow; drops pressure |
| **Capacitor** | Storage tank | Stores and releases fluid quickly |
| **Inductor** | Heavy flywheel | Resists sudden changes in flow |
| **Diode** | One-way check valve | Current flows in one direction only |
| **Transistor** | Motorized valve | Small control signal opens/closes a large flow |
| **Op-Amp** | Pressure amplifier | Boosts weak signals to strong ones |
| **Relay** | Solenoid-actuated giant valve | Small circuit switches a big circuit |
| **Switch** | Manual tap | Opens/closes the circuit manually |

> [!NOTE]
> This "plumbing" mental model is widely used in KTU Board question papers for the **Part A 3-mark questions**, where students are often asked to "explain the function of X in simple terms." Mastery of the analogy guarantees marks in such questions.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Mechatronics

In a typical **CNC machine**, **robotic arm**, **autonomous vehicle**, or **drone**, the following chain of events occurs:

> **Physical World → Sensor (transducer) → Signal Conditioning (electronics) → Microcontroller/PLC (logic) → Driver Electronics → Actuator (motor/valve)**

**Every single block** in this chain is built from the components discussed in this module. Without understanding them, you cannot troubleshoot, design, or even read a mechatronic schematic.

## 1.5 Standard Units and Constants (Must Memorize for KTU)

| Symbol | Quantity | Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $V$ | Voltage (potential difference) | **Volt (V)** |
| $I$ | Current | **Ampere (A)** |
| $R$ | Resistance | **Ohm (Ω)** |
| $C$ | Capacitance | **Farad (F)** |
| $L$ | Inductance | **Henry (H)** |
| $P$ | Power | **Watt (W)** |
| $f$ | Frequency | **Hertz (Hz)** |
| $q$ | Electron charge | **$1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The electron charge constant $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C has appeared in multiple KTU university exams for deriving drift velocity or current density in semiconductor problems. Memorize it.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Resistors — The Most Fundamental Passive Component

A **resistor** is a two-terminal device that obeys **Ohm's Law** and opposes the flow of direct current (DC) or alternating current (AC).

### 2.1.1 Ohm's Law (Foundational Theorem)

$$\begin{aligned}
V &= I \times R
\end{aligned}$$

Where:
- $V$ = Voltage across the resistor (Volts)
- $I$ = Current through the resistor (Amperes)
- $R$ = Resistance (Ohms)

### 2.1.2 Resistor Color Code (KTU Favourite — 3-Mark Question)

A standard 4-band resistor is read as:
> **Band 1 | Band 2 | Band 3 (Multiplier) | Band 4 (Tolerance)**

| Color | Digit | Multiplier | Tolerance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Black | 0 | $10^0 = 1$ | — |
| Brown | 1 | $10^1$ | ±1% |
| Red | 2 | $10^2$ | ±2% |
| Orange | 3 | $10^3$ | — |
| Yellow | 4 | $10^4$ | — |
| Green | 5 | $10^5$ | ±0.5% |
| Blue | 6 | $10^6$ | ±0.25% |
| Violet | 7 | $10^7$ | ±0.1% |
| Grey | 8 | $10^8$ | — |
| White | 9 | $10^9$ | — |
| Gold | — | $10^{-1}$ | ±5% |
| Silver | — | $10^{-2}$ | ±10% |

### 2.1.3 Power Dissipation in Resistor

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= I^2 R = \frac{V^2}{R} = V \cdot I
\end{aligned}$$

This is critical when **selecting the wattage rating** of a resistor. A common KTU pitfall is specifying a 0.25 W resistor where a 1 W resistor is required.

### 2.1.4 Series and Parallel Combinations

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{series}} &= R_1 + R_2 + \cdots + R_n \\
\frac{1}{R_{\text{parallel}}} &= \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} + \cdots + \frac{1}{R_n}
\end{aligned}$$

For **two resistors in parallel**, the quick "product over sum" form is:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{parallel}} = \frac{R_1 \cdot R_2}{R_1 + R_2}
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 2.2 Capacitors — Energy Storage in the Electric Field

A **capacitor** stores energy in the form of an electric field between two conductive plates separated by a dielectric.

### 2.2.1 Fundamental Capacitance Equation

$$\begin{aligned}
C = \frac{Q}{V} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}
\end{aligned}$$

Where:
- $C$ = Capacitance in Farads
- $Q$ = Charge stored in Coulombs
- $V$ = Voltage across the plates
- $\varepsilon_0$ = Permittivity of free space = **$8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m**
- $\varepsilon_r$ = Relative permittivity (dielectric constant)
- $A$ = Plate area
- $d$ = Distance between plates

### 2.2.2 Charging and Discharging Equations (RC Circuit)

For a capacitor charging through a resistor from a source $V_s$:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_C(t) &= V_s \left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right) \\
I_C(t) &= \frac{V_s}{R} \, e^{-t/RC}
\end{aligned}$$

For a capacitor discharging through a resistor:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_C(t) &= V_0 \, e^{-t/RC} \\
I_C(t) &= -\frac{V_0}{R} \, e^{-t/RC}
\end{aligned}$$

The **time constant** $\tau = RC$ defines how quickly the capacitor charges/discharges. After $5\tau$, the capacitor is considered fully charged (>99%).

### 2.2.3 Series and Parallel Capacitances

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{C_{\text{series}}} &= \frac{1}{C_1} + \frac{1}{C_2} + \cdots + \frac{1}{C_n} \\
C_{\text{parallel}} &= C_1 + C_2 + \cdots + C_n
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the **opposite** logic to resistors! Capacitors add in **parallel** (more area), resistors add in **series**. This is a classic KTU trick question.

### 2.2.4 Energy Stored

$$\begin{aligned}
E = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 = \frac{Q^2}{2C}
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 2.3 Inductors — Energy Storage in the Magnetic Field

An **inductor** is a coil of wire that stores energy in a magnetic field when current flows through it.

### 2.3.1 Voltage-Current Relationship

$$\begin{aligned}
V_L = L \frac{dI}{dt}
\end{aligned}$$

The inductor **opposes sudden changes in current**. This is the foundation of all inductive switching (relays, solenoids, motor coils).

### 2.3.2 RL Circuit Transients

For an inductor energizing through a resistor:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_L(t) &= \frac{V_s}{R} \left(1 - e^{-t \cdot R/L}\right) \\
\tau &= \frac{L}{R}
\end{aligned}$$

### 2.3.3 Energy Stored

$$\begin{aligned}
E = \frac{1}{2} L I^2
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 2.4 Diodes — The One-Way Valve

A **diode** is a two-terminal semiconductor device that allows current to flow in only one direction (forward bias) and blocks it in the reverse direction.

### 2.4.1 Ideal Diode Equation (Shockley Equation)

$$\begin{aligned}
I_D = I_S \left( e^{\frac{V_D}{n V_T}} - 1 \right)
\end{aligned}$$

Where:
- $I_S$ = Reverse saturation current (typically $10^{-12}$ to $10^{-6}$ A)
- $V_D$ = Voltage across the diode
- $V_T$ = Thermal voltage = **$kT/q \approx 25.85$ mV at 300 K**
- $n$ = Ideality factor (1 to 2)
- $k$ = Boltzmann's constant = **$1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K**

### 2.4.2 Important Diode Types for KTU

| Diode Type | Symbol | Function | Application in Mechatronics |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PN Junction** | ▷\| | Rectification | AC to DC power supply |
| **Zener** | ▷\| (bent) | Voltage regulation | Reference voltage, protection |
| **LED** | ▷\| (arrows) | Light emission | Indicators, optocouplers |
| **Photodiode** | Reverse symbol | Light sensing | Optical encoders, line-following robots |
| **Schottky** | ▷\| (barbed) | Fast switching | High-frequency SMPS, solar panels |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Zener diode** operated in **reverse breakdown** acts as a voltage regulator. If $V_Z$ is the Zener voltage, the output is clamped at $V_Z$ regardless of input variations. This is a 3-mark KTU favourite.

---

## 2.5 Transistors — The Amplifying Heart

A **transistor** is a three-terminal semiconductor device used for **switching** and **amplification**. Two major families:

### 2.5.1 BJT (Bipolar Junction Transistor)

Has three terminals: **Base (B), Collector (C), Emitter (E)**. Two flavors: NPN and PNP.

$$\begin{aligned}
I_C &= \beta \cdot I_B \\
I_E &= I_C + I_B = (\beta + 1) I_B
\end{aligned}$$

Where $\beta$ (or $h_{FE}$) is the **DC current gain**, typically 50 to 300.

**BJT as a Switch (Saturation/Cutoff)** — the most common KTU question:
- **Cutoff:** $V_{BE} < 0.7\,V$ → $I_B = 0$ → Transistor OFF
- **Saturation:** $V_{CE} \approx 0.2\,V$ → Transistor ON (acts like closed switch)

### 2.5.2 MOSFET (Metal-Oxide Semiconductor FET)

Three terminals: **Gate (G), Drain (D), Source (S)**. Voltage-controlled (BJT is current-controlled).

$$\begin{aligned}
I_D &= k \left(V_{GS} - V_{th}\right)^2 \quad \text{(in saturation)}
\end{aligned}$$

Where $V_{th}$ is the **threshold voltage** (typically 1 to 4 V). When $V_{GS} > V_{th}$, the MOSFET turns ON.

> [!NOTE]
> MOSFETs are preferred over BJTs in **motor drivers, H-bridges, and PWM control** of mechatronic actuators because of their high input impedance and low on-state resistance $R_{DS(on)}$.

---

## 2.6 Operational Amplifiers (Op-Amps)

An **op-amp** is a high-gain DC-coupled differential amplifier with two inputs (inverting $-$ and non-inverting $+$) and one output.

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{out} = A_{OL} (V^+ - V^-)
\end{aligned}$$

Where $A_{OL}$ is the **open-loop gain** (typically $10^5$ to $10^6$).

### 2.6.1 Key KTU Configurations

| Configuration | Gain Formula | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Inverting Amplifier** | $A_v = -R_f / R_{in}$ | Phase-inverted signal amplification |
| **Non-Inverting Amplifier** | $A_v = 1 + R_f / R_{in}$ | Buffer and gain stage |
| **Voltage Follower (Buffer)** | $A_v = 1$ | Impedance matching |
| **Summing Amplifier** | $V_o = -R_f \sum (V_i / R_i)$ | Adder circuit |
| **Differential Amplifier** | $V_o = (R_f/R_{in})(V^+ - V^-)$ | Subtractor (sensor bridge readout) |
| **Integrator** | $V_o = -\frac{1}{R_1 C_f} \int V_{in} \, dt$ | PID control (I term) |
| **Differentiator** | $V_o = -R_f C_1 \frac{dV_{in}}{dt}$ | Rate sensing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **integrator and differentiator** are direct bridges to **PID control in mechatronics**. This is a frequently asked 14-mark question where students must derive the output and discuss its application in motor position control.

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master This Table)

| Component | Governing Equation | Units | Key Insight |
|---|---|---|---|
| Resistor | $V = IR$ | $\Omega$ | Energy dissipator |
| Resistor Power | $P = I^2 R$ | W | Select proper wattage |
| Capacitor | $C = Q/V$ | F | Energy storer (E field) |
| Capacitor Energy | $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$ | J | Increases with V² |
| Capacitor Time Constant | $\tau = RC$ | s | Charging speed |
| Inductor | $V_L = L \, dI/dt$ | H | Opposes current change |
| Inductor Energy | $E = \frac{1}{2}LI^2$ | J | Stored in magnetic field |
| Diode | $I = I_S(e^{V_D/nV_T} - 1)$ | A | One-way current |
| Thermal Voltage | $V_T = kT/q$ | V | **25.85 mV at 300 K** |
| BJT | $I_C = \beta I_B$ | A | Current amplifier |
| MOSFET | $I_D = k(V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$ | A | Voltage-controlled switch |
| Op-Amp (Inverting) | $A_v = -R_f/R_{in}$ | — | Phase inversion |
| Op-Amp (Non-Inverting) | $A_v = 1 + R_f/R_{in}$ | — | Same phase |
| Op-Amp Integrator | $V_o = -\frac{1}{R_1 C_f} \int V_{in}\,dt$ | V | Used in PID |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Mechatronics Utility

| Application | Components Used |
|---|---|
| DC Motor Speed Control (PWM) | MOSFET, gate driver, flyback diode |
| Sensor Signal Conditioning | Op-amp, filter (R + C), voltage regulator (Zener) |
| Relay-Based Solenoid Driving | BJT/MOSFET switch, flyback diode, snubber RC |
| Line-Following Robot | Photodiodes, comparators (op-amp) |
| Encoder Signal Reading | Schmitt trigger (op-amp), RC filter |
| Power Supply (12V/5V/3.3V) | Bridge rectifier (diodes), filter capacitor, Zener/LDO |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Equivalent Resistance in Series and Parallel

**Problem:** Two resistors $R_1 = 100\,\Omega$ and $R_2 = 200\,\Omega$ are connected in (a) series and (b) parallel. Find the equivalent resistance in each case.

### Part (a) — Series Combination

**Step 1:** Recall the definition of series connection. The same current $I$ flows through both resistors, and the total voltage is the sum of individual voltage drops.

$$V_{\text{total}} = V_1 + V_2$$

**Step 2:** Apply Ohm's Law to each resistor.

$$V_1 = I R_1, \quad V_2 = I R_2$$

**Step 3:** Substitute back into the total voltage equation.

$$V_{\text{total}} = I R_1 + I R_2 = I (R_1 + R_2)$$

**Step 4:** By definition of equivalent resistance, $V_{\text{total}} = I \cdot R_{eq}$.

$$I \cdot R_{eq} = I (R_1 + R_2)$$

**Step 5:** Cancel $I$ on both sides (assuming $I \neq 0$).

$$R_{eq} = R_1 + R_2$$

**Step 6:** Plug in numerical values.

$$R_{eq} = 100 + 200 = 300\,\Omega$$

### Part (b) — Parallel Combination

**Step 1:** In parallel, the same voltage $V$ appears across both resistors, and the total current splits.

$$I_{\text{total}} = I_1 + I_2$$

**Step 2:** Apply Ohm's Law.

$$I_1 = \frac{V}{R_1}, \quad I_2 = \frac{V}{R_2}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute.

$$I_{\text{total}} = \frac{V}{R_1} + \frac{V}{R_2} = V \left( \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} \right)$$

**Step 4:** By definition $I_{\text{total}} = V / R_{eq}$.

$$\frac{V}{R_{eq}} = V \left( \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} \right)$$

**Step 5:** Cancel $V$ and solve.

$$\frac{1}{R_{eq}} = \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} \;\Rightarrow\; R_{eq} = \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 6:** Plug in numerical values.

$$R_{eq} = \frac{100 \times 200}{100 + 200} = \frac{20000}{300} = 66.67\,\Omega$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: RC Charging Transients

**Problem:** A $C = 100\,\mu F$ capacitor is charged through $R = 10\,k\Omega$ from a $V_s = 12\,V$ DC source. Find (i) the time constant, (ii) the capacitor voltage at $t = 0.5$ s, and (iii) the time required to reach 90% of $V_s$.

### Step 1 — KVL Around the Loop

The loop equation is:

$$V_s = I R + V_C$$

**Step 2 — Express Current in Terms of $V_C$**

For a capacitor:

$$I = C \frac{dV_C}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the Loop Equation**

$$V_s = R C \frac{dV_C}{dt} + V_C$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange into Standard Linear ODE Form**

$$R C \frac{dV_C}{dt} = V_s - V_C$$

$$\frac{dV_C}{dt} = \frac{V_s - V_C}{RC}$$

**Step 5 — Separate Variables and Integrate**

$$\frac{dV_C}{V_s - V_C} = \frac{dt}{RC}$$

Integrating from $0$ to $t$ (with $V_C(0) = 0$):

$$-\ln(V_s - V_C) + \ln(V_s) = \frac{t}{RC}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $V_C(t)$**

$$\ln\left(\frac{V_s - V_C}{V_s}\right) = -\frac{t}{RC}$$

$$\frac{V_s - V_C}{V_s} = e^{-t/RC}$$

$$V_C(t) = V_s \left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right)$$

This is the canonical charging equation.

### Step 7 — Numerical Computations

**Time constant:**

$$\tau = R C = (10 \times 10^3) \times (100 \times 10^{-6}) = 1\,\text{second}$$

**Voltage at $t = 0.5\,s$:**

$$V_C(0.5) = 12 \left(1 - e^{-0.5/1}\right) = 12 \left(1 - e^{-0.5}\right)$$

$$e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$$

$$V_C(0.5) = 12 \times (1 - 0.6065) = 12 \times 0.3935 = 4.722\,\text{V}$$

**Time to reach 90% of $V_s$:**

Set $V_C(t) = 0.9 V_s$:

$$0.9 V_s = V_s \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

$$e^{-t/\tau} = 0.1$$

$$-\frac{t}{\tau} = \ln(0.1) = -2.3026$$

$$t = 2.3026 \cdot \tau = 2.3026 \times 1 = 2.30\,\text{seconds}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The general rule: time to reach **63.2%** is $1\tau$, **86.5%** is $2\tau$, **95%** is $3\tau$, **98.2%** is $4\tau$, and **99.3%** is $5\tau$. This is asked verbatim in many KTU exams.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Thermal Voltage $V_T$ at Room Temperature

**Step 1:** Start from the thermodynamic definition of the thermal voltage.

$$V_T = \frac{kT}{q}$$

**Step 2:** Plug in the values at $T = 300\,K$ (room temperature).

- $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}\,\text{J/K}$
- $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\,\text{C}$

$$V_T = \frac{(1.381 \times 10^{-23}) \times 300}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the numerator.

$$1.381 \times 300 = 414.3$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 414.3 \times 10^{-23} = 4.143 \times 10^{-21}$$

**Step 4:** Divide by the denominator.

$$V_T = \frac{4.143 \times 10^{-21}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = \frac{4.143}{160.2} = 0.02586\,V = 25.86\,mV$$

This is why the approximation $V_T \approx 26\,mV$ is widely used in diode circuit analysis.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Op-Amp Inverting Amplifier Gain

**Problem:** Derive the closed-loop gain of an inverting op-amp configuration.

### Step 1 — Identify the Virtual Ground

Because the op-amp has very high open-loop gain and the negative feedback forces the differential input to nearly zero, the inverting input ($V^-$) is held at the same potential as the non-inverting input ($V^+$). Since $V^+$ is grounded, $V^- = 0$. This is the **virtual ground** concept.

### Step 2 — Apply KCL at the Inverting Node

The currents entering the inverting node must sum to zero (no current flows into the ideal op-amp input).

$$I_{in} + I_f = 0$$

### Step 3 — Express Each Current Using Ohm's Law

$$I_{in} = \frac{V_{in} - V^-}{R_{in}} = \frac{V_{in}}{R_{in}}$$

(since $V^- = 0$)

$$I_f = \frac{V^- - V_{out}}{R_f} = \frac{-V_{out}}{R_f}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute into KCL

$$\frac{V_{in}}{R_{in}} - \frac{V_{out}}{R_f} = 0$$

### Step 5 — Solve for the Gain

$$\frac{V_{out}}{R_f} = \frac{V_{in}}{R_{in}}$$

$$V_{out} = -\frac{R_f}{R_{in}} V_{in}$$

$$\boxed{A_v = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_{in}}}$$

The **negative sign** indicates a 180° phase inversion between input and output.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: LED Series Resistor Selection

**Problem:** A red LED with forward voltage $V_F = 2.0\,V$ and desired forward current $I_F = 20\,mA$ is to be driven from a $V_s = 5\,V$ microcontroller pin. Find the required series resistor $R_S$.

**Step 1:** Apply KVL around the series loop.

$$V_s = V_R + V_F$$

**Step 2:** Express the resistor voltage.

$$V_R = V_s - V_F = 5 - 2 = 3\,V$$

**Step 3:** Apply Ohm's Law to the resistor.

$$R_S = \frac{V_R}{I_F} = \frac{3\,V}{20 \times 10^{-3}\,A}$$

**Step 4:** Compute.

$$R_S = 150\,\Omega$$

**Step 5:** Standard resistor selection.

Choose the nearest E12 standard value: **$R_S = 150\,\Omega$** (or use $180\,\Omega$ to be safe — it will reduce $I_F$ to 16.7 mA, still safe for the LED).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU often asks: *"What is the purpose of the series resistor in an LED circuit?"* The correct answer: **to limit the current** through the LED to a safe value, because LEDs are **current-driven** devices. Without the resistor, the LED will draw excessive current and burn out.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numericals

| Problem Type | Given | To Find | Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| Color code | Bands: Brown, Black, Red, Gold | Resistance | $10 \times 10^2 \pm 5\% = 1000\,\Omega \pm 5\%$ |
| RC charging | $R, C, V_s$ | $V_C(t)$ | $V_s(1 - e^{-t/RC})$ |
| BJT switching | $V_{CC}, R_C, \beta, I_C$ | $I_B$ required | $I_B = I_C / \beta$ |
| Inverting op-amp | $R_f, R_{in}$ | $A_v$ | $A_v = -R_f / R_{in}$ |
| Voltage divider | $V_s, R_1, R_2$ | $V_{out}$ | $V_{out} = V_s \cdot R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$ |
| Zener regulator | $V_{in}, V_Z, R_S$ | $I_L$ | $I_L = (V_{in} - V_Z)/R_S - I_Z$ |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q: Explain the working of a Zener diode as a voltage regulator. Draw its I-V characteristics.**

**Model Answer:**

A Zener diode is a heavily doped PN junction diode that operates in **reverse breakdown** without getting damaged. When the reverse voltage across the Zener reaches a specific value called the **Zener voltage ($V_Z$)**, the diode enters the breakdown region. In this region, the voltage across the diode remains nearly constant at $V_Z$, even as the current through it varies over a wide range.

**Working as a Voltage Regulator:**

Consider a Zener diode connected in reverse bias across a load resistor $R_L$, with a series resistor $R_S$ from the unregulated DC source $V_{in}$.

- If $V_{in}$ increases, the current through $R_S$ increases, but the Zener maintains a constant $V_Z$ across the load.
- If $V_{in}$ decreases, the Zener current decreases, but the load voltage stays at $V_Z$ (as long as $V_{in} > V_Z$).

**Condition for proper regulation:**

$$V_{in} \geq V_Z$$

The output voltage = **$V_Z$** (constant).

**I-V Characteristics:**
- Forward region: Same as a normal diode (cut-in voltage ≈ 0.7 V).
- Reverse region: Sharp breakdown at $V = -V_Z$ where current increases rapidly at near-constant voltage.

**Use in Mechatronics:** Provides stable reference voltage for sensor bridges, ADC reference, and op-amp power rails.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q: With the help of a neat diagram, explain the function of a resistor in a voltage divider circuit. Why is the voltage divider important in sensor interfacing?**

**Model Answer:**

A **voltage divider** consists of two resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$ connected in series across a DC source $V_s$, with the output taken across $R_2$.

The output voltage is given by:

$$V_{out} = V_s \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Function:** The voltage divider **scales down** a higher voltage to a lower, measurable level. For example, a 12 V signal can be reduced to 5 V for an Arduino analog input (which has a maximum of 5 V).

**Importance in Sensor Interfacing:**
1. **Level shifting** — Many sensors output voltages (0–10 V, 0–24 V) that exceed microcontroller ADC ranges (0–3.3 V or 0–5 V). A voltage divider safely scales the signal.
2. **Reference creation** — Provides a known reference voltage for comparator circuits and ADCs.
3. **Sensor excitation** — Provides a stable voltage to resistive sensors like thermistors, LDRs, and strain gauges.

**Example:** A 10 kΩ thermistor and a 10 kΩ reference resistor form a divider. As temperature changes, the thermistor's resistance changes, producing a varying $V_{out}$ proportional to temperature.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question Choice A (14 Marks)

### **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Dec 2023 Pattern]**

### **Question A**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, derive the expression for the charging voltage of a capacitor in an RC series circuit connected to a DC source. Also define the time constant and mention its significance.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the construction and working of an NPN bipolar junction transistor (BJT) as a switch. Mention its application in driving a relay coil in a mechatronic system.

---

### **Model Solution to Q(a):**

**Step 1 — Circuit Description**

Consider a resistor $R$ and capacitor $C$ connected in series across a DC source $V_s$. The switch is closed at $t = 0$, and the capacitor is initially uncharged ($V_C(0) = 0$).

**Step 2 — Apply KVL**

The sum of voltages around the loop equals zero:

$$V_s = V_R + V_C$$

**Step 3 — Express $V_R$ in terms of current and $V_C$ in terms of charge**

$$V_R = I R = R \cdot C \frac{dV_C}{dt}$$

(The current charging the capacitor is $I = C \frac{dV_C}{dt}$)

**Step 4 — Form the Differential Equation**

$$V_s = RC \frac{dV_C}{dt} + V_C$$

$$\frac{dV_C}{dt} = \frac{V_s - V_C}{RC}$$

**Step 5 — Separate Variables and Integrate**

$$\int_0^{V_C} \frac{dV_C'}{V_s - V_C'} = \int_0^t \frac{dt'}{RC}$$

$$-\ln(V_s - V_C) + \ln V_s = \frac{t}{RC}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $V_C(t)$**

$$\ln\left(\frac{V_s - V_C}{V_s}\right) = -\frac{t}{RC}$$

$$V_C(t) = V_s \left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right)$$

**Time constant:**

$$\tau = RC$$

**Significance:** $\tau$ is the time required for the capacitor voltage to reach **63.2%** of the source voltage $V_s$. After $5\tau$, the capacitor is considered fully charged (99.3%). The time constant sets the speed of any signal conditioning, filter, or timing circuit in mechatronics.

---

### **Model Solution to Q(b):**

**Construction of NPN BJT:**

An NPN transistor consists of two N-type semiconductor regions (emitter and collector) separated by a thin P-type base region. The three terminals are:
- **Emitter (E)** — heavily doped
- **Base (B)** — very thin and lightly doped
- **Collector (C)** — moderately doped

**Biasing for Switch Operation:**

- **Emitter-Base junction:** Forward biased ($V_{BE} \approx 0.7\,V$)
- **Collector-Base junction:** Reverse biased ($V_{CE} > 0$)

**Two States:**

| State | $V_{BE}$ | $I_B$ | $I_C$ | $V_{CE}$ | Equivalent |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cutoff (OFF)** | $< 0.7\,V$ | 0 | 0 | $\approx V_{CC}$ | Open switch |
| **Saturation (ON)** | $0.7\,V$ | $> I_{B(sat)}$ | $I_{C(sat)}$ | $\approx 0.2\,V$ | Closed switch |

**Application — Driving a Relay Coil:**

In a mechatronic system, a microcontroller (e.g., Arduino) cannot directly source enough current to energize a 12 V/50 mA relay coil. An NPN BJT acts as a current amplifier:

- A small base current (e.g., 1 mA) from the microcontroller controls a much larger collector current (e.g., 50 mA) through the relay coil.
- A **flyback diode** (e.g., 1N4007) is connected in reverse bias across the relay coil to protect the transistor from the inductive kickback voltage generated when the relay is switched off.
- A base resistor $R_B$ limits $I_B$ to a safe value:

$$R_B = \frac{V_{in} - 0.7}{\beta / 10}$$

(The $\beta/10$ factor ensures the BJT is well into saturation.)

---

## 5.3 Part B Question Choice B (14 Marks)

### **Question B**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working of a 741 operational amplifier as (i) an inverting amplifier and (ii) a non-inverting amplifier. Derive the gain expressions for both.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare BJTs and MOSFETs based on control signal, input impedance, switching speed, and applications. Which one is preferred for PWM-based DC motor control in mechatronics and why?

---

### **Model Solution to Q(a):**

**(i) Inverting Amplifier:**

The input signal $V_{in}$ is applied through $R_1$ to the inverting terminal. The non-inverting terminal is grounded. A feedback resistor $R_f$ connects the output back to the inverting terminal.

**Virtual short concept:** $V^- = V^+ = 0$ (virtual ground).

**KCL at inverting node:**

$$\frac{V_{in} - 0}{R_1} + \frac{V_{out} - 0}{R_f} = 0$$

$$V_{out} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} V_{in}$$

$$\boxed{A_v = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}}$$

**Significance:** The output is 180° out of phase with input.

**(ii) Non-Inverting Amplifier:**

The input $V_{in}$ is applied to the non-inverting terminal. The inverting terminal is connected to a divider $R_1$ (to ground) and $R_f$ (to output).

**Virtual short:** $V^- = V^+ = V_{in}$.

**KCL at inverting node:**

$$\frac{0 - V_{in}}{R_1} + \frac{V_{out} - V_{in}}{R_f} = 0$$

$$\frac{V_{out}}{R_f} = \frac{V_{in}}{R_1} + \frac{V_{in}}{R_f}$$

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right)$$

$$\boxed{A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}}$$

**Significance:** Output is in phase with input; used for buffering and gain stages.

---

### **Model Solution to Q(b):**

| Parameter | BJT (NPN) | MOSFET (N-channel) |
|---|---|---|
| **Control signal** | Current-controlled ($I_B$) | Voltage-controlled ($V_{GS}$) |
| **Input impedance** | Low (kΩ) | Very high (MΩ to GΩ) |
| **Switching speed** | Slower (charge storage delay) | Faster (no minority carriers) |
| **Static power consumption** | Always draws base current | Almost zero gate current |
| **Drive complexity** | Requires base resistor and current | Driven directly by logic levels |
| **Thermal stability** | Poorer (thermal runaway risk) | Better |
| **Cost (low power)** | Slightly cheaper | Comparable |

**Preferred for PWM Motor Control — MOSFET:**

In **PWM-based DC motor control**, the MOSFET is preferred because:
1. **Voltage control** — A microcontroller's GPIO can directly drive the MOSFET gate (no continuous current required, unlike BJT base). This saves power.
2. **High switching speed** — PWM frequencies often reach 20 kHz to 25 kHz (above audible range). MOSFETs switch in nanoseconds, while BJTs have storage time delays.
3. **Low $R_{DS(on)}$** — Reduces conduction losses, leading to cooler operation and higher efficiency.
4. **Parallel operation** — Multiple MOSFETs can be paralleled for higher current with automatic current sharing, which is harder with BJTs.
5. **Logic-level MOSFETs** — Special variants with $V_{th} < 3.3\,V$ can be driven directly by 3.3 V or 5 V logic.

**Conclusion:** For any mechatronic system driving DC motors, solenoids, or relays via PWM, the **MOSFET** is the modern, energy-efficient, and thermally stable choice.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout**
> 
> Students **frequently lose marks** in the following ways during this topic:
> 1. **Forgetting to mention virtual ground** while deriving op-amp gain. The virtual ground concept is the **key assumption**; if missing, expect 2–3 marks deduction.
> 2. **Mixing up capacitor series/parallel formulas** with resistors. Memorize: Resistors add in series; capacitors add in parallel.
> 3. **Not specifying the wattage** when solving resistor-based numericals. Always compute $P = I^2 R$ and verify the wattage rating.
> 4. **Confusing BJT and MOSFET control mechanisms.** BJT is **current-controlled**; MOSFET is **voltage-controlled**. Examiners trap students here deliberately.
> 5. **Missing the flyback diode** in relay-driving circuits. Any inductive load (motor, solenoid, relay) needs a flyback diode; missing it loses 2 marks.
> 6. **Using $V_T = 26$ mV vs $25.85$ mV** — both are acceptable, but be consistent. Some questions require derivation from $kT/q$.
> 7. **Wrong direction for Zener diode** — Zener operates in **reverse** bias for regulation, not forward. Drawing it forward-biased is a fatal error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Ohm's Law** $V = IR$ — the foundation of all electronic analysis.
- [x] **Color code** for resistors: 4 bands → Digit, Digit, Multiplier, Tolerance.
- [x] **Time constant** $\tau = RC$ for RC circuits; $V_C(t) = V_s(1 - e^{-t/RC})$ during charging.
- [x] **Inductor** $V_L = L \, dI/dt$ — opposes sudden current change; energy stored in magnetic field.
- [x] **Capacitor** energy $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$ and inductor energy $E = \frac{1}{2}LI^2$.
- [x] **Diode** — one-way current device; obeys Shockley equation. Forward drop ≈ 0.7 V for Si.
- [x] **Zener diode** — operates in reverse breakdown for voltage regulation at $V_Z$.
- [x] **Thermal voltage** $V_T = kT/q = 25.85\,mV$ at 300 K.
- [x] **BJT** — current-controlled; $I_C = \beta I_B$; used as switch in cutoff/saturation.
- [x] **MOSFET** — voltage-controlled; turns on when $V_{GS} > V_{th}$; preferred for PWM.
- [x] **Op-amp inverting gain** $A_v = -R_f / R_{in}$.
- [x] **Op-amp non-inverting gain** $A_v = 1 + R_f / R_{in}$.
- [x] **Op-amp integrator** $V_o = -\frac{1}{R_1 C_f} \int V_{in}\,dt$ — used in PID controllers.
- [x] **Virtual ground** is the key to op-amp analysis; $V^- = V^+ = 0$ (in inverting config).
- [x] **Flyback diode** must be used across any inductive load (relay, motor, solenoid).
- [x] **LED** always requires a **current-limiting series resistor**.
- [x] **Boltzmann constant** $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K and **electron charge** $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C — both must be memorized.
- [x] **Voltage divider** $V_{out} = V_s \cdot R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$ — fundamental to sensor interfacing.
- [x] **Power rating of resistor** $P = I^2 R$ must be checked during numerical design.
- [x] In a **BJT switch**, design base resistor as $R_B = (V_{in} - 0.7) / (I_C / 10)$ to ensure saturation.
- [x] **MOSFETs** are preferred over BJTs for **PWM motor control** in mechatronic systems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensors and Actuators in Agricultural Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT864 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sensors and Actuators in Agricultural Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Sensors and Actuators in Agricultural Systems?

A **sensor** is a device that detects a physical, chemical, or biological quantity from the agricultural environment and converts it into an equivalent electrical signal (voltage, current, resistance, or frequency) that can be processed by a microcontroller or data acquisition system. An **actuator**, in contrast, is a device that takes an electrical command signal from a controller and converts it into a physical action — such as motion, force, flow of water, or rotation — to modify or control the agricultural environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Exam-Worthy):**
> *"In agricultural mechatronics, a sensor is the 'sensory organ' of the system that perceives field parameters (soil, weather, crop), while an actuator is the 'muscle' that performs corrective or productive actions (irrigation, spraying, harvesting, steering)."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **farmer with a smart helmet**:

- The farmer's **eyes, skin, nose, and ears** are like **sensors** — they collect information (light intensity, temperature, smell of gas, sound of pests).
- The farmer's **hands and legs** are like **actuators** — they take that information, decide what to do, and physically act: open a valve, start a pump, steer a tractor, or pick a fruit.

So in a smart-agriculture system:
- **Sensors** continuously listen to the field.
- **Microcontroller** (the brain) processes the data.
- **Actuators** physically respond: water flows, motors rotate, valves open, sprayers spray.

### 1.3 Classification Snapshot

> [!NOTE]
> **Categories of Agricultural Sensors and Actuators — KTU High-Yield Classification**

| Category | Examples in Agriculture |
|---|---|
| **Physical sensors** | Soil moisture, temperature, humidity, light, pressure, flow |
| **Chemical sensors** | pH sensor, NPK sensor, EC sensor, CO₂ sensor, gas sensor (NH₃, CH₄) |
| **Biological / Optical** | Chlorophyll fluorescence, NDVI camera, multispectral imaging, GPS |
| **Electrical actuators** | DC motor, stepper motor, servo motor, relay, solenoid |
| **Fluid / Mechanical actuators** | DC water pump, solenoid valve, pneumatic cylinder, robotic arm gripper |

### 1.4 Why This Topic is a KTU Hot-Spot

Sensors and actuators form the **I/O boundary of every mechatronic system**. In KTU 2024 Scheme, the examiner typically asks for:
- Block diagram of a sensor-actuator system
- Working principle of one sensor and one actuator
- Signal conditioning
- Real-world applications in precision agriculture

### 1.5 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Memory)

- **Soil moisture unit:** % VWC (Volumetric Water Content), typically **0–100 %**.
- **Soil temperature range:** **−10 °C to +70 °C** for most field sensors.
- **Relative Humidity (RH):** **0–100 %**.
- **NPK:** Nitrogen (N), Phosphorus (P), Potassium (K), in **mg/kg (ppm)** of soil.
- **pH range for soil:** Typically **3.5 to 9.0**.
- **Standard ADC resolution:** 10-bit (Arduino) → **1024 levels**; 12-bit → **4096 levels**.
- **Supply voltage (common):** **+5 V DC** or **+3.3 V DC** for sensor modules.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of an Agricultural Sensor-Actuator System

The closed-loop mechatronic system in agriculture follows the classic **Sense → Process → Act** paradigm. Let us break this into the structured logical flow expected in KTU 14-mark answers.

### 2.1.1 Stage 1 — Sensing (Input Stage)
1. The **physical/chemical variable** (e.g., soil moisture) acts on the transducer element.
2. The transducer converts it into an **electrical quantity** (resistance, capacitance, voltage).
3. A **signal conditioning circuit** (amplifier, filter, Wheatstone bridge) normalises the signal.
4. An **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)** converts it into a digital number for the microcontroller.

### 2.1.2 Stage 2 — Processing (Decision Stage)
1. The microcontroller (Arduino, ESP32, STM32, or Raspberry Pi Pico) reads the digital value.
2. The firmware compares the value with a **threshold** (e.g., soil moisture < 30 % → start irrigation).
3. A control algorithm (ON-OFF, PID, or fuzzy) decides the corrective action.

### 2.1.3 Stage 3 — Actuation (Output Stage)
1. The microcontroller sends a **logic-level signal** to a driver (relay, MOSFET, motor driver L298N).
2. The driver switches the **high-power actuator** (pump, motor, valve).
3. Feedback sensors confirm the action (e.g., flow sensor verifies water was actually delivered).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 'Signal Conditioning' is asked in exams:**
> Raw sensor output is often **non-linear, weak (mV), or noisy**. Without amplification (e.g., using **LM358 op-amp** with gain **A = 1 + R_f/R_in**), the microcontroller cannot read it accurately. This is a 7-mark sub-question favourite.

---

## 2.2 Deep-Dive: Common Agricultural Sensors

### 2.2.1 Soil Moisture Sensor (Capacitive Type — KTU Favourite)

- **Principle:** A capacitor is formed by two conductive plates embedded in the soil. The **dielectric constant of water (ε_water ≈ 80)** is much higher than dry soil (ε ≈ 4). Hence, capacitance **C** increases with moisture.
- **Equation:**
$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_r \, \varepsilon_0 \, A}{d}$$
where **A** = plate area, **d** = separation, **ε_r** = relative permittivity of soil, **ε_0 = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**.
- **Output:** Analogue voltage (e.g., 0–3 V) corresponding to dry (low V) to wet (high V).

### 2.2.2 Soil Temperature Sensor (DS18B20 / Thermistor)

- **Digital type (DS18B20):** Uses **1-Wire protocol**, range **−55 °C to +125 °C**, accuracy **±0.5 °C**.
- **Thermistor (NTC):** Resistance decreases with temperature following the **Steinhart–Hart equation**:
$$\frac{1}{T} = a + b \ln(R) + c \left[\ln(R)\right]^3$$
- **Output:** Resistance (kΩ) → converted via voltage divider to voltage → ADC.

### 2.2.3 Humidity Sensor (DHT11 / DHT22)

- **DHT11:** RH range **20–90 %**, accuracy **±5 % RH**, temperature **0–50 °C**.
- **DHT22 (AM2302):** RH range **0–100 %**, accuracy **±2–5 % RH**.
- **Working principle:** Polymer capacitor whose dielectric changes with humidity.

### 2.2.4 pH Sensor (Soil Probe)

- **Principle:** A **glass membrane electrode** develops a potential proportional to H⁺ ion activity:
$$E = E^0 - \frac{RT}{F} \ln(a_{H^+}) = E^0 - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{F} \, pH$$
- At standard temperature **T = 298 K**:
$$\frac{2.303 \, RT}{F} \approx 0.05916 \, V$$
- So pH sensor output is a small voltage (**±0.5 V range**), requiring a high-impedance amplifier.

### 2.2.5 NPK Sensor (Optical / Ion-Selective)

- **Principle:** **Ion-Selective Electrodes (ISEs)** measure the activity of NO₃⁻, H₂PO₄⁻, K⁺ ions in soil solution.
- **Output:** Voltage proportional to log of ion concentration (similar Nernst equation as pH).

### 2.2.6 Light Sensor (LDR / BH1750 / TSL2561)

- **LDR (Light Dependent Resistor):** Resistance decreases with light.
$$R_{LDR} \propto \frac{1}{LUX}$$
- **BH1750:** Digital I²C sensor, range **1–65535 lux**, used for canopy light measurement.

### 2.2.7 Gas Sensors (MQ Series)

- **MQ-135:** Detects NH₃, NOₓ, CO₂, benzene — used to monitor **greenhouse air quality** and **composting gases**.
- **Principle:** Tin-dioxide (SnO₂) layer changes resistance with gas adsorption.

### 2.2.8 GPS and Camera (Vision)

- **GPS (e.g., NEO-6M):** Provides field location for **precision agriculture tractors / drones** (accuracy **±2.5 m CEP**).
- **Camera (e.g., Pi Camera, OpenMV):** Used for **weed detection, fruit counting, disease identification** (CNN-based).

---

## 2.3 Deep-Dive: Common Agricultural Actuators

### 2.3.1 DC Motor (Brushed)

- **Working:** Armature current in a magnetic field produces torque **T = k_t · I**.
- **Speed equation:**
$$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k_e \, \Phi}$$
- **Agricultural use:** Small water pumps, conveyor belts, drone propellers.

### 2.3.2 Servo Motor (e.g., SG90, MG996R)

- **Working:** Closed-loop DC motor + potentiometer feedback + control electronics. Takes **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** signal (**50 Hz, 1–2 ms pulse width**) to position shaft at 0°–180°.
- **Use:** Steering of autonomous tractor, robotic arm joints, valve opening angle.

### 2.3.3 Stepper Motor (e.g., NEMA 17)

- **Working:** Rotates in discrete **steps** (commonly **1.8°/step = 200 steps/revolution**). Driven by a **stepper driver (A4988, DRV8825)**.
- **Use:** Seed sowing mechanism, precise valve control, 3D-printed farm parts.

### 2.3.4 Solenoid Valve (12 V DC)

- **Working:** When energised, an electromagnetic coil pulls a plunger, opening the valve. When de-energised, a spring closes it.
- **Use:** Drip irrigation ON/OFF control, fertiliser injection.

### 2.3.5 DC Water Pump (Submersible / Centrifugal)

- **Working:** Impeller rotated by DC motor pushes water.
- **Flow rate equation:**
$$Q = A \cdot v$$
where **Q** is in L/min, **A** = pipe area, **v** = fluid velocity.
- **Use:** Smart irrigation.

### 2.3.6 Relay (Electromechanical Switch)

- **Working:** A small control current energises a coil, which closes a contact to switch a larger load (pump, motor).
- **Use:** Isolating the low-power microcontroller from the high-power actuator.

### 2.3.7 Robotic Arm / Gripper (Pneumatic / Servo-based)

- **Use:** Fruit picking, sorting, transplanting seedlings.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Comprehensive Formula Reference for PEAGT864 — Module 1**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A}{d}$ | Capacitance of moisture sensor | F (Farad) | Capacitive soil sensor |
| 2 | $\dfrac{1}{T} = a + b \ln R + c (\ln R)^3$ | Steinhart–Hart | K, Ω | Thermistor temperature |
| 3 | $E = E^0 - \dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{F} \, pH$ | Nernst equation for pH | V (Volts) | pH sensor output |
| 4 | $V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot \dfrac{R_s}{R_s + R_{ref}}$ | Voltage divider | V | Sensor interface circuit |
| 5 | $A_v = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_{in}}$ | Non-inverting amp gain | unitless | Signal conditioning |
| 6 | $ADC = \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times (2^n - 1)$ | ADC conversion | integer | Microcontroller reading |
| 7 | $NEMA: \theta_{step} = \dfrac{360°}{N}$ | Stepper resolution | degrees | Stepper motor |
| 8 | $PWM_{duty} = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{period}} \times 100\,\%$ | PWM duty cycle | % | Servo / motor speed control |
| 9 | $Q = A \cdot v$ | Volumetric flow rate | m³/s | Water pump |
| 10 | $T = k_t \cdot I$ | Motor torque | N·m | DC motor |
| 11 | $\omega = k_e \cdot V$ | Motor speed (no load) | rad/s | DC motor |
| 12 | $P = V \cdot I$ | Electrical power | W | Power budget for actuator |
| 13 | $Q = m \, c \, \Delta T$ | Heat (thermal actuator) | J | Greenhouse heater |
| 14 | $R_{LDR} = R_0 \left(\dfrac{L_0}{L}\right)^\alpha$ | LDR response | Ω | Light sensor |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students write `|V|` (absolute value) inside a table cell — this **breaks the markdown table** because `|` is the column separator. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Precision Irrigation:** Capacitive soil moisture sensor + solenoid valve + ESP32 Wi-Fi → saves **30–50 % water**.
- **Greenhouse Automation:** DHT22 + LDR + exhaust fan (BLDC motor) + heater → maintains microclimate.
- **Autonomous Tractor:** GPS + IMU + camera + steering servo motor → driverless ploughing.
- **Smart Spraying:** NDVI camera detects weed zones → solenoid valve opens nozzle only over weed.
- **Livestock Monitoring:** Gas sensor (NH₃) in shed → exhaust fan actuator.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the rigorous derivations that examiners love to award full 7 marks on a sub-question. Three high-yield derivations are given below.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Converting a Thermistor Resistance to Temperature (Steinhart–Hart)

The NTC thermistor resistance **R** at absolute temperature **T** is governed by the Steinhart–Hart equation. Let us derive the simplified **β-parameter equation** step by step.

### Step 1: Start with the exponential model
The resistance of an NTC thermistor varies exponentially with temperature:

$$R(T) = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$$

where:
- **R(T)** = resistance at temperature T (Kelvin)
- **R_0** = resistance at reference temperature T_0 (typically 298 K = 25 °C)
- **β** = material constant of the thermistor (in K), typically 3000–5000 K

### Step 2: Take the natural logarithm on both sides

$$\ln\left(\frac{R(T)}{R_0}\right) = \beta \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)$$

### Step 3: Isolate 1/T

$$\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{T_0} + \frac{1}{\beta} \ln\left(\frac{R(T)}{R_0}\right)$$

### Step 4: Solve for T

$$T = \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{T_0} + \dfrac{1}{\beta} \ln\left(\dfrac{R(T)}{R_0}\right)}$$

### Step 5: Convert to Celsius if required

$$T_{^\circ C} = T_K - 273.15$$

### Worked Numerical Example

Given: **R_0 = 10 kΩ at T_0 = 25 °C = 298.15 K**, **β = 3950 K**, measured **R(T) = 4.5 kΩ**.

Step (a): Compute the log term:

$$\ln\left(\frac{4500}{10000}\right) = \ln(0.45) = -0.7985$$

Step (b): Substitute:

$$\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{298.15} + \frac{-0.7985}{3950} = 0.003354 - 0.0002022 = 0.003152 \, K^{-1}$$

Step (c): Invert:

$$T = \frac{1}{0.003152} = 317.26 \, K$$

Step (d): Convert to Celsius:

$$T_{^\circ C} = 317.26 - 273.15 = 44.11 \, ^\circ C$$

**Interpretation:** The soil is at 44 °C — irrigation or shade may be needed.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: ADC Resolution and Voltage Calculation

Microcontrollers use an **n-bit ADC** to convert an analogue sensor voltage into a digital number. Let us derive the relationship.

### Step 1: The ADC divides V_ref into 2ⁿ equal steps

Each **LSB (Least Significant Bit)** represents:

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$$

### Step 2: Digital output for an input voltage V_in

$$D = \text{round}\left(\frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times (2^n - 1)\right)$$

### Step 3: Invert to recover V_in from digital count

$$V_{in} = D \times \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$$

### Step 4: For 10-bit Arduino ADC with V_ref = 5 V

$$\Delta V = \frac{5}{1024} = 4.883 \, mV/LSB$$

### Worked Example

A capacitive soil moisture sensor outputs **V_in = 2.40 V** to a 10-bit Arduino ADC with **V_ref = 5.0 V**.

Step (a): Compute digital count:

$$D = \frac{2.40}{5.0} \times 1023 = 0.48 \times 1023 = 491$$

Step (b): Convert back:

$$V_{in} = 491 \times \frac{5.0}{1023} = 2.4005 \, V$$

Step (c): The mapped soil moisture percentage (assuming linear 0–3 V → 0–100 % VWC):

$$\theta_{VWC} = \frac{2.40}{3.0} \times 100 = 80\,\%$$

**Interpretation:** Soil is wet — no irrigation needed.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Non-Inverting Amplifier Gain for Signal Conditioning

Many sensor outputs (e.g., pH, NPK) are in the **mV range** and require amplification before the ADC.

### Step 1: Consider a non-inverting op-amp with feedback R_f and input resistor R_in to ground

The standard non-inverting amplifier configuration gives:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_{in}}\right)$$

### Step 2: Define the closed-loop gain A_CL

$$A_{CL} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_{in}}$$

### Step 3: Derivation using virtual short and superposition

At the inverting node (V⁻ = V⁺ = V_in, by virtual short):

Current through R_in:

$$I_{R_{in}} = \frac{V_{in} - 0}{R_{in}} = \frac{V_{in}}{R_{in}}$$

This same current flows through R_f (no current into op-amp input):

$$V_{out} = V_{in} + I_{R_{in}} \cdot R_f = V_{in} + V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_f}{R_{in}}$$

$$V_{out} = V_{in}\left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_{in}}\right) \qquad \blacksquare$$

### Worked Example

A pH sensor outputs **V_in = 0.295 V** (corresponding to pH 5). Required output: **V_out = 4.5 V** to span Arduino's 5 V range.

Step (a): Required gain:

$$A_{CL} = \frac{4.5}{0.295} = 15.25$$

Step (b): Choose **R_in = 10 kΩ**, then:

$$R_f = R_{in} (A_{CL} - 1) = 10\,k\Omega \times 14.25 = 142.5\,k\Omega$$

Step (c): Use a standard value **R_f = 150 kΩ**:

$$A_{CL} = 1 + \frac{150}{10} = 16$$

$$V_{out} = 0.295 \times 16 = 4.72 \, V$$

This is within Arduino's measurable range.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Sensor Selection)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Comparison Table for KTU 14-Mark Answers**

| Parameter | Capacitive Soil Moisture | Resistive Soil Moisture | DS18B20 Temp | DHT22 Humidity | pH Sensor | NPK Sensor |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Output type | Analog V | Analog V | Digital (1-Wire) | Digital (1-Wire) | Analog V | Analog V |
| Accuracy | Moderate | Low | ±0.5 °C | ±2 % RH | ±0.1 pH | ±5 % |
| Corrosion | None (coated) | Yes (exposed) | Sealed | Moderate | Glass | ISE |
| Cost (₹) | 80–150 | 40–80 | 100–180 | 150–250 | 600–1500 | 3000+ |
| Interface | ADC pin | ADC pin | GPIO + lib | GPIO + lib | ADC + amp | ADC + amp |
| KTU relevance | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Closed-Loop Smart Irrigation System

This is the **most repeated diagram** in KTU Module 1 examinations. It is also asked in 7-mark sub-questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    soilA[Soil Moisture Sensor] --> sigA[Signal Conditioning]
    tempA[Temperature Sensor] --> sigA
    humA[Humidity Sensor] --> sigA
    sigA --> adcA[ADC Interface]
    adcA --> mcuA[Microcontroller ESP32]
    mcuA -->|PWM Signal| drvA[Motor Driver L298N]
    mcuA -->|GPIO + Relay| drvA2[Relay Module]
    drvA --> pumpA[DC Water Pump]
    drvA2 --> valveA[Solenoid Valve]
    pumpA --> valveA
    valveA --> fieldA[Crop Field]
    fieldA --> soilA
    mcuA --> wifiA[WiFi Module]
    wifiA --> cloudA[Cloud Dashboard Blynk]
    cloudA -->|Threshold Set| mcuA
```

**Explanation of Flow:**
- Three sensors (moisture, temperature, humidity) measure field parameters → signal conditioning normalises the signal → ADC converts to digital → ESP32 processes → driver and relay switch the pump and solenoid valve → water flows to the field → new readings taken → cloud dashboard allows remote threshold setting.

---

## 4.2 Architecture Diagram: Sensor-to-Actuator Signal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    processA[Agricultural Variable Soil, Air, Light, Gas] --> transA[Transducer Element]
    transA -->|mV, Ohm, pF| condA[Signal Conditioner Amplifier and Filter]
    condA --> adcA[ADC Converter]
    adcA --> procA[Processor Microcontroller or Microprocessor]
    procA -->|Decision Logic| driveA[Driver Circuit Relay, MOSFET, Motor Driver]
    driveA --> actuA[Actuator Pump, Motor, Valve, Heater]
    actuA -->|Physical Action| processA
    fbA[Feedback Sensor] --> procA
```

---

## 4.3 Functional Flow Diagram: Greenhouse Automation System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sens1[Temperature Sensor] --> ctrl1[Microcontroller]
    sens2[Humidity Sensor] --> ctrl1
    sens3[Light Sensor LDR] --> ctrl1
    sens4[CO2 Sensor MH Z19] --> ctrl1
    ctrl1 -->|Threshold Logic| act1[Exhaust Fan BLDC Motor]
    ctrl1 -->|Threshold Logic| act2[Heater Relay]
    ctrl1 -->|Threshold Logic| act3[Mist Pump]
    ctrl1 -->|Threshold Logic| act4[Shade Motor Servo]
    ctrl1 -->|Threshold Logic| act5[CO2 Cylinder Solenoid]
    ctrl1 -->|WiFi| dash1[Cloud Dashboard]
```

**Explanation:**
- The greenhouse controller continuously monitors **temperature, humidity, light intensity, and CO₂**.
- Based on **set thresholds** (e.g., T > 35 °C → fan ON; RH < 60 % → mist pump ON; light < 200 lux → shade motor retracts), the controller drives the appropriate actuator.
- All data is sent to the cloud for remote monitoring.

---

## 4.4 Hierarchical Block Diagram of an Agricultural Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph perception[Perception Layer Sensors]
        cam1[RGB Camera]
        gps1[GPS Module]
        imu1[IMU]

        moist1[Soil Moisture]
    end

        mcu1[Raspberry Pi 4]
        algo1[Image Processing OpenCV]
        algo2[Path Planning Algorithm]
    end
    subgraph action[Action Layer Actuators]

        m1[DC Motors Wheels x4]

        grip1[Gripper]
        spr1[Nozzle Solenoid]
    end

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution as per KTU 2024 Scheme (PEAGT864 ESE Pattern):**
> - Part A: 3 marks — short answer (one question may come from this module).
> - Part B: 14 marks — choice-based, with two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]
**Differentiate between a sensor and a transducer. Give one example of each used in precision agriculture.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A **sensor** is a device that senses a physical/chemical change and produces a measurable response. A **transducer** is a broader term for any device that converts one form of energy into another.
- In practice, most sensors are also transducers (e.g., a **DS18B20** temperature sensor converts thermal energy to digital electrical signal).
- **Example (sensor):** Capacitive soil moisture sensor — senses dielectric change due to water and outputs an analog voltage.
- **Example (transducer, non-sensing):** A **DC motor** is a transducer (electrical → mechanical) but not a sensor.

*(1 mark definition, 1 mark difference, 1 mark example)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any three actuators used in a smart irrigation system and state their function.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Solenoid valve (12 V DC)** — controls ON/OFF of water flow to drip lines. (1 mark)
2. **DC submersible pump (12 V or 24 V)** — pumps water from a tank/borewell to the field. (1 mark)
3. **Servo motor (SG90 / MG996R)** — controls the angle of a diverter valve to direct water to different zones. (1 mark)

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Choice-Based)

### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**Question:** *(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **smart irrigation system** using soil moisture and temperature sensors. Discuss the role of signal conditioning and the ADC. (7 marks)*

*(b) Explain the **working principle, construction, and applications of a capacitive soil moisture sensor** in agricultural fields. Compare it with the resistive type. (7 marks)*

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**
The system consists of: soil moisture sensor → signal conditioner (amplifier + filter) → ADC → microcontroller (ESP32) → relay/driver → DC pump + solenoid valve → irrigation lines. The temperature sensor (DS18B20) is also read to disable irrigation in extreme cold. Feedback is provided by a flow sensor. Data is sent to a cloud dashboard via Wi-Fi (Blynk/ThingSpeak).

**Working Explanation (3 marks):**
The capacitive soil moisture sensor outputs an analog voltage proportional to the soil's dielectric constant. This voltage is amplified (e.g., by LM358) and filtered (RC low-pass, fc = 1/(2πRC)) to remove noise. The microcontroller's 12-bit ADC converts it to a digital value (0–4095). The firmware compares the moisture percentage (VWC) with a threshold (e.g., 30 %). If VWC < 30 % AND soil temperature > 5 °C, the relay is energised, switching the 24 V DC pump, and the solenoid valve opens. After a fixed duration, the valve closes. A flow sensor confirms water delivery.

**Role of Signal Conditioning and ADC (1 mark):**
Raw sensor output is non-linear, low-amplitude, and noisy. Signal conditioning amplifies and filters it. The ADC quantises the analog voltage into a digital number for the microcontroller to process.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
A capacitive soil moisture sensor consists of two concentric or parallel copper plates separated by a dielectric (the soil itself). The capacitance C is given by **C = ε_r · ε_0 · A / d**, where ε_r is the dielectric constant of the soil. Since water has a very high dielectric constant (ε_water ≈ 80) compared to dry soil (ε_soil ≈ 4), the capacitance increases as soil moisture increases. The sensor electronics use a **555-timer-based astable multivibrator** or an LC oscillator whose output frequency depends on C. The frequency is then converted to a DC voltage for the ADC.

**Construction (2 marks):**
- Two copper-clad plates etched on a PCB, coated with a **corrosion-resistant epoxy** (to prevent direct DC contact with soil, avoiding electrolytic corrosion).
- An onboard **LC tank circuit** and rectifier.
- Power: 3.3–5.5 V DC; output: 0–3 V analog.

**Applications (1 mark):**
Used in smart drip irrigation, greenhouse soil monitoring, and research-grade soil profile sensing.

**Comparison with Resistive Type (1 mark):**

| Feature | Capacitive | Resistive |
|---|---|---|
| Corrosion | None (no DC contact) | Yes (electrolysis) |
| Lifespan | 3–5 years | 6 months – 1 year |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |
| Accuracy | Better | Moderate |

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**Question:** *(a) Describe the **working principle and interfacing of a pH sensor** for soil acidity measurement. Derive the Nernst equation used by the sensor. (7 marks)*

*(b) Explain the **different types of actuators** used in agricultural robots and mechatronic systems, with examples. (7 marks)*

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
A soil pH sensor uses a **glass membrane electrode** and a **reference electrode** (usually Ag/AgCl). When the membrane contacts the soil solution, H⁺ ions exchange across the glass, generating a potential difference **E** between the two electrodes. This potential is proportional to the **pH** of the soil.

**Nernst Equation Derivation (3 marks):**
The Nernst equation is derived from the change in Gibbs free energy of an electrochemical reaction:

$$\Delta G = -nFE$$

For the reaction in equilibrium with the standard state:

$$E = E^0 - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

For the H⁺/H₂ half-cell, the reaction quotient is **Q = a_{H⁺}** (activity of H⁺ ions), and **n = 1**. Substituting:

$$E = E^0 - \frac{RT}{F} \ln(a_{H^+})$$

Using **pH = −log₁₀(a_{H⁺})**, i.e., **ln(a_{H⁺}) = −2.303 · pH**:

$$E = E^0 + \frac{2.303 \, RT}{F} \cdot pH$$

At **T = 298 K**, the slope is:

$$\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}{96485} \approx 0.05916 \, V/pH$$

So the final Nernst equation for pH:

$$E = E^0 - 0.05916 \times pH \quad \text{(at 25 °C)}$$

**Interfacing (1 mark):**
The pH sensor output (typically **±0.5 V**) is fed to a high-impedance instrumentation amplifier (e.g., **AD620** or **LM358** with BNC adapter) and then to the Arduino's ADC. Calibration is done with **buffer solutions of pH 4, 7, and 10**.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Actuator Classification in Agricultural Mechatronics (7 marks, 1 mark per major type + examples):**

1. **Electrical Actuators (DC Motors):** Used for driving wheels of an autonomous tractor or running a small water pump. Equation: **T = k_t · I**.
2. **Servo Motors:** Used for steering (autonomous tractor) and robotic arm joints. Takes PWM (50 Hz, 1–2 ms) signal for positional control.
3. **Stepper Motors (NEMA 17):** Used in seed sowing machines and precision valves. Provides **1.8° per step** accuracy.
4. **Solenoid Valves:** Used in drip irrigation and fertiliser injection. 12 V DC, normally closed, energise to open.
5. **Relays (Electromechanical):** Used to switch high-power loads (pump, motor) from a low-power microcontroller GPIO.
6. **Pneumatic / Hydraulic Cylinders:** Used in heavy agricultural machinery — tractor loaders, harvesters, ploughs.
7. **Robotic Grippers / End Effectors:** Used in fruit-picking robots (e.g., tomato, apple harvesters) with soft pneumatic fingers.

**Examples (cross-cut):** DJI Agras drone uses **BLDC motors** for propellers and **solenoid valves** for nozzles.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing sensor and actuator roles:** A common error is to write "pH sensor releases acid to balance pH" — that is an actuator, not a sensor. (Lose 1–2 marks.)
> 2. **Missing units in formulas:** Writing **C = εA/d** without the dielectric constant — examiner will deduct 1 mark.
> 3. **No block diagram in 14-mark answers:** Most 14-mark questions explicitly ask for a diagram. Skipping it costs **2–3 marks**.
> 4. **Forgetting signal conditioning:** Many raw answers jump from sensor directly to "microcontroller reads it" — must mention **amplification and filtering**.
> 5. **Using resistive moisture sensor as "soil moisture sensor" without qualification:** Examiners want you to know the difference and the corrosion problem.
> 6. **Confusing PWM frequency for servo (50 Hz) with that of a DC motor speed control (kHz range).** This is a 1-mark trap.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PEAGT864 — Module 1 — Sensors and Actuators in Agricultural Systems — KTU 2024 One-Page Cheat Sheet**

- **Sensor** = sense; **Actuator** = act. Sensor is the "eye/ear/nose"; actuator is the "hand/leg/muscle."
- **Soil moisture:** Capacitive preferred (no corrosion) over resistive. Equation: **C = ε_r ε_0 A / d**.
- **Temperature:** DS18B20 (digital, 1-Wire) preferred over thermistor due to digital noise immunity. Steinhart–Hart used for thermistor.
- **Humidity:** DHT11 (cheap, ±5 %) vs DHT22 (better, ±2 %).
- **pH sensor:** Glass electrode + reference electrode. Output follows **Nernst equation** with slope **0.05916 V/pH at 25 °C**.
- **Light sensor:** LDR (analog, cheap) vs BH1750 (digital I²C, accurate).
- **NPK sensor:** Ion-selective electrodes; expensive (~₹3000+); gives N, P, K in **mg/kg**.
- **Gas sensor (MQ-135):** Detects NH₃, CO₂, benzene — used in greenhouses and livestock sheds.
- **DC motor:** T = k_t · I; ω = k_e · V.
- **Servo motor:** PWM **50 Hz**, pulse width **1–2 ms**, angle **0°–180°**.
- **Stepper motor:** NEMA 17 — **200 steps/rev (1.8°/step)**, driven by A4988/DRV8825.
- **Solenoid valve:** 12 V DC, energise-to-open, used in drip irrigation.
- **ADC:** **D = (V_in / V_ref) × (2ⁿ − 1)** — for 10-bit Arduino, **4.88 mV/LSB** with V_ref = 5 V.
- **Signal conditioning:** Amplification (LM358, gain **A = 1 + R_f / R_in**) + RC low-pass filter (**f_c = 1 / (2πRC)**).
- **Closed-loop system:** Sensor → Signal conditioning → ADC → Microcontroller → Driver → Actuator → Field → Sensor (feedback).
- **Greenhouse automation:** DHT22 + LDR + CO₂ sensor + exhaust fan + heater + mist pump + shade motor.
- **Smart irrigation savings:** 30–50 % water with capacitive moisture + solenoid valve + ESP32.
- **Standard interface protocols in agricultural sensors:** **Analog (V), I²C (BH1750, DS1307), 1-Wire (DS18B20), UART (GPS NEO-6M), SPI (SD card), PWM (servo).**
- **KTU favourite block diagram:** Smart irrigation system with **ESP32 + capacitive moisture + DS18B20 + relay + 12 V pump + solenoid valve + Blynk cloud**.
- **One-line answer for "What is mechatronics in agriculture?":** Integration of **mechanics, electronics, control, and computing** for smart farming.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 1 — Sensors and Actuators in Agricultural Systems — PEAGT864 (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
