```mermaid
        olPlant[P plant]
        olOut[Y y of t]
        olRef --> olCtrl --> olPlant --> olOut
    end

    subgraph CLOSEDLOOP[Closed Loop System]
        clRef[R r of t]
        clSum[summing junction]
        clErr[E error]
        clCtrl[Gc controller]
        clPlant[Gp plant]
        clOut[Y y of t]
        clSensor[H sensor]
        clFb[B feedback]
        clRef --> clSum --> clErr --> clCtrl --> clPlant --> clOut
        clOut --> clSensor --> clFb --> clSum
    end
```

**Key visual takeaway:** the only structural addition in closed-loop is the **return arrow from output back to the input summing junction** through a sensor block $H(s)$. That single arrow transforms an unregulated system into a self-correcting one.

---

## 4.3 Signal-Flow Graph (Mason's Gain Formula Setup)

For larger systems, KTU Module-1 problems often reduce to a signal-flow graph. The single-loop forward path and feedback loop gains that appear most often are:

| Path / Loop | Forward Gain | Loop Gain |
|-------------|--------------|------------|
| Direct path $R \rightarrow Y$ | $P_1 = G_c G_p$ | — |
| Loop $R \rightarrow Y \rightarrow B \rightarrow \Sigma$ | — | $L_1 = -G_c G_p H$ |

Determinant of the graph:

$$\Delta = 1 - L_1 = 1 + G_c G_p H$$

Closed-loop TF (Mason's rule):

$$T(s) = \dfrac{P_1}{\Delta} = \dfrac{G_c G_p}{1 + G_c G_p H}$$

This **independently confirms** the derivation in §3.1 — a hallmark KTU cross-check.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define a feedback control system. List any four advantages of using negative feedback in control systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **feedback control system** is one in which a portion of the output is fed back to the input and compared with the reference, the difference (error) driving the controller so that the output follows the reference. **(1 mark)**

Four advantages of negative feedback:
1. **Reduces sensitivity** of closed-loop gain to plant parameter variations by the factor $(1+GH)$. **(0.5)**
2. **Improves disturbance rejection** — disturbance transfer function becomes $\dfrac{1}{1+GH}$. **(0.5)**
3. **Reduces steady-state error** and can drive it to zero for the right system type. **(0.5)**
4. **Linearises non-linear plants** and can extend bandwidth. **(0.5)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Derive the closed-loop transfer function of a negative-feedback system with forward-path transfer function $G(s)$ and feedback-path transfer function $H(s)$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$Y(s) = G(s)\,[R(s) - H(s)Y(s)]$$

$$Y(s)\,[1 + G(s)H(s)] = G(s)\,R(s)$$

$$\boxed{\,\dfrac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}\,} \quad \textbf{(3 marks for complete derivation)}$$

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before You Write!**
> 1. **Sign error at the summing junction** is the single biggest mark-deduction cause. Always write $E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$ for negative feedback and stick to $1+GH$ in the denominator.
> 2. Forgetting to apply the **Final Value Theorem's stability condition** (poles of $sE(s)$ must lie in the LHP) loses the steady-state-error mark.
> 3. Writing the characteristic equation as $1 - GH$ instead of $1 + GH$ flips the entire stability conclusion.
> 4. In sensitivity problems, the dimensionless form $\dfrac{\alpha}{F}\dfrac{\partial F}{\partial \alpha}$ is mandatory — partial marks are awarded only if this form appears.
> 5. **Always quote units** for any numerical answer — KTU valuation keys explicitly deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit.

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the functional elements of a **closed-loop feedback control system**. Define the terms: reference input, error signal, controller, plant, actuator, sensor, and disturbance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a unity negative-feedback system the open-loop transfer function is
$$G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(1 + 0.5s)(1 + 0.2s)}$$
Determine the **range of $K$** for which the closed-loop system is stable using the **Routh-Hurwitz criterion**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

1. **Block diagram description (2 marks):** Draw the canonical closed-loop diagram with the six blocks: Reference $r(t)$, Summing Junction $\Sigma$, Controller $G_c(s)$, Plant $G_p(s)$, Output $y(t)$, Sensor $H(s)$, Disturbance entry point $d(t)$, Feedback signal $b(t)$. *(Refer to §4.1 of these notes.)*

2. **Definitions (5 × 1 mark each):**
   - **Reference input $r(t)$:** the desired value of the controlled variable (e.g., set-temperature).
   - **Error signal $e(t)$:** difference $r(t) - b(t)$, drives the controller.
   - **Controller $G_c(s)$:** block that processes the error to produce the corrective control signal (PID, lead-lag, etc.).
   - **Plant $G_p(s)$:** the physical process or machine whose output is to be controlled (motor, furnace, robot arm).
   - **Actuator:** part of the plant that converts the controller signal into physical action (motor, valve, heater).
   - **Sensor $H(s)$:** device that measures the output and converts it into a comparable signal (thermocouple, encoder, LVDT).
   - **Disturbance $d(t)$:** any unwanted input that tends to deviate the output from the reference (load torque, wind, supply variation).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Write the characteristic equation.**

For unity feedback, characteristic equation is $1 + G(s) = 0$:

$$1 + \dfrac{K}{s(1 + 0.5s)(1 + 0.2s)} = 0$$

$$s(1 + 0.5s)(1 + 0.2s) + K = 0$$

**Step 2 — Expand the polynomial.**

$(1+0.5s)(1+0.2s) = 1 + 0.7s + 0.1s^2$

$$s(1 + 0.7s + 0.1s^2) + K = 0$$

$$0.1s^3 + 0.7s^2 + s + K = 0$$

**Step 3 — Construct the Routh array.**

$$
\begin{array}{c|cc}
s^3 & 0.1 & 1 \\
s^2 & 0.7 & K \\
s^1 & \dfrac{0.7 \cdot 1 - 0.1K}{0.7} & 0 \\
s^0 & K & \\
\end{array}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply stability conditions (all first-column entries must be positive).**

- $0.1 > 0$ ✓ always
- $0.7 > 0$ ✓ always
- $\dfrac{0.7 - 0.1K}{0.7} > 0 \;\Rightarrow\; 0.7 - 0.1K > 0 \;\Rightarrow\; K < 7$
- $K > 0$

**Step 5 — Final answer.**

$$\boxed{\,0 < K < 7\,}$$

The closed-loop system is stable only when $K$ lies between **0 and 7**. **(1 mark for final boxed answer)**

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **sensitivity of the closed-loop gain** with respect to the forward-path gain $G$, and explain its physical significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A temperature-control process has the open-loop transfer function
$$G(s) = \dfrac{2}{s + 1}$$
A proportional controller of gain $K_c$ is placed in cascade, and unity negative feedback is implemented. **(i)** Find the closed-loop transfer function. **(ii)** Compute the **steady-state error** for a unit step input. **(iii)** Determine the value of $K_c$ that limits the maximum overshoot to **5 %**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Definition of sensitivity.**

$$S^{T}_{G} = \dfrac{\partial T / T}{\partial G / G} = \dfrac{G}{T}\,\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial G}$$

**Step 2 — Write the closed-loop transfer function.**

$$T = \dfrac{G}{1 + GH} \quad \text{(assume $H$ constant)}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to $G$.**

$$\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial G} = \dfrac{(1+GH) - G(H)}{(1+GH)^2} = \dfrac{1}{(1+GH)^2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the sensitivity formula.**

$$S^{T}_{G} = \dfrac{G(1+GH)}{G}\cdot\dfrac{1}{(1+GH)^2} = \dfrac{1}{1+GH}$$

**Step 5 — Physical significance (3 marks):**
- The sensitivity of the closed-loop gain to variations in $G$ is reduced by a factor of $(1+GH)$ compared to the open-loop case (where sensitivity = 1).
- **Higher loop gain $GH$ → lower sensitivity** → more robust to plant ageing, temperature drift, manufacturing tolerances.
- **Example:** If $GH = 99$, then $S^{T}_{G} = 1/100 = 0.01$. A 20 % drift in $G$ causes only a 0.2 % change in closed-loop gain.
- This is the engineering reason why operational amplifiers use very high open-loop gain — to achieve near-ideal closed-loop behaviour.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**(i) Closed-loop transfer function — 2 marks**

Forward path = controller + plant = $K_c \cdot \dfrac{2}{s+1} = \dfrac{2K_c}{s+1}$

Unity feedback ($H = 1$):

$$T(s) = \dfrac{2K_c / (s+1)}{1 + 2K_c / (s+1)} = \dfrac{2K_c}{s + 1 + 2K_c}$$

$$\boxed{\,T(s) = \dfrac{2K_c}{s + (1 + 2K_c)}\,} \quad \textbf{(1st-order system, time constant } \tau = \dfrac{1}{1+2K_c}\textbf{)}$$

**(ii) Steady-state error for unit step — 2 marks**

For a first-order unity-feedback system, a unit-step input produces:

$$y_{ss} = \lim_{s\to 0} s \cdot T(s) \cdot \dfrac{1}{s} = T(0) = \dfrac{2K_c}{1 + 2K_c}$$

$$e_{ss} = 1 - y_{ss} = 1 - \dfrac{2K_c}{1 + 2K_c} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2K_c}$$

$$\boxed{\,e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2K_c}\,}$$

**(iii) Value of $K_c$ for 5 % overshoot — 3 marks**

The closed-loop transfer function is **first-order**. A first-order system has **no overshoot** (it is a monotonic exponential approach to the setpoint). Therefore, **the 5 % overshoot requirement can never be satisfied** for any value of $K_c$ in this configuration.

$$\boxed{\,\text{No value of } K_c \text{ can produce overshoot in a first-order unity-feedback closed loop.}\,} \quad \textbf{(1 mark for the conclusion)}$$

**Engineering interpretation (bonus 1 mark):** To obtain overshoot, the closed-loop transfer function must be at least **second-order**, which requires either (a) an integral term in the controller (making the open loop Type-1 and hence the closed loop second-order with two energy-storage elements), or (b) a plant that already contains an integrator.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1, Feedback Control Systems**

- ☐ **Definition:** A feedback control system measures its output, compares it with the reference, and uses the error to drive a controller. *(Must-know for 2-mark and 3-mark questions.)*

- ☐ **CLTF formula (negative feedback, unity sensor possible):**
$$\dfrac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$
**Sign convention:** Negative → $+GH$ in denominator. Positive (regenerative) → $-GH$.

- ☐ **Disturbance Rejection TF:** $\dfrac{Y(s)}{D(s)} = \dfrac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ — independent of how $d(t)$ enters the plant.

- ☐ **Sensitivity:** $S^{T}_{G} = \dfrac{1}{1+GH}$ — the loop gain $GH$ is the engineering knob for robustness.

- ☐ **Steady-state error formula (unity feedback):**
$e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1+K_p}$ (step), $\dfrac{A}{K_v}$ (ramp), $\dfrac{A}{K_a}$ (parabola).
$Type$ $0$: finite step error. $Type$ $1$: zero step error, finite ramp error. $Type$ $2$: zero step & ramp error.

- ☐ **Characteristic Equation:** $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$. Its roots determine closed-loop stability.

- ☐ **Block-Diagram Reduction Rules:** Cascade → multiply; Parallel → add; Feedback → $\dfrac{G}{1 \pm GH}$.

- ☐ **Sign convention trap:** Always double-check $E = R - B$ vs $E = R + B$ at the summing junction.

- ☐ **Practical Rule-of-Thumb:** $GH \gg 1 \;\Rightarrow\; T \approx \dfrac{1}{H}$ — closed-loop behaviour dominated by the sensor, not the plant. *(High-yield 2-mark insight.)*

- ☐ **Applications to remember:** cruise control, CNC servo, robotic arm, thermostat, autopilot — at least one industrial example earns the "real-world relevance" mark in any 14-mark answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PID Controllers in Agricultural Machinery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT864 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** PID Controllers in Agricultural Machinery

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) Controller** is a closed-loop feedback control mechanism that continuously calculates an error value $e(t)$ as the difference between a desired setpoint (SP) and a measured process variable (PV), and applies a corrective actuation based on three tunable gain terms: **Proportional ($K_p$)**, **Integral ($K_i$)**, and **Derivative ($K_d$)**.

In the context of **agricultural machinery**, a PID controller is the embedded control brain of modern mechatronic farm equipment. It is used to regulate critical physical quantities such as:

- **Tractor implement depth** in soil-engaging tools
- **Sprayer boom flow rate** for precision agro-chemical application
- **Seeding motor RPM** for uniform seed metering
- **Combine harvester threshing cylinder speed**
- **Greenhouse actuator positions** (ventilation, irrigation valves)

The continuous-time governing equation of a PID controller is universally stated as:

$$
u(t) = K_p\,e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d\,\frac{d\,e(t)}{d\,t}
$$

Where:
- $u(t)$ = Controller output (actuator command signal)
- $e(t)$ = Instantaneous tracking error $= \text{Setpoint} - \text{Process Variable}$
- $K_p, K_i, K_d$ = Tuning constants (dimensionless or with engineering units)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** PID control forms the bridge between sensing (mechatronic input) and actuation (mechatronic output). Every agricultural mechatronic subsystem — from GPS-guided autosteer to nozzle-by-nozzle spray modulation — depends fundamentally on a PID or its derivative architecture.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **driving a vintage Mahindra tractor pulling a plough through a freshly tilled field**. Your goal is to maintain a precise depth of exactly **15 cm**.

- **Proportional (P) action:** The deeper the plough sinks below 15 cm, the harder you pull the hydraulic lever upward. The shallower it rides, the harder you push it down. The corrective effort is *proportional* to how far off you are — this is your **P-action**.
- **Integral (I) action:** Suppose a small, persistent error keeps you at 14 cm instead of 15 cm due to soil compaction. Your brain remembers this past mistake and adds an extra nudge to compensate. This accumulating correction is **I-action** — it eliminates steady-state bias.
- **Derivative (D) action:** Suddenly, the tractor hits a soft patch and the plough plunges. Before the error becomes huge, you *anticipate* the plunge rate and quickly react. This prediction of future error based on current rate of change is **D-action** — it damps oscillation and overshoot.

Together, the **P-I-D triad** is exactly what an experienced human operator does — and what we want to encode electronically into the ECU (Electronic Control Unit) of an autonomous tractor or precision sprayer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A PID controller is essentially a **mechanized imitation of a skilled human operator's intuition**, executed at millisecond speeds using microcontrollers, PWM signals, and hydraulic/electric actuators.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Symbol | Physical Meaning | Standard SI Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $K_p$ | Proportional Gain | V/unit error (or dimensionless) |
| $K_i$ | Integral Gain | V/(unit error·s) |
| $K_d$ | Derivative Gain | V·s/unit error |
| $e(t)$ | Tracking Error | Same unit as process variable |
| $u(t)$ | Control Signal Output | V (or mA, PWM %) |
| $T_i$ | Integral Time Constant | seconds (s) |
| $T_d$ | Derivative Time Constant | seconds (s) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Tuning forms:** Many agricultural controller implementations use the **standard (parallel) form** with separate $K_p, K_i, K_d$, while some industrial PLCs use the **ISA (series) form** with $K_c, T_i, T_d$. KTU examinations typically prefer the **parallel form**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Three Control Actions

#### 2.1.1 Proportional (P) Action

- **What it does:** Produces a control output directly proportional to the current error.
- **Mathematical form:** $u_P(t) = K_p \cdot e(t)$
- **Effect on agricultural machinery:** Quick, immediate response. For example, if the seed-metering disc RPM drops below setpoint, the P-term immediately boosts motor voltage.
- **Limitation:** Leaves a **steady-state offset** because it needs a non-zero error to produce output.
- **Tuning intuition:** Increasing $K_p$ → faster response, but **more oscillation** and potential instability.

#### 2.1.2 Integral (I) Action

- **What it does:** Integrates (accumulates) past errors over time and produces an output proportional to the cumulative error.
- **Mathematical form:** $u_I(t) = K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau$
- **Effect on agricultural machinery:** Eliminates persistent bias. Useful in **closed-loop irrigation systems** where a constant dripper flow offset would otherwise starve crops of water.
- **Limitation:** Too high $K_i$ → **integral windup**, slow recovery, and overshoot.
- **Tuning intuition:** Increasing $K_i$ → zero steady-state error achieved faster, but response becomes sluggish and oscillatory.

#### 2.1.3 Derivative (D) Action

- **What it does:** Predicts future error based on the current rate of change of error.
- **Mathematical form:** $u_D(t) = K_d \dfrac{d\,e(t)}{dt}$
- **Effect on agricultural machinery:** Damps sudden disturbances — e.g., when a sprayer boom hits an uneven terrain bump and nozzle pressure spikes, D-action pre-emptively reduces the pump command.
- **Limitation:** Amplifies high-frequency sensor noise. Often combined with a **low-pass filter** in real ECUs.
- **Tuning intuition:** Increasing $K_d$ → more damping, reduced overshoot, but sensitive to noisy signals.

### 2.2 The Laplace-Domain Transfer Function (Engineering Power Tool)

Taking the Laplace transform of the time-domain PID equation (assuming zero initial conditions):

$$
U(s) = \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d\,s\right) E(s)
$$

Therefore, the controller transfer function $C(s)$ is:

$$
C(s) = \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d\,s
$$

In practice, a **filtered derivative** is used to avoid noise amplification:

$$
C_{\text{practical}}(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + \frac{K_d\,s}{1 + \tau_f s}
$$

where $\tau_f$ is the derivative filter time constant (typically $0.01$–$0.1$ s in agricultural ECU firmware).

### 2.3 Why PID? — Engineering Justification in Agricultural Context

| Property | Engineering Benefit in Agricultural Machinery |
|---|---|
| **Zero steady-state error** (with I-action) | Ensures exact seed rate, exact spray volume per hectare |
| **Fast transient response** (P-action) | Compensates for sudden load changes (e.g., tractor pulling uphill) |
| **Damping of oscillations** (D-action) | Stabilizes long implement booms against wind gusts |
| **Simplicity of implementation** | Easily coded in microcontrollers (Arduino, STM32, ESP32) |
| **Tunability on-the-fly** | Allows adaptive control based on crop, soil, weather |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ | Error = Setpoint $-$ Measured Output | Same as PV unit |
| 2 | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dfrac{de}{dt}$ | Parallel-form PID law | Output in V or PWM % |
| 3 | $C(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ | PID transfer function | Dimensionless ratio |
| 4 | $\dfrac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_p\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s\right)$ | ISA / standard form | $T_i = K_p/K_i$, $T_d = K_d/K_p$ |
| 5 | $G_{\text{cl}}(s) = \dfrac{C(s)\,G(s)}{1 + C(s)\,G(s)}$ | Closed-loop transfer function | $G(s)$ = Plant TF |
| 6 | $K_u,\; P_u$ | Ultimate gain & period (Ziegler–Nichols tuning) | Empirical |
| 7 | $K_p = 0.6\,K_u$ | ZN PID proportional setting | — |
| 8 | $T_i = 0.5\,P_u$ | ZN PID integral time | seconds |
| 9 | $T_d = 0.125\,P_u$ | ZN PID derivative time | seconds |
| 10 | $\text{ITAE} = \int_{0}^{T} t \cdot \vert e(t) \vert\, dt$ | Integral of Time-weighted Absolute Error | Performance index |
| 11 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_p\,K}{J}}$ | Natural frequency (2nd-order plant) | rad/s |
| 12 | $\zeta = \dfrac{K_d}{2\sqrt{K_p\,K\,J}}$ | Damping ratio (2nd-order plant) | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When the question specifies the **plant** (e.g., DC motor, hydraulic actuator, sprayer pump), always combine $C(s)$ with $G(s)$ to form the closed-loop transfer function $G_{\text{cl}}(s)$ — this is where marks are awarded for analytical depth.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: PID Transfer Function from Time-Domain Equation

**Given:** The continuous-time PID control law

$$
u(t) = K_p\,e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d\,\frac{d\,e(t)}{dt}
$$

**To derive:** The Laplace-domain transfer function $C(s) = U(s)/E(s)$.

**Step 1:** Apply the Laplace transform operator $\mathcal{L}\{\cdot\}$ to each term on the right-hand side, assuming zero initial conditions (standard for linear control theory at KTU level).

$$
\mathcal{L}\{K_p\,e(t)\} = K_p\,E(s)
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the standard Laplace transform pair for integration: $\mathcal{L}\left\{\int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau\right\} = \dfrac{E(s)}{s}$.

$$
\mathcal{L}\left\{K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau\right\} = \frac{K_i\,E(s)}{s}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the standard Laplace transform pair for differentiation: $\mathcal{L}\left\{\dfrac{de(t)}{dt}\right\} = s\,E(s) - e(0)$. With $e(0) = 0$:

$$
\mathcal{L}\left\{K_d\,\frac{d\,e(t)}{dt}\right\} = K_d\,s\,E(s)
$$

**Step 4:** Sum the three transformed components:

$$
U(s) = K_p\,E(s) + \frac{K_i\,E(s)}{s} + K_d\,s\,E(s)
$$

**Step 5:** Factor out $E(s)$:

$$
U(s) = \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d\,s\right) E(s)
$$

**Step 6:** Form the ratio $C(s) = U(s)/E(s)$:

$$
C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d\,s
$$

This is the canonical **parallel-form PID transfer function**, universally required in KTU derivations.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Closed-Loop Transfer Function with First-Order Agricultural Plant

**Given:** A typical agricultural spray-pump plant modeled as a first-order system with transfer function

$$
G(s) = \frac{K}{\tau\,s + 1}
$$

where $K$ is the pump gain (litres per volt-second) and $\tau$ is the hydraulic lag time constant.

**Controller:** $C(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d\,s$ (PID).

**Standard negative feedback block:** The closed-loop transfer function is

$$
G_{\text{cl}}(s) = \frac{C(s)\,G(s)}{1 + C(s)\,G(s)}
$$

**Step 1:** Compute the forward path product $L(s) = C(s)\,G(s)$:

$$
L(s) = \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d\,s\right) \cdot \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}
$$

**Step 2:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $s(\tau s + 1)$ to clear fractions. Numerator becomes:

$$
\text{Num}(s) = K\left[K_p\,s(\tau s + 1) + K_i(\tau s + 1) + K_d\,s^2(\tau s + 1)\right]
$$

**Step 3:** Expand each term carefully:

$$
K_p\,s(\tau s + 1) = K_p\,\tau\,s^2 + K_p\,s
$$

$$
K_i(\tau s + 1) = K_i\,\tau\,s + K_i
$$

$$
K_d\,s^2(\tau s + 1) = K_d\,\tau\,s^3 + K_d\,s^2
$$

**Step 4:** Sum the three expansions:

$$
\text{Num}(s) = K\,K_d\,\tau\,s^3 + K(K_p\,\tau + K_d)\,s^2 + K(K_p + K_i\,\tau)\,s + K\,K_i
$$

**Step 5:** Denominator = $\text{Num}(s) + K\,s(\tau s + 1)$:

$$
\text{Den}(s) = K_d\,\tau\,K\,s^3 + K(K_p\tau + K_d + \tau)\,s^2 + K(K_p + K_i\tau + 1)\,s + K\,K_i
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
G_{\text{cl}}(s) = \frac{K\left[K_d\,\tau\,s^3 + (K_p\tau + K_d)s^2 + (K_p + K_i\tau)s + K_i\right]}{K_d\tau K\,s^3 + K(K_p\tau + K_d + \tau)s^2 + K(K_p + K_i\tau + 1)s + K K_i}
$$

This third-order closed-loop expression is the analytical foundation for **agricultural sprayer pump stability analysis** and is a high-yield KTU derivation.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Ziegler–Nichols PID Tuning for an Agricultural Implement

**Given:** A tractor's hydraulic depth-control loop exhibiting **sustained oscillation** at ultimate gain $K_u = 8.0$ and ultimate period $P_u = 4.0$ s.

**To derive:** Recommended $K_p, T_i, T_d$ for a PID controller using the **Ziegler–Nichols closed-loop tuning method**.

**Step 1:** Apply the ZN empirical formulas (from the KTU Formula Sheet):

$$
K_p = 0.6\,K_u
$$

$$
T_i = 0.5\,P_u
$$

$$
T_d = 0.125\,P_u
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute numerical values:

$$
K_p = 0.6 \times 8.0 = 4.8
$$

$$
T_i = 0.5 \times 4.0 = 2.0\ \text{s}
$$

$$
T_d = 0.125 \times 4.0 = 0.5\ \text{s}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert ISA-form to parallel-form gains (if needed):

$$
K_{i,\text{parallel}} = \frac{K_p}{T_i} = \frac{4.8}{2.0} = 2.4\ \text{s}^{-1}
$$

$$
K_{d,\text{parallel}} = K_p \cdot T_d = 4.8 \times 0.5 = 2.4\ \text{s}
$$

**Step 4:** Final parallel-form PID gains:

$$
\boxed{K_p = 4.8,\quad K_i = 2.4\ \text{s}^{-1},\quad K_d = 2.4\ \text{s}}
$$

These are the tuning parameters programmed into the agricultural ECU's firmware for that specific implement.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — PID Tuning Effects on Agricultural Systems

| Tuning Parameter | Effect of **Increasing** | Effect of **Decreasing** | Typical Use in Agriculture |
|---|---|---|---|
| $K_p$ | Faster response, more overshoot, possible oscillation | Slower, more stable, steady-state error grows | Sprayer flow regulation |
| $K_i$ | Eliminates steady-state error faster, risk of windup | Larger residual error, slower recovery | Irrigation dripper consistency |
| $K_d$ | More damping, less overshoot | More oscillation, faster rise | Boom suspension damping |
| Derivative filter $\tau_f$ | Smoother control, slower D-response | Noisier D-action, sensor noise passes through | Nozzle pressure control |
| Sample time $T_s$ | Lower CPU load, slower response | Better accuracy, higher CPU demand | GPS autosteer updates |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Modern precision agriculture ECUs often implement **gain-scheduling PID**, where $K_p, K_i, K_d$ are themselves functions of operating conditions (e.g., tractor speed, soil moisture). This is an advanced extension expected in KTU 14-mark problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a PID-Controlled Agricultural Sprayer

The following Mermaid block-diagram represents the **closed-loop mechatronic architecture** of an automatic agricultural sprayer used for variable-rate pesticide application. Each node represents a physical or functional subsystem.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Operator Setpoint - Target Spray Rate L per hectare] --> B[Error Calculator e_t]
    C[Flow Sensor - Turbine Meter on Boom] --> B
    B --> D[P Controller - Kp times e_t]
    B --> E[I Controller - Ki times Integral of e_t]
    B --> F[D Controller - Kd times de_t by dt]
    D --> G[Summing Junction for u_t]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[PWM Driver Stage]
    H --> I[DC Servo Pump - Actuator]
    I --> J[Hydraulic Spray Nozzles on Boom]
    J --> K[Actual Flow on Crop Canopy]
    K --> L[Feedback Loop to Flow Sensor]
    L --> C
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- **A** is the human-entered or GPS-mapped target rate (e.g., 200 L/ha).
- **B** computes the error $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$.
- **D, E, F** are the three parallel PID branches.
- **G** sums the three branches to form the controller output $u(t)$.
- **H** converts $u(t)$ into PWM duty cycle for the DC pump motor.
- **I–J** is the electro-hydraulic actuation chain.
- **K** is the physical process variable — actual spray deposited on the field.
- **L** closes the loop by feeding back to the flow sensor.

### 4.2 Mermaid Architecture of an Autonomous Tractor Steering PID System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M1[GPS Receiver - Target Path Coordinates] --> S1[Error Calculator - Lateral Deviation e_t]
    M2[IMU Sensor - Roll and Yaw Rate] --> S1
    M3[Wheel Encoder - Actual Ground Speed] --> S1
    S1 --> N1[Proportional Path - Immediate Correction]
    S1 --> N2[Integral Path - Drift Compensation]
    S1 --> N3[Derivative Path - Yaw Rate Damping]
    N1 --> J1[Combine to Steering Command u_t]
    N2 --> J1
    N3 --> J1
    J1 --> A1[Hydraulic Steering Valve - Actuator]
    A1 --> A2[Front Wheel Angle Adjustment]
    A2 --> P1[Actual Vehicle Trajectory on Field]
    P1 --> F1[Feedback to GPS and IMU Sensors]
    F1 --> M1
    F1 --> M2
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- This is a **multi-input PID controller** where setpoints come from GPS and the process variable is the actual lateral deviation measured by the IMU + wheel encoders.
- The **D-path** specifically damps yaw oscillations caused by uneven terrain — a critical agricultural stability requirement.

### 4.3 PID Component Interaction Block Diagram (Generic Agricultural ECU)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Setpoint Input] --> SUM((Sum Node))
    PV[Process Variable from Field Sensor] --> SUM
    SUM --> ERR[Error Signal e_t]
    ERR --> P1[Proportional Gain Kp]
    ERR --> I1[Integrator with Ki]
    ERR --> D1[Differentiator with Kd]
    P1 --> OUT((Summing Output Node))
    I1 --> OUT
    D1 --> OUT
    OUT --> ACT[Actuator - Pump Motor or Hydraulic Cylinder]
    ACT --> PROC[Agricultural Process - Soil, Crop, Water]
    PROC --> MEAS[Measurement Transducer]
    MEAS --> PV
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- **SUM** node computes $e(t) = \text{SP} - \text{PV}$ (negative feedback convention).
- **P1, I1, D1** are the three controller branches in parallel form.
- **OUT** node combines them to form the control signal $u(t)$.
- **ACT** drives the physical actuator on the agricultural machine.
- **PROC** is the actual physical process being controlled.
- **MEAS** is the transducer (flow meter, pressure sensor, GPS, encoder, etc.) that closes the loop.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** In real agricultural ECUs, the integrator branch often includes **anti-windup clamping** logic and the differentiator branch includes a **first-order low-pass filter** — both are practical refinements frequently asked in KTU 14-mark problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

*Define a PID controller. Write its mathematical expression in time-domain and explain the role of each of the three control actions.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative) controller** is a closed-loop feedback controller that computes an actuating signal based on the present error, the accumulated past error, and the predicted future error.

**Mathematical Expression:**

$$
u(t) = K_p\,e(t) + K_i\int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d\,\frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

**Roles of the three actions:**

- **Proportional ($K_p$):** Provides an immediate corrective output proportional to the current error. Gives fast response but cannot eliminate steady-state error.
- **Integral ($K_i$):** Accumulates past errors and eliminates steady-state offset. Ensures the process variable eventually reaches the setpoint exactly.
- **Derivative ($K_d$):** Responds to the rate of change of error, predicting future deviation and damping oscillations. Improves transient stability.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

*List any four applications of PID controllers in agricultural machinery. State one typical controlled variable for each application.*

**Model Answer (4 × 0.75 = 3 marks):**

| Application | Controlled Variable |
|---|---|
| Automatic tractor steering (autosteer) | Lateral deviation from GPS path |
| Variable-rate boom sprayer | Spray flow rate (L/min) |
| Precision seed metering unit | Seeder disc RPM |
| Combine harvester threshing control | Cylinder rotational speed |
| Drip irrigation valve control | Soil moisture / flow rate |
| Greenhouse vent positioning | Internal temperature |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With the aid of a block diagram, derive the transfer function of a parallel-form PID controller in the Laplace domain, starting from its time-domain equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A precision seed-metering unit on a tractor-driven planter is modeled as a first-order plant with transfer function

$$
G(s) = \frac{2.0}{0.5\,s + 1}
$$

A PID controller $C(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d\,s$ is used in unity negative feedback. Using the Ziegler–Nichols closed-loop tuning method, the ultimate gain is observed to be $K_u = 6.0$ and the ultimate period is $P_u = 3.0$ s. Calculate the tuned PID gains and write the final controller transfer function. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

Apply the Laplace transform to each term of the PID time-domain equation:

$$
u(t) = K_p\,e(t) + K_i\int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d\,\frac{d\,e(t)}{dt}
$$

**Step 1 — Laplace of P-term:**

$$
\mathcal{L}\{K_p\,e(t)\} = K_p\,E(s)
$$

**Step 2 — Laplace of I-term:** Using $\mathcal{L}\left\{\int_0^t e(\tau)d\tau\right\} = E(s)/s$:

$$
\mathcal{L}\left\{K_i\int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau\right\} = \frac{K_i}{s}\,E(s)
$$

**Step 3 — Laplace of D-term:** Using $\mathcal{L}\{\dot e(t)\} = sE(s) - e(0)$, with $e(0) = 0$:

$$
\mathcal{L}\left\{K_d\,\dot e(t)\right\} = K_d\,s\,E(s)
$$

**Step 4 — Sum:**

$$
U(s) = K_p E(s) + \frac{K_i}{s} E(s) + K_d s E(s) = \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s\right) E(s)
$$

**Step 5 — Final transfer function:**

$$
\boxed{C(s) = \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s}
$$

*(Block diagram reference: see SECTION 4.3 Generic PID Block Diagram.)*

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1:** Apply Ziegler–Nichols closed-loop (ultimate cycle) PID formulas:

$$
K_p = 0.6\,K_u = 0.6 \times 6.0 = 3.6
$$

$$
T_i = 0.5\,P_u = 0.5 \times 3.0 = 1.5\ \text{s}
$$

$$
T_d = 0.125\,P_u = 0.125 \times 3.0 = 0.375\ \text{s}
$$

**Step 2:** Convert to parallel-form gains:

$$
K_i = \frac{K_p}{T_i} = \frac{3.6}{1.5} = 2.4\ \text{s}^{-1}
$$

$$
K_d = K_p \cdot T_d = 3.6 \times 0.375 = 1.35\ \text{s}
$$

**Step 3:** Final PID transfer function:

$$
\boxed{C(s) = 3.6 + \frac{2.4}{s} + 1.35\,s}
$$

**Step 4:** Optional closed-loop verification — forward path gain:

$$
L(s) = C(s)\,G(s) = \left(3.6 + \frac{2.4}{s} + 1.35 s\right)\cdot \frac{2.0}{0.5 s + 1}
$$

**Mark Allocation Summary (Part a):**
- Time-domain equation stated: 1 mark
- Three Laplace transform pairs correctly applied: 3 marks
- Final summation and transfer function derivation: 2 marks
- Block diagram clarity: 1 mark

**Mark Allocation Summary (Part b):**
- ZN formulas correctly cited: 2 marks
- Correct substitution and arithmetic: 2 marks
- Parallel-form conversion done correctly: 2 marks
- Final $C(s)$ written cleanly with units/dimensions noted: 1 mark

---

#### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram how a PID controller is used in an **automatic boom-sprayer flow-control system** in agricultural machinery. Label the setpoint, process variable, error, controller branches, actuator, and feedback path. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a tractor's hydraulic implement depth-control loop, the closed-loop step response with a PI controller (only $K_p$ and $K_i$) has rise time $t_r = 1.2$ s and settling time $t_s = 4.0$ s. If $K_p = 5.0$ is held constant, what value of $K_i$ is needed to reduce the steady-state error to **zero within 2 seconds**, assuming the I-term contributes as $\dfrac{K_i}{s}$? Use the approximation that the integrated-error correction time $T_{\text{int}} \approx \dfrac{e_{\text{ss}}}{K_i \cdot e_0}$, where $e_0$ is the initial error. Take $e_{\text{ss}} = 0$ target. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The block diagram of an automatic boom-sprayer flow-control system is as follows (refer SECTION 4.1 Functional Block Diagram):

- **Setpoint (A):** Target flow rate (e.g., 200 L/ha) entered by operator or computed from GPS-prescription map.
- **Process Variable (C):** Measured flow from a turbine flow meter on the spray boom.
- **Error Calculator (B):** Computes $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$.
- **Controller (D, E, F, G):** Three parallel branches — Proportional, Integral, Derivative — summed to form $u(t)$.
- **PWM Driver (H):** Converts $u(t)$ to a PWM duty cycle for the DC pump motor.
- **Actuator (I):** DC servo pump that physically drives the spray liquid.
- **Plant Output (J):** Hydraulic spray nozzles on the boom applying pesticide to the crop.
- **Feedback (L):** Flow sensor reading returns to close the loop.

**Working Principle:**

When the actual flow deviates from the setpoint (e.g., due to nozzle clogging or speed change), the error grows, the PID outputs a corrective voltage, the pump RPM adjusts, and the flow re-converges to setpoint — typically within **0.5–2.0 seconds** for a well-tuned sprayer system.

**Mark Allocation Summary (Part a):**
- Correct identification of setpoint, PV, error: 1 mark
- All three PID branches correctly labeled: 2 marks
- Actuator and plant correctly described: 2 marks
- Feedback loop closure explained: 1 mark
- Diagram neatness and labeling: 1 mark

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1:** Given data:
- $K_p = 5.0$ (held constant)
- Rise time $t_r = 1.2$ s, settling time $t_s = 4.0$ s
- Required steady-state error correction time $T_{\text{target}} \leq 2.0$ s
- Formula: $T_{\text{int}} \approx \dfrac{e_{\text{ss}}}{K_i \cdot e_0}$
- Target: $e_{\text{ss}} = 0$

**Step 2:** Recognize that the I-term alone, by definition, drives the steady-state error to zero as $t \to \infty$. To achieve *effective* zero error within 2 seconds, the integrated-correction time constant must satisfy:

$$
T_{\text{int}} \approx \frac{1}{K_i} \cdot \frac{e_0}{|e_{\text{ss,initial}}|}
$$

**Step 3:** Using the standard approximation for a first-order-plus-integral system, the time to reach within 2% of setpoint is approximately $4/K_i$ (analogous to a first-order time constant). For 2-second settling:

$$
4/K_i \approx 2.0\ \text{s}
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $K_i$:

$$
K_i = \frac{4}{2.0} = 2.0\ \text{s}^{-1}
$$

**Step 5:** Verify dimensional consistency — $K_i$ has units of $\text{s}^{-1}$, which matches the convention for parallel-form integral gain.

**Step 6:** Practical engineering recommendation: in a real tractor ECU, the value would be capped by an **anti-windup limit** to prevent excessive overshoot during sudden load changes (e.g., plough hitting a rock).

**Mark Allocation Summary (Part b):**
- Formula correctly cited: 2 marks
- Algebraic manipulation clearly shown: 2 marks
- Final numerical answer with units: 2 marks
- Anti-windup / practical engineering note: 1 mark

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting zero initial conditions** when applying the Laplace transform to derive the PID transfer function. This alone costs **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing parallel-form and ISA-form gains.** In KTU papers, if the question states "standard form" with $K_c, T_i, T_d$, do not give $K_p, K_i, K_d$ without converting. Conversion formulas:
>    $K_i = K_c / T_i$ and $K_d = K_c \cdot T_d$.
> 3. **Skipping units in final numerical answers.** Every $K_p, K_i, K_d$ must have correct engineering units (V/unit error, s⁻¹, s respectively).
> 4. **Not drawing a block diagram** when the question says "with the aid of a block diagram." A textual description alone loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Writing $|e(t)|$ with vertical bars inside a markdown table** — this breaks table rendering. Use `\vert e(t) \vert` in LaTeX.
> 6. **Omitting anti-windup discussion** in 14-mark answers on practical PID implementation — examiners specifically reward this practical awareness.
> 7. **Mixing up rise time, settling time, and peak time** in transient-response questions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — PID Controllers in Agricultural Machinery**

- [x] **PID = Proportional + Integral + Derivative** — three parallel control actions.
- [x] **Time-domain equation:** $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dfrac{de}{dt}$
- [x] **Transfer function:** $C(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d\,s$
- [x] **P-action** → fast response, leaves steady-state error.
- [x] **I-action** → eliminates steady-state error, can cause windup.
- [x] **D-action** → damps oscillations, amplifies sensor noise → needs low-pass filter.
- [x] **Closed-loop TF:** $G_{\text{cl}}(s) = \dfrac{C(s)G(s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)}$
- [x] **Ziegler–Nichols PID tuning:** $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = 0.5 P_u$, $T_d = 0.125 P_u$.
- [x] **Parallel ↔ ISA form conversions:** $K_i^{\text{par}} = K_c / T_i$ and $K_d^{\text{par}} = K_c \cdot T_d$.
- [x] **Agricultural applications:** autosteer (lateral deviation), sprayer (flow rate), seeder (RPM), harvester (cylinder speed), irrigation (moisture/flow), greenhouse (temperature).
- [x] **Practical refinements:** anti-windup clamping, derivative filter $\tau_f$, gain scheduling, sample-time selection.
- [x] **Performance metrics:** rise time $t_r$, settling time $t_s$, peak overshoot $M_p$, steady-state error $e_{\text{ss}}$, ITAE index.
- [x] **Stability rule of thumb:** increasing $K_p$ beyond critical → sustained oscillation → instability; D-action restores damping.
- [x] **Always specify units** in the final answer — examiners allocate marks for dimensional correctness.
- [x] **Block diagrams must include:** Setpoint, Error, Controller branches (P, I, D), Actuator, Plant, Sensor, Feedback path.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PLC (Programmable Logic Controller) Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT864 - MECHATRONICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** PLC (Programmable Logic Controller) Applications # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** is a ruggedized, solid-state, digital computing device specifically engineered for the real-time control of industrial automation processes and machinery. As per the **NEMA (National Electrical Manufacturers Association) standard ICS3-1978**, a PLC is defined as: > *"A digitally operating electronic apparatus which uses a programmable memory for the internal storage of instructions for implementing specific functions, such as logic, sequencing, timing, counting, and arithmetic, to control, through digital or analog inputs and outputs, various types of machines or processes."* In the context of KTU Mechatronics (PEAGT864), the topic of **PLC Applications** extends this definition to the **practical deployment of PLCs** as the central decision-making brain of a mechatronic system — interfacing sensors (input), executing user-defined logic (CPU), and driving actuators (output) in industries ranging from automotive assembly to packaging and process control. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must understand PLC applications in real industrial scenarios, ladder logic programming, I/O interfacing, and the role of PLCs in modern mechatronic systems. Expect application-based questions (not just definitions) in the ESE. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "PLC as a Digital Reflex Brain" Imagine you are teaching a small child to **catch a ball**: - The **eyes** detect the ball (just like a PLC's **input module** reading a sensor). - The **brain** decides: *"Move hand forward, close fingers"* (just like the PLC's **CPU** running ladder logic). - The **hand muscles** perform the action (just like the PLC's **output module** activating a motor or solenoid). A PLC behaves exactly like this **digital reflex brain** — but it never tires, never panics, executes decisions in **milliseconds**, and survives in harsh factory environments full of dust, heat, vibration, and electrical noise. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:** A PLC is **NOT** a general-purpose computer. It is purpose-built for **deterministic, real-time, cyclic control** of industrial machinery. ## 1.3 Why PLCs? — The Engineering Motivation Before PLCs (pre-1968), industrial control relied on **electromechanical relay panels** containing hundreds of hard-wired relays, timers, and counters. Problems included: | Drawback of Relay Logic | Advantage of PLC | |---|---| | Bulky wiring (thousands of wires) | Single program replaces wiring | | Difficult troubleshooting | Software-based diagnostics | | No flexibility — must re-wire for changes | Edit program on PC/laptop | | High failure rate (mechanical wear) | Solid-state, MTBF > 100,000 hrs | | Time-consuming modifications | Logic changes in minutes | ## 1.4 Classification of PLC Applications (KTU High-Yield) PLC applications are broadly categorized as follows: 1. **Discrete (Digital) Control Applications** — On/Off control (e.g., traffic lights, conveyor sorting). 2. **Sequential Control Applications** — Step-by-step process execution (e.g., bottle filling, assembly lines). 3. **Process Control Applications** — Continuous variable monitoring (e.g., temperature, pressure loops). 4. **Motion Control Applications** — Coordinated servo/stepper drive control. 5. **Batch Control Applications** — Recipe-driven production (e.g., chemical mixing). 6. **Building Automation** — HVAC, lighting, security integration. > [!TIP] > **For KTU 2024 ESE:** Memorize at least **two real-world case studies** under each category — examiners award full marks only when students can justify *why* a PLC is chosen over a microcontroller or DCS for a given application. ## 1.5 Standard Performance Metrics (Memorize for Numerical Questions) - **Scan Time:** Typically **5 ms to 20 ms** per cycle. - **I/O Capacity:** From **16 I/O** (micro PLC) to **over 5000 I/O** (large modular PLC). - **Memory:** **4 KB to 32 MB** user program memory. - **Operating Temperature:** **0°C to 60°C** (industrial grade). - **Response Time:** Input filtering ≈ **0.1 ms to 20 ms** (configurable). # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The PLC Scan Cycle — The Heartbeat of Every Application Every PLC application, regardless of complexity, runs on a **continuous, deterministic scan cycle**. Missing this concept guarantees loss of marks on any 14-mark KTU question on PLC applications. ### Step-by-Step Operating Sequence 1. **Input Scan (Read Phase)** - The CPU reads the **physical status** of all input modules simultaneously. - Input voltages (e.g., **24 V DC** for discrete inputs) are converted to logical **1 (TRUE)** or **0 (FALSE)**. - These values are stored in the **Process Input Image (PII)** memory table. 2. **Program Scan (Logic Execution Phase)** - The CPU executes the user program **instruction by instruction, top to bottom, left to right** (ladder logic convention). - Intermediate results are written to internal flags, timers, and counters. - **Critical Rule:** The CPU does **NOT** read physical inputs again during this phase — it uses the PII snapshot to ensure determinism. 3. **Output Scan (Write Phase)** - After program execution completes, the **Process Output Image (POI)** is transferred to the physical output modules. - Output relays/transistors switch ON or OFF based on final logic results. - Actuators (motors, solenoids, lamps) respond accordingly. 4. **Housekeeping (Peripheral & Communication Tasks)** - Communication with HMI, SCADA, peer PLCs, and diagnostics are performed. 5. **Cycle Restarts** — Back to Step 1. ### Mathematical Model of Scan Time The total scan time $T_{scan}$ is governed by: $$ T_{scan} = T_{input} + T_{program} + T_{output} + T_{housekeeping} $$ Where: - $T_{input}$ = Time to read all input modules (typically **0.5 ms to 2 ms**) - $T_{program}$ = Time to execute the user program ≈ $\Sigma$ (instruction execution times) - $T_{output}$ = Time to update all outputs (typically **0.5 ms to 2 ms**) - $T_{housekeeping}$ = Communication and self-diagnostics (typically **1 ms to 5 ms**) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Numerical:** Given a program with 1000 instructions (avg 1 $\mu s$ each), input scan = 2 ms, output scan = 1 ms, housekeeping = 3 ms. Then $T_{program} = 1000 \times 1 \mu s = 1 ms$. Therefore, $T_{scan} = 2 + 1 + 1 + 3 = 7 ms$. ## 2.2 I/O Addressing Convention (Universal KTU Expectation) PLC applications require students to read I/O address tables. The standard format is: $$ \underbrace{\text{Input/Output}}_{\text{Type}} \quad \underbrace{\text{I/Q}}_{\text{Memory Area}} \quad \underbrace{\text{0.x}}_{\text{Slot. Bit}} $$ **Example:** `I 0.1` = **Input from Slot 0, Terminal 1**. `Q 0.3` = **Output from Slot 0, Terminal 3**. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Revision Table | Symbol / Term | Meaning | Typical Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | $T_{scan}$ | Total PLC scan time | **5 – 20 ms** | | $T_{input}$ | Input image acquisition time | **0.5 – 2 ms** | | $T_{program}$ | Ladder logic execution time | $\Sigma (\text{instr.} \times t_{exec})$ | | $T_{output}$ | Output image update time | **0.5 – 2 ms** | | $T_{housekeeping}$ | Comm. + diagnostics time | **1 – 5 ms** | | $f_{PLC}$ | Scan frequency = $1 / T_{scan}$ | **50 – 200 Hz** | | $V_{in}$ | Discrete input voltage | **24 V DC** / **120 V AC** | | $V_{out}$ | Discrete output voltage | **24 V DC** / **230 V AC** | | $I_{sink}$ / $I_{source}$ | Input wiring topology | **Sinking** vs **Sourcing** | | MTBF | Mean Time Between Failures | **> 100,000 hrs** | | $N_{I/O}$ | Total I/O points | **16 – 5000+** | | Resolution | Analog I/O resolution | **12-bit / 16-bit** | | $t_{filter}$ | Input de-bounce filter | **0.1 – 20 ms** configurable | ## 2.4 Engineering Decision Framework — When to Use a PLC A KTU-favorite 14-mark question is *"Justify the use of a PLC over a microcontroller/DCS in [application]."* Use this decision matrix: | Criterion | Microcontroller | PLC | DCS | |---|---|---|---| | Real-time deterministic scan | No | **Yes** | Yes | | Harsh industrial environment | No | **Yes** | Yes | | Programming ease (electricians) | Low | **High (Ladder)** | Medium | | Scalability (add I/O) | Hard | **Easy (modular)** | Easy | | Cost for < 100 I/O | Lowest | **Medium** | High | | Cost for > 1000 I/O | Impractical | **Medium-High** | Highest | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Utility:** PLCs dominate **factory floor discrete control** because ladder logic allows plant electricians (not software engineers) to maintain programs. This is a major reason PLCs have survived the microcontroller revolution. ## 2.5 PLC Programming Languages (IEC 61131-3 Standard) KTU may ask students to **identify the language from a given snippet**. Memorize these: 1. **Ladder Diagram (LD)** — Most common; resembles electrical relay schematics. 2. **Function Block Diagram (FBD)** — Uses graphical blocks for arithmetic and logic functions. 3. **Structured Text (ST)** — High-level Pascal-like language for complex math. 4. **Instruction List (IL)** — Assembly-like textual language (now deprecated in some standards). 5. **Sequential Function Chart (SFC)** — Used for step-based sequential process control. 6. **Continuous Function Chart (CFC)** — Extension of FBD with free-form placement. > [!TIP] > **KTU Preference:** Ladder diagrams are the **most heavily tested** language in PEAGT864. Always practice drawing ladder logic for traffic lights, conveyor control, and motor starters. ## 2.6 Categories of PLC Hardware Used in Applications | PLC Type | Typical I/O | Typical Application | |---|---|---| | **Micro / Nano PLC** | 8 – 32 | Small machines, lab demos, home automation | | **Compact PLC** | 32 – 256 | Packaging machines, conveyors, small assembly | | **Modular / Rack-mounted PLC** | 256 – 5000+ | Automotive plants, power plants, paper mills | | **Safety PLC (SIL-rated)** | Variable | Emergency shutdown, fire suppression, robotic cells | | **Soft PLC (PC-based)** | Virtual | Simulation, training, large process control | # SECTION_3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Examples ## 3.1 Worked Analytical Example 1 — Scan Time Calculation **Problem Statement:** A PLC application controls a bottling plant. The ladder program contains the following instructions: - 500 normally-open contact evaluations - 200 timer/coil energize instructions - 300 arithmetic/comparison instructions Given: Input scan = 1.5 ms, Output scan = 1 ms, Housekeeping = 2.5 ms. Instruction execution times: - Contact eval: 0.5 $\mu s$ - Timer/coil: 1.2 $\mu s$ - Arithmetic/comparison: 1.8 $\mu s$ **Find the total scan time and the maximum event detection frequency.** ### Solution — Step-by-Step **Step 1:** Compute individual instruction execution sub-times. $$ T_{contacts} = 500 \times 0.5 \, \mu s = 250 \, \mu s = 0.250 \, ms $$ $$ T_{timers} = 200 \times 1.2 \, \mu s = 240 \, \mu s = 0.240 \, ms $$ $$ T_{arithmetic} = 300 \times 1.8 \, \mu s = 540 \, \mu s = 0.540 \, ms $$ **Step 2:** Sum the program execution time. $$ T_{program} = T_{contacts} + T_{timers} + T_{arithmetic} $$ $$ T_{program} = 0.250 + 0.240 + 0.540 = 1.030 \, ms $$ **Step 3:** Apply the scan cycle equation. $$ T_{scan} = T_{input} + T_{program} + T_{output} + T_{housekeeping} $$ $$ T_{scan} = 1.5 + 1.030 + 1.0 + 2.5 = 6.030 \, ms $$ **Step 4:** Compute the maximum event detection frequency (inverse of scan time). $$ f_{PLC} = \frac{1}{T_{scan}} = \frac{1}{6.030 \times 10^{-3} \, s} $$ $$ f_{PLC} \approx 165.8 \, Hz $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Interpretation:** If the bottling line operates at 100 bottles/second, the PLC scan frequency of 165.8 Hz is **sufficient** (100 Hz < 165.8 Hz). However, the engineer must also consider **input filter delay** (e.g., 0.5 ms) — the effective response time is $T_{scan} + t_{filter} = 6.530$ ms, still within tolerance. ## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example 2 — Traffic Light PLC Application **Problem:** Design a PLC-based traffic light controller for a junction with **North-South (NS)** and **East-West (EW)** directions. Sequence (one full cycle = 60 s): - NS Green for 25 s - NS Yellow for 5 s - All Red for 2 s (safety clearance) - EW Green for 25 s - EW Yellow for 5 s - All Red for 2 s ### Step-by-Step Ladder Logic Design **Step 1:** I/O Allocation Table. | Address | Device | Type | |---|---|---| | I 0.0 | Start Push Button (NO) | Input | | I 0.1 | Stop Push Button (NC) | Input | | I 0.2 | Manual Override Switch | Input | | Q 0.0 | NS Red Lamp | Output | | Q 0.1 | NS Yellow Lamp | Output | | Q 0.2 | NS Green Lamp | Output | | Q 0.3 | EW Red Lamp | Output | | Q 0.4 | EW Yellow Lamp | Output | | Q 0.5 | EW Green Lamp | Output | **Step 2:** Internal Memory Allocation. | Address | Function | |---|---| | T1 | NS Green timer (25 s) | | T2 | NS Yellow timer (5 s) | | T3 | All-Red-1 timer (2 s) | | T4 | EW Green timer (25 s) | | T5 | EW Yellow timer (5 s) | | T6 | All-Red-2 timer (2 s) | **Step 3:** State Sequence Logic. The cycle progresses as: NS-Green $\rightarrow$ NS-Yellow $\rightarrow$ All-Red $\rightarrow$ EW-Green $\rightarrow$ EW-Yellow $\rightarrow$ All-Red $\rightarrow$ (loop back to NS-Green). A practical implementation uses **3 cascaded timers with self-resetting logic**: $$ \text{NS-Green (Q 0.2)} = \text{Start} \cdot \overline{\text{T1\_done}} \cdot \overline{\text{EW-Green}} $$ $$ \text{NS-Yellow (Q 0.1)} = \text{T1\_done} \cdot \overline{\text{T2\_done}} $$ $$ \text{All-Red-1 (Q 0.0 AND Q 0.3)} = \text{T2\_done} \cdot \overline{\text{T3\_done}} $$ **Step 4:** Total cycle verification. $$ T_{cycle} = 25 + 5 + 2 + 25 + 5 + 2 = 64 \, s $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Always draw the **timing diagram** (Gantt-style chart) alongside the ladder logic. Examiners award 2–3 bonus marks for a clear timing diagram showing overlap-free transitions. ## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example 3 — Bottle Filling Station **Problem:** A PLC controls a bottle filling line: 1. Proximity sensor detects empty bottle on conveyor. 2. Conveyor stops; filling valve opens for **3 seconds**. 3. After fill, valve closes, conveyor runs for **5 seconds** to move next bottle. 4. Cycle repeats until "Stop" button is pressed. ### Sequential Ladder Logic Construction **Rung 1 — Bottle Detection and Conveyor Stop:** - Input: Proximity sensor `I 0.0` (NO) - Output: Conveyor motor `Q 0.0` (energized when no bottle detected) **Rung 2 — Fill Timer Activation:** - Condition: Bottle present ($\overline{Q 0.0}$ condition) AND system running (`M 0.0` master run flag) - Timer: `T1` preset = 3000 ms - Action: Energize fill valve `Q 0.1` **Rung 3 — Conveyor Restart After Fill:** - Condition: `T1` done bit - Timer: `T2` preset = 5000 ms - Action: Re-energize conveyor `Q 0.0` **Cycle time per bottle:** $$ T_{bottle} = T_{fill} + T_{conveyor} = 3 \, s + 5 \, s = 8 \, s $$ **Hourly throughput:** $$ N_{bottles} = \frac{3600 \, s/hr}{8 \, s/bottle} = 450 \, bottles/hour $$ ## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — PLC vs Relay vs Microcontroller | Parameter | Relay Logic | PLC | Microcontroller (e.g., Arduino) | |---|---|---|---| | Response Time | 10 – 50 ms | **1 – 10 ms** | 0.1 – 1 ms (but non-deterministic) | | Wiring Complexity | **Very High** | Low (program-based) | Low | | Field Modifiability | Very Difficult | **Trivial (laptop edit)** | Moderate (re-flash) | | Environmental Tolerance | **High** | **High (IP65 variants)** | Low (needs enclosures) | | Cost per I/O (industrial) | Low for < 20 I/O | **Medium** | Lowest | | Scalability | Poor | **Excellent (modular)** | Limited | | Standards Compliance | None | **IEC 61131-3** | None | | Mean Time Between Failures | ~ 10,000 hrs | **> 100,000 hrs** | ~ 50,000 hrs | ## 3.5 Analytical Derivation — Maximum Safe Actuator Frequency For a given PLC application, the **maximum safe switching frequency** of an actuator is: $$ f_{max} = \frac{1}{2 \times T_{scan}} $$ This is because the actuator must complete **one ON cycle + one OFF cycle** within **two scan cycles** to avoid missed commands. **Example derivation:** If $T_{scan} = 10$ ms: $$ f_{max} = \frac{1}{2 \times 10 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1}{0.02} = 50 \, Hz $$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often write $f_{max} = 1/T_{scan}$. This is **wrong** — that gives the scan frequency, not the safe actuator switching frequency. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Generic PLC Architecture in an Application Context ```mermaid flowchart LR fieldA[FIELD INPUT DEVICES
Sensors Push Buttons Limit Switches] --> scanin[INPUT MODULE
Signal Conditioning Isolation] scanin --> pii[PROCESS INPUT IMAGE
PII Memory Table] pii --> cpu[CPU MODULE
Ladder Logic Execution] cpu --> poi[PROCESS OUTPUT IMAGE
POI Memory Table] poi --> scanout[OUTPUT MODULE
Relay Transistor Triac Drivers] scanout --> fieldB[FIELD OUTPUT DEVICES
Motors Valves Lamps Contactors] cpu -.-> hmi[HMI PANEL
Operator Interface] cpu -.-> comm[COMMUNICATION PORTS
Ethernet Profibus Modbus] cpu -.-> ps[POWER SUPPLY
24 V DC or 230 V AC] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Reading Guide for KTU:** The solid arrows represent **real-time signal flow** (data path). The dotted arrows represent **auxiliary/communication channels** that run in parallel with the main scan cycle but do not interrupt it. ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — PLC Scan Cycle Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD start([POWER ON]) --> init[INITIALIZATION
Self Diagnostics Hardware Check] init --> readin[INPUT SCAN
Read All Physical Inputs] readin --> logic[PROGRAM SCAN
Execute Ladder Logic Top to Bottom] logic --> writeout[OUTPUT SCAN
Update Physical Outputs] writeout --> comm[HOUSEKEEPING
Communication HMI Diagnostics] comm --> readin logic -.-> fault[Fault Detected] fault --> stop([EMERGENCY STOP
All Outputs OFF]) ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Traffic Light Application Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB startbtn[START BUTTON I0.0] --> masterrun[MASTER RUN FLAG M0.0] stopbtn[STOP BUTTON I0.1] --> masterrun masterrun --> t1[TIMER T1
NS Green 25 s] t1 --> t2[TIMER T2
NS Yellow 5 s] t2 --> t3[TIMER T3
All Red 2 s] t3 --> t4[TIMER T4
EW Green 25 s] t4 --> t5[TIMER T5
EW Yellow 5 s] t5 --> t6[TIMER T6
All Red 2 s] t6 -.resets.-> t1 t1 --> lampA[NS GREEN LAMP Q0.2] t2 --> lampB[NS YELLOW LAMP Q0.1] t3 --> lampC[ALL RED LAMPS Q0.0 Q0.3] t4 --> lampD[EW GREEN LAMP Q0.5] t5 --> lampE[EW YELLOW LAMP Q0.4] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Bottle Filling Application Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR sens[PROXIMITY SENSOR I0.0
Detects Empty Bottle] --> conv[CONVEYOR MOTOR Q0.0
Stop Position] conv --> valve[FILL VALVE Q0.1
Open for 3 s] valve --> timer1[TIMER T1
Preset 3000 ms] timer1 --> closevalve[VALVE CLOSES
Conveyor Restarts] closevalve --> timer2[TIMER T2
Preset 5000 ms] timer2 --> nextbottle[NEXT BOTTLE
Cycle Repeats] nextbottle -.-> sens master[MASTER START M0.0] -.controls.-> conv ``` ## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — PLC Selection Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD q1{Number of I/O Points} -->|Under 32| q2{Environment} q1 -->|32 to 256| q3{Compact PLC} q1 -->|Over 256| q4{Modular Rack PLC} q2 -->|Harsh Industrial| r1[Micro PLC with IP65] q2 -->|Lab or Clean Room| r2[Micro PLC Standard] q3 --> r3[Compact PLC e.g. Siemens S7-1200] q4 --> r4[Modular PLC e.g. Allen Bradley ControlLogix] r1 --> app1[TYPICAL APPLICATIONS
Small Machines Lab Automation] r3 --> app2[TYPICAL APPLICATIONS
Conveyors Packaging Bottling] r4 --> app3[TYPICAL APPLICATIONS
Automotive Plants Power Stations] ``` ## 4.6 Mermaid Diagram — Typical Motor Starter PLC Application ```mermaid flowchart LR startpb[START PUSH BUTTON I0.0 NO] --> aux[OUTPUT Q0.0
DOL Contactor] aux --> motor[THREE PHASE MOTOR
Forward Direction] stoppb[STOP PUSH BUTTON I0.1 NC] -.interrupts.-> aux overload[OVERLOAD RELAY I0.2 NC] -.interrupts.-> aux aux -.seal in contact.-> startpb ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Insight:** The "seal-in contact" (also called "holding contact" or "latching contact") where `Q 0.0` is wired in parallel with `I 0.0` is a **classic exam trap**. Examiners specifically test whether students understand **why** this parallel branch exists — it allows the motor to keep running even after the Start button is released. ## 4.7 Functional Architecture Block Diagram — Mechatronic System with PLC ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph mechanical[MECHANICAL SUBSYSTEM] m1[Gearbox] m2[Linear Actuator] m3[Conveyor Belt] end subgraph electrical[ELECTRICAL SUBSYSTEM] e1[Power Supply 24V DC] e2[Motor Drivers VFD] e3[Solenoid Valves] end subgraph plccontrol[PLC CONTROL SUBSYSTEM] p1[Input Module DI AI] p2[CPU with Ladder Logic] p3[Output Module DO AO] p4[HMI Touchscreen] end subgraph sensors[SENSOR SUBSYSTEM] s1[Inductive Proximity] s2[Optical Encoder] s3[Load Cell] s4[Temperature RTD] end sensors --> p1 p1 --> p2 p2 --> p3 p3 --> electrical electrical --> mechanical p2 <--> p4 p4 --> operator[HUMAN OPERATOR] operator --> p4 ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define a Programmable Logic Controller (PLC) as per NEMA standards. List any four advantages of PLCs over electromagnetic relay control.** **Model Answer:** > A PLC is a solid-state, digital industrial computer that uses programmable memory to implement logic, sequencing, timing, counting, and arithmetic functions for real-time control of machines and processes through digital/analog I/O (per NEMA ICS3-1978). > > **Advantages over relay control:** > 1. **Programming flexibility** — Logic changes via software instead of re-wiring. > 2. **Compactness** — A single PLC replaces hundreds of relays. > 3. **Reliability** — Solid-state design gives **MTBF > 100,000 hours**. > 4. **Built-in diagnostics** — Self-test routines detect faults instantly. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Explain the three phases of a PLC scan cycle with a neat block diagram.** **Model Answer:** The PLC scan cycle has three main phases: 1. **Input Scan Phase:** CPU reads the status of all physical inputs and stores them in the Process Input Image (PII). 2. **Program Scan Phase:** CPU executes the user ladder logic top-to-bottom, left-to-right using PII values. 3. **Output Scan Phase:** Final results from the Process Output Image (POI) are transferred to physical output modules to drive actuators. > *(Students should also mention the housekeeping phase and draw the cycle as a closed loop.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students answer **either the full Question A or the full Question B**. --- ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` **A. (a)** With the help of a neat ladder logic diagram and timing chart, explain the operation of a **PLC-based traffic light controller** for a four-way junction with North-South and East-West directions. The sequence must be: - NS Green = **30 s** → NS Yellow = **5 s** → All Red = **3 s** → EW Green = **30 s** → EW Yellow = **5 s** → All Red = **3 s**. **(b)** A PLC program contains 800 logical contacts (0.4 $\mu s$ each), 350 timer/coil instructions (1.0 $\mu s$ each), and 250 arithmetic instructions (1.5 $\mu s$ each). Given: input scan = 2 ms, output scan = 1.2 ms, housekeeping = 2.8 ms. **Calculate:** - (i) Total program execution time - (ii) Total scan time - (iii) Maximum safe actuator switching frequency - (iv) Comment on whether the PLC can reliably handle a process event occurring at 100 Hz. #### Model Solution for A(a) — 7 Marks **I/O Allocation:** - Inputs: `I 0.0` = Start NO push button, `I 0.1` = Stop NC push button. - Outputs: `Q 0.0` = NS Red, `Q 0.1` = NS Yellow, `Q 0.2` = NS Green, `Q 0.3` = EW Red, `Q 0.4` = EW Yellow, `Q 0.5` = EW Green. **Timer Allocation:** - `T1` = NS Green (30 s preset), `T2` = NS Yellow (5 s preset), `T3` = All Red-1 (3 s preset), `T4` = EW Green (30 s preset), `T5` = EW Yellow (5 s preset), `T6` = All Red-2 (3 s preset). **Ladder Logic (Textual Representation — 3 marks):** ``` RUNG 1: [I 0.0] ----[TON T1, PT=30s]----(M0.1) // NS Green timer RUNG 2: [I 0.0 AND M0.1 NOT DONE] ----(Q 0.2) // NS Green ON RUNG 3: [T1 DONE] ----[TON T2, PT=5s]----(M0.2) // NS Yellow timer RUNG 4: [T1 DONE AND T2 NOT DONE] ----(Q 0.1) // NS Yellow ON RUNG 5: [T2 DONE] ----[TON T3, PT=3s]----(M0.3) // All-Red-1 timer RUNG 6: [T2 DONE AND T3 NOT DONE] ----(Q 0.0 AND Q 0.3) // All Red ON RUNG 7: [T3 DONE] ----[TON T4, PT=30s]----(M0.4) // EW Green timer RUNG 8: [T3 DONE AND T4 NOT DONE] ----(Q 0.5) // EW Green ON RUNG 9: [T4 DONE] ----[TON T5, PT=5s]----(M0.5) // EW Yellow timer RUNG 10: [T4 DONE AND T5 NOT DONE] ----(Q 0.4) // EW Yellow ON RUNG 11: [T5 DONE] ----[TON T6, PT=3s]----(M0.6) // All-Red-2 timer RUNG 12: [T5 DONE AND T6 NOT DONE] ----(Q 0.0 AND Q 0.3) // All Red ON RUNG 13: [T6 DONE] ----[RESET T1, RESET T2, ...] // Cycle restart ``` **Timing Chart (4 marks):** Draw a Gantt-style chart showing NS Green (0-30s), NS Yellow (30-35s), All Red (35-38s), EW Green (38-68s), EW Yellow (68-73s), All Red (73-76s), then loop. **Total cycle:** $30 + 5 + 3 + 30 + 5 + 3 = 76$ seconds. #### Model Solution for A(b) — 7 Marks **(i) Program Execution Time (2 marks):** $$ T_{contacts} = 800 \times 0.4 \, \mu s = 320 \, \mu s = 0.320 \, ms $$ $$ T_{timers} = 350 \times 1.0 \, \mu s = 350 \, \mu s = 0.350 \, ms $$ $$ T_{arithmetic} = 250 \times 1.5 \, \mu s = 375 \, \mu s = 0.375 \, ms $$ $$ T_{program} = 0.320 + 0.350 + 0.375 = 1.045 \, ms $$ **(ii) Total Scan Time (2 marks):** $$ T_{scan} = T_{input} + T_{program} + T_{output} + T_{housekeeping} $$ $$ T_{scan} = 2.0 + 1.045 + 1.2 + 2.8 = 7.045 \, ms $$ **(iii) Maximum Safe Actuator Switching Frequency (1.5 marks):** $$ f_{max} = \frac{1}{2 \times T_{scan}} = \frac{1}{2 \times 7.045 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 70.97 \, Hz $$ **(iv) Reliability Comment (1.5 marks):** > [!NOTE] > Since the maximum safe frequency is ≈ **70.97 Hz** which is **less than** the event frequency of **100 Hz**, the PLC will **NOT reliably detect** every event. Some events will be missed between scan cycles. **Recommendation:** Either upgrade to a faster CPU, simplify the program, or use a high-speed counter module (HSC). --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **B. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a PLC** showing all major modules. Differentiate between **sinking** and **sourcing** input wiring configurations with examples. **(b)** Design a **PLC-based automatic bottle filling system** with the following sequence: - An empty bottle on a conveyor is detected by a proximity sensor. - The conveyor stops, and a filling valve opens for **4 seconds**. - After filling, the valve closes and the conveyor runs for **6 seconds** to advance the next bottle. - A cycle counter counts the total bottles filled and resets after **100 bottles**. Draw the **ladder logic diagram**, prepare the **I/O allocation table**, and calculate the **hourly throughput**. #### Model Solution for B(a) — 7 Marks **PLC Architecture Diagram (3 marks):** Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes. Key blocks: Power Supply, CPU, Memory (RAM/ROM), Input Module, Output Module, Communication Ports, HMI Interface. **Sinking vs Sourcing — Difference Table (4 marks):** | Feature | Sinking Input | Sourcing Input | |---|---|---| | Current direction | Current flows **into** the PLC input terminal | Current flows **out of** the PLC input terminal | | Sensor type used | **PNP** sensor | **NPN** sensor | | Common terminal | Connects to **+V (24V)** | Connects to **0V (GND)** | | Logic state when active | Input = **TRUE (1)** | Input = **TRUE (1)** | | Typical use | **European/Asian** standard (Siemens) | **American/Japanese** standard (Allen-Bradley, Mitsubishi) | > **Mnemonic:** PNP → Sourcing sensor → Use **Sinking input**. NPN → Sinking sensor → Use **Sourcing input**. *(Always cross-check sensor and PLC datasheets before wiring.)* #### Model Solution for B(b) — 7 Marks **I/O Allocation Table (1.5 marks):** | Address | Device | Type | |---|---|---| | I 0.0 | Start PB (NO) | Input | | I 0.1 | Stop PB (NC) | Input | | I 0.2 | Proximity Sensor (Bottle Detect) | Input | | Q 0.0 | Conveyor Motor | Output | | Q 0.1 | Fill Valve Solenoid | Output | | Q 0.2 | Cycle Complete Lamp | Output | | C 0 | Bottle Counter | Counter (Preset = 100) | | T1 | Fill Timer (Preset = 4 s) | Timer | | T2 | Conveyor Timer (Preset = 6 s) | Timer | **Ladder Logic (3 marks):** ``` RUNG 1: [Start I0.0] ----[/Stop I0.1] ----[Master Run M0.0] RUNG 2: [I 0.2 Bottle Detected] ----[/M0.1] ----(Q 0.1 Fill Valve) RUNG 3: [Q 0.1] ----[TON T1, PT=4000ms] ----(M0.1) RUNG 4: [T1 Done] ----(RESET Q 0.1) ----[TON T2, PT=6000ms] ----(Q 0.0 Conveyor) RUNG 5: [T2 Done] ----(CTU C 0, PV=100) ----(Q 0.2 Cycle Complete) RUNG 6: [C 0 Done] ----(RESET C 0) ``` **Throughput Calculation (2.5 marks):** Cycle time per bottle: $$ T_{cycle} = T_{fill} + T_{advance} = 4 \, s + 6 \, s = 10 \, s/bottle $$ Hourly throughput: $$ N = \frac{3600 \, s/hr}{10 \, s/bottle} = 360 \, bottles/hour $$ With 100-bottle batch reset time negligible (≈ 2 s for counter reset), effective throughput ≈ **358 bottles/hour**. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Traps in PLC Application Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the Seal-in Contact** — Examiners deduct **up to 2 marks** when students draw motor starter logic without the holding/latching contact. The seal-in (`Q 0.0` parallel to Start) is the **defining feature** of PLC motor control. > > 2. **Wrong Scan Time Formula** — Using $f_{max} = 1/T_{scan}$ instead of $1/(2 T_{scan})$ will cost **1.5 marks** immediately. > > 3. **Confusing PNP/NPN with Sinking/Sourcing** — Many students write *"PNP sensor is used with sinking input"*. **Correct pairing:** PNP sensor (sources current) **with sinking input**; NPN sensor (sinks current) **with sourcing input**. > > 4. **Skipping the Timing Diagram** — In traffic light and sequential control questions, the timing diagram is worth **3–4 marks** on its own. Never skip it. > > 5. **Ignoring the Process Input Image (PII)** — The PII ensures **deterministic behavior** during a single scan. Students who claim "the PLC reads inputs continuously" lose marks. > > 6. **Mixing Up I/O Address Format** — Always state addresses as `I 0.1`, `Q 0.2` (Allen-Bradley style) or `I 0.1`, `Q 0.2` (Siemens style with I/Q prefix) consistently. Don't mix conventions mid-solution. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [x] **PLC Definition (NEMA):** Solid-state digital computer for real-time industrial control via programmable memory and discrete/analog I/O. - [x] **Scan Cycle Phases:** Input Scan → Program Scan → Output Scan → Housekeeping → (Loop). Total time = sum of all four. - [x] **Scan Time Formula:** $T_{scan} = T_{input} + T_{program} + T_{output} + T_{housekeeping}$. - [x] **Max Safe Actuator Frequency:** $f_{max} = 1/(2 T_{scan})$ — not $1/T_{scan}$. - [x] **IEC 61131-3 Languages:** LD, FBD, ST, IL, SFC, CFC — Ladder Diagram is the most tested. - [x] **Seal-in (Holding) Contact:** Always required in motor starter logic — output coil in parallel with Start button. - [x] **Sinking vs Sourcing:** PNP sensor ↔ Sinking input; NPN sensor ↔ Sourcing input. - [x] **I/O Address Format:** `I slot.bit` for inputs, `Q slot.bit` for outputs. - [x] **Common Industrial Applications:** Traffic lights, bottle filling, conveyor sorting, elevator control, motor starters (DOL, Star-Delta), packaging machines, water treatment SCADA. - [x] **PLC vs Relay vs Microcontroller:** PLCs win in **determinism, scalability, harsh environments, and field maintainability**. - [x] **PLC Categories:** Micro (< 32 I/O), Compact (32–256 I/O), Modular (256+ I/O), Safety (SIL-rated), Soft (PC-based). - [x] **Throughput Formula for Sequential Apps:** $N = 3600 / T_{cycle}$ bottles per hour (or units/hour). - [x] **Process Input Image (PII)** and **Process Output Image (POI)** are critical for deterministic scan operation. - [x] **Always draw a timing diagram** for sequential application questions — it carries independent marks. --- > [!TIP] > **Final Exam Strategy for PEAGT864 Module 1 (PLC Applications):** > - Allocate **30 seconds** to read the question fully and identify the application type. > - Start with the **I/O allocation table** (1–2 marks guaranteed). > - Draw a **clear ladder logic diagram** with proper addressing. > - Add a **timing chart** for any sequential process. > - Conclude with a **numerical justification** (scan time, throughput, or frequency check). > - Mention at least **one industrial example** to demonstrate application-level understanding.

topics.title: Modeling and Simulation of Agricultural Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT864 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Modeling and Simulation of Agricultural Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Agricultural System Modeling** is the systematic development of mathematical, logical, and computational representations of biophysical, chemical, mechanical, and ecological processes that govern agricultural production. **Simulation** is the act of executing such a model over time (or space) on a digital computer to study the emergent behavior of crops, livestock, soil, machines, and the agricultural environment without performing costly, time-consuming, or destructive field experiments.

In the **KTU 2024 Mechatronics framework**, agricultural system modeling sits at the intersection of:

- **Mechanical systems** (tractors, implements, harvesters, robotic actuators)
- **Electronic systems** (sensors — soil moisture, NPK, chlorophyll, RTK-GPS)
- **Control systems** (precision irrigation, auto-steering, variable rate technology)
- **Computer systems** (embedded MCUs, PLCs, SCADA, edge AI)

The combined system is termed a **Cyber-Physical Agricultural System (CPAS)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Twin of a Farm"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: A Flight Simulator for Farming**
> 
> Imagine a pilot learning to fly without ever leaving the ground — they use a **flight simulator** that mimics the aircraft, weather, and controls. Agricultural modeling is exactly this: a **digital twin of a farm field, a greenhouse, or a tractor**. Instead of wasting a year testing whether a new irrigation schedule works, the farmer asks the *digital twin* "what if I irrigate 20% less?" and sees the answer in seconds. The simulator is the *model*; running it is the *simulation*; the farm is the *real system*.

## 1.3 Why Model Agricultural Systems? (KTU High-Yield Motivation)

Agricultural systems are **complex, non-linear, time-varying, distributed-parameter, and stochastic**. Field experimentation is:

- **Expensive** (a single multi-site crop trial can exceed **₹15,00,000**)
- **Slow** (one growing season = 4–8 months)
- **Weather-dependent** (irreproducible)
- **Destructive** (yield cannot be measured without harvesting)

Modeling overcomes these by enabling **what-if analysis**, **forecasting**, **optimization**, and **control design**.

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Bold Constants & Standards)

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Base soil temperature for germination** | $T_{base}$ | **10 °C** | Threshold for most cereals |
| **Field Capacity** | $\theta_{FC}$ | **0.30 cm³/cm³** (loam) | Upper drainage limit |
| **Wilting Point** | $\theta_{WP}$ | **0.12 cm³/cm³** (loam) | Lower crop-extraction limit |
| **Reference ET (Penman-Monteith)** | $ET_0$ | **3–6 mm/day** (tropics) | FAO-56 standard |
| **Photosynthetically Active Radiation** | PAR | **45–50 %** of $R_s$ | McCree (1972) |
| **Water Use Efficiency** | WUE | **1.2 kg/m³** (wheat) | kg grain per m³ water |
| **Light Use Efficiency** | LUE | **1.0–1.5 g/MJ** | Monteith's LUE model |

---

## 1.5 Classification of Agricultural Models (Foundational Matrix)

Agricultural models are classified along **five orthogonal axes**. KTU examiners frequently test these classifications.

1. **Static vs Dynamic** — Static models ignore time (e.g., allometric yield equations). Dynamic models use differential equations with respect to time.
2. **Deterministic vs Stochastic** — Deterministic gives one output per input. Stochastic incorporates probability distributions (e.g., rainfall variability).
3. **Empirical (Black-box) vs Mechanistic (White-box)** — Empirical uses regression/statistics; mechanistic uses first-principles physics/biology.
4. **Continuous vs Discrete** — Continuous models ODEs/PDEs; discrete models use difference equations or event-driven logic.
5. **Lumped vs Distributed** — Lumped models assume spatial uniformity; distributed models use spatial grids or finite elements.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick-Recall Mnemonic: "S-D-E-C-L"** → **S**tatic, **D**eterministic, **E**mpirical, **C**ontinuous, **L**umped. The "opposites" make a dynamic, stochastic, mechanistic, discrete, distributed model.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Modeling-Simulation Pipeline (Five Logical Phases)

The process is **sequential, iterative, and cyclic**. KTU frequently asks for a flow diagram or step description.

### Phase 1 — Problem Definition
- Identify the **system boundary**: what is *inside* (state variables) vs *outside* (forcing functions).
- Define the **objective**: predict yield, optimize water, design a controller, etc.
- Specify **accuracy** and **time horizon** requirements.

### Phase 2 — Conceptual Model Formulation
