- Choose modeling approach: **empirical, mechanistic, or hybrid (grey-box)**.
- List **state variables** $x(t)$, **inputs** $u(t)$, **outputs** $y(t)$, **parameters** $\theta$, **disturbances** $d(t)$.
- Draw **influence diagrams / causal loop diagrams**.

### Phase 3 — Mathematical Model Specification
- Translate the conceptual model into **ODEs, PDEs, algebraic equations, or stochastic processes**.
- Validate dimensional consistency using **dimensional analysis (Buckingham π theorem)**.

### Phase 4 — Computational Implementation & Simulation
- Select numerical solvers: **Euler, RK4, Heun** for ODEs; **finite-difference, finite-element** for PDEs.
- Choose software: **MATLAB/Simulink, Modelica/Dymola, STELLA, AnyLogic, APSIM, DSSAT, OpenFOAM** for CFD.

### Phase 5 — Verification, Validation & Experimentation
- **Verification** = "Are we solving the equations right?" (code bugs, solver errors).
- **Validation** = "Are we solving the right equations?" (model vs field data).
- Use statistical metrics: **RMSE, R², NSE (Nash-Sutcliffe Efficiency), MAE, Willmott's d**.

## 2.2 Core Modeling Theories in Agricultural Mechatronics

### 2.2.1 System Dynamics (Forrester, 1961)

The agricultural system is represented using **stocks (levels)** and **flows (rates)** governed by feedback loops.

$$
\frac{dL(t)}{dt} = R_{in}(t) - R_{out}(t)
$$

where $L(t)$ is the stock level, $R_{in}$ and $R_{out}$ are inflow and outflow rates.

**Application example:** Soil nitrogen stock dynamics in a paddy field.

### 2.2.2 Crop Growth Modeling

The most widely used framework is the **Monteith LUE (Light Use Efficiency) approach**:

$$
Y = \varepsilon_s \cdot \int_{t_1}^{t_2} S_{t,i}(t) \cdot f(T) \cdot f(W) \cdot f(N) \, dt
$$

where:
- $Y$ = above-ground biomass (kg/m²)
- $\varepsilon_s$ = **1.0–1.5 g/MJ** (species-specific LUE constant)
- $S_{t,i}$ = intercepted solar radiation (MJ/m²/day)
- $f(T), f(W), f(N)$ = dimensionless stress factors (0–1) for temperature, water, and nitrogen

### 2.2.3 Soil Water Flow (Richards Equation — 1931)

The governing PDE for variably saturated flow in soils:

$$
\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left[K(\psi)\left(\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial z} + 1\right)\right] - S(z,t)
$$

where $\theta$ is volumetric water content, $\psi$ is matric potential (cm), $K(\psi)$ is hydraulic conductivity, $S$ is root water uptake sink term.

### 2.2.4 Evapotranspiration (FAO-56 Penman-Monteith)

$$
ET_0 = \frac{0.408 \, \Delta (R_n - G) + \gamma \frac{900}{T+273} u_2 (e_s - e_a)}{\Delta + \gamma (1 + 0.34 u_2)}
$$

Units: mm/day. Inputs: $R_n$ (net radiation, MJ/m²/day), $G$ (soil heat flux), $T$ (°C), $u_2$ (wind speed at 2 m, m/s), $e_s - e_a$ (vapor pressure deficit, kPa).

### 2.2.5 Logistic Crop-Pest Population Dynamics

$$
\frac{dP}{dt} = r P \left(1 - \frac{P}{K}\right) - \mu P
$$

where $P$ = pest population, $r$ = intrinsic growth rate, $K$ = carrying capacity, $\mu$ = control mortality (e.g., pesticide effect).

### 2.2.6 Greenhouse Thermal Model (Lumped Capacitance)

$$
C_{gh} \frac{dT_{in}}{dt} = Q_{solar} + Q_{heater} - Q_{vent} - Q_{cond} - Q_{transpiration}
$$

where $C_{gh} = \rho \, c_p \, V$ is the thermal capacity of the air mass.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is the single most important revision artifact for Module 2. Memorize the **equation form, variable meaning, and units** — KTU exams test these directly.

| # | Model / Equation | Mathematical Form | Key Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | System Dynamics (Stock-Flow) | $\dfrac{dL}{dt} = R_{in} - R_{out}$ | $L$ = kg; $R$ = kg/day | Soil N, water, biomass stocks |
| 2 | Logistic Growth | $\dfrac{dP}{dt} = rP\left(1 - \dfrac{P}{K}\right)$ | $r$ = 1/day; $K$ = indiv./ha | Pest / weed / microbial |
| 3 | Exponential Decay (Pesticide) | $\dfrac{dC}{dt} = -k C$ | $C$ = mg/L; $k$ = 1/day | Pesticide dissipation |
| 4 | Monod Kinetics (Microbial) | $\mu = \mu_{max} \dfrac{S}{K_s + S}$ | $S$ = substrate (g/L) | Bioreactor / silage |
| 5 | Monteith LUE | $Y = \varepsilon_s \int S_{t,i} \prod f_i \, dt$ | $\varepsilon_s$ = g/MJ | Crop yield forecast |
| 6 | Penman-Monteith ET₀ | (see §2.2.4) | mm/day | Irrigation scheduling |
| 7 | Richards Equation | $\dfrac{\partial \theta}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot [K(\psi)\nabla(\psi + z)] - S$ | $\theta$ = m³/m³ | Soil water modeling |
| 8 | Darcy's Law (soil) | $q = -K \dfrac{d\psi}{dz}$ | $q$ = m/s | Drainage design |
| 9 | Van Genuchten (SWRC) | $\theta(\psi) = \theta_r + \dfrac{\theta_s - \theta_r}{[1 + (\alpha \vert \psi \vert)^n]^m}$ | $\alpha, n, m$ shape params | Soil hydraulics |
| 10 | Greenhouse Energy Balance | $C_{gh} \dfrac{dT_{in}}{dt} = \sum Q_i$ | $T$ = °C; $Q$ = W | Climate control |
| 11 | State-Space Form | $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x} + B\mathbf{u}$; $\mathbf{y} = C\mathbf{x} + D\mathbf{u}$ | $A,B,C,D$ matrices | Control design |
| 12 | Newton Cooling | $\dfrac{dT}{dt} = -\dfrac{hA}{mc_p}(T - T_{\infty})$ | $h$ = W/m²K | Produce cooling |
| 13 | Cumulative Rainfall Runoff (SCS-CN) | $Q = \dfrac{(P - 0.2S)^2}{P + 0.8S}$; $S = \dfrac{25400}{CN} - 254$ | $P$ = mm; $CN$ = curve number | Watershed modeling |
| 14 | Lambert-Beer (Canopy Light) | $I = I_0 e^{-k \cdot LAI}$ | $k$ = extinction coeff. | Light interception |
| 15 | Reliability (Failure) | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda$ = failure rate | Machinery availability |

> [!NOTE]
> Notice how the **absolute value** of $\psi$ in the Van Genuchten equation is written as $\vert \psi \vert$ — never as a single vertical pipe inside markdown tables (per the V10 protocol).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Precision irrigation** uses Richards-equation-based soil models to schedule variable-rate drip systems.
- **Greenhouse climate controllers** in the Netherlands (e.g., Certhon, Priva) run real-time Penman-Monteith + thermal-balance models on industrial PLCs.
- **Crop yield forecasting** for governments (USDA, ICAR) uses LUE + remote-sensing LAI data assimilation in DSSAT.
- **Autonomous tractor-implement systems** use vehicle-dynamics + soil-tillage models to optimize draft force and fuel consumption.
- **Disease outbreak prediction** uses coupled logistic pest + weather models for warning systems (e.g., late blight in potato).
- **Post-harvest cold-chain** design uses transient heat-transfer models with Newton cooling and Arrhenius respiration kinetics.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Logistic Crop-Pest Model with Pesticide Control

This is the **highest-frequency derivation** asked in KTU Module 2 questions. We derive the closed-form solution step-by-step.

### Problem Statement

A pest population in a field grows logistically with intrinsic rate $r = 0.5$ /day and carrying capacity $K = 10{,}000$ insects/ha. A pesticide is applied at time $t_0 = 0$ producing an instantaneous kill fraction $f = 0.7$. Determine:
1. The **time** for the population to grow back to the **economic threshold** $P_{ET} = 2{,}000$ insects/ha.
2. The **optimal re-spray interval** if $P_{ET}$ must not be exceeded.

### Step 1 — Write the Governing ODE

$$
\frac{dP}{dt} = r P \left(1 - \frac{P}{K}\right) - \mu(t) P
$$

**Textual explanation:** The first term is logistic growth, the second term is pesticide mortality. $\mu(t)$ is the (time-varying) mortality coefficient from pesticide, $P$ is pest density, $K$ is environmental carrying capacity.

### Step 2 — Assume Pesticide Acts as Instantaneous Knockdown

For **contact neurotoxins** (pyrethroids, organophosphates), we approximate the action as **discontinuous** at spray time:

$$
P(t_0^+) = (1 - f) \cdot P(t_0^-)
$$

**Textual explanation:** This decouples continuous growth from discrete intervention. The model becomes a **hybrid dynamical system**.

### Step 3 — Solve the Logistic ODE Without Pesticide

We integrate:

$$
\frac{dP}{P(K - P)} = \frac{r}{K} \, dt
$$

**Textual explanation:** Partial fraction decomposition of the LHS. Using identity:

$$
\frac{1}{P(K - P)} = \frac{1}{K}\left(\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{K - P}\right)
$$

### Step 4 — Apply Partial Fractions and Integrate

$$
\int \frac{1}{K}\left(\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{K - P}\right) dP = \int \frac{r}{K} dt
$$

$$
\frac{1}{K} \left[ \ln \vert P \vert - \ln \vert K - P \vert \right] = \frac{r}{K} t + C
$$

$$
\ln\left(\frac{P}{K - P}\right) = r t + C_1
$$

**Textual explanation:** $C_1 = K C$ absorbs the integration constant. Absolute value removed because $0 < P < K$.

### Step 5 — Exponentiate and Solve for the Constant

$$
\frac{P}{K - P} = A \, e^{rt}
$$

where $A = \frac{P_0}{K - P_0}$ is set by initial condition $P(0) = P_0$.

### Step 6 — Closed-Form Logistic Solution (Verhulst, 1838)

$$
\boxed{\,P(t) = \frac{K}{1 + A e^{-rt}} = \frac{K}{1 + \left(\dfrac{K - P_0}{P_0}\right) e^{-rt}}\,}
$$

**Textual explanation:** This S-shaped curve is the canonical **logistic growth model** in agricultural pest and crop biomass modeling.

### Step 7 — Apply Numerical Values to the Problem

- $P_0 = 3{,}000$ insects/ha (immediately after spray = $10{,}000 \times 0.3$)
- $K = 10{,}000$, $r = 0.5$ /day
- $A = \dfrac{10{,}000 - 3{,}000}{3{,}000} = 2.333$

So:

$$
P(t) = \frac{10{,}000}{1 + 2.333 \, e^{-0.5 t}}
$$

### Step 8 — Solve for Time to Reach Economic Threshold

Set $P(t^*) = 2{,}000$:

$$
2{,}000 = \frac{10{,}000}{1 + 2.333 \, e^{-0.5 t^*}}
$$

$$
1 + 2.333 \, e^{-0.5 t^*} = 5
$$

$$
e^{-0.5 t^*} = \frac{4}{2.333} = 1.7143
$$

**Wait** — $e^{-0.5 t^*}$ cannot exceed 1. The threshold $P_{ET} = 2{,}000 < P_0 = 3{,}000$, so the pest population is **already above the threshold at $t = 0$**. The model correctly indicates re-spray must occur **before day 0** or use a stronger pesticide.

Let us instead use $P_{ET} = 5{,}000$ (more realistic upper threshold for some crops) and $P_0 = 3{,}000$:

$$
5{,}000 = \frac{10{,}000}{1 + 2.333 \, e^{-0.5 t^*}}
$$

$$
1 + 2.333 \, e^{-0.5 t^*} = 2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; e^{-0.5 t^*} = \frac{1}{2.333} = 0.4286
$$

$$
-0.5 t^* = \ln(0.4286) = -0.8473
$$

$$
\boxed{\,t^* = 1.695 \text{ days}\,}
$$

**Conclusion:** Re-spray must occur at intervals **strictly less than 1.7 days** to keep pest below the economic threshold of 5,000 insects/ha.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Soil Water Balance for a Field (Book-keeping Model)

A simple but **examinable** derivation showing how lumped soil water balance is obtained from first principles.

### Step 1 — Define the State Variable

Let $S(t)$ be the **root-zone soil water storage** in mm (typical root depth 0–60 cm).

### Step 2 — Identify All Inflows and Outflows

$$
\frac{dS}{dt} = P_{eff} + I - ET_c - D - R
$$

where:
- $P_{eff}$ = effective precipitation (mm/day) = $P \times$ runoff coefficient
- $I$ = irrigation (mm/day)
- $ET_c = K_c \cdot ET_0$ = crop evapotranspiration
- $D$ = deep drainage (mm/day)
- $R$ = surface runoff (mm/day)

### Step 3 — Apply the SCS Curve Number Method for Runoff

$$
R = \frac{(P - 0.2 S_{max})^2}{P + 0.8 S_{max}}, \quad S_{max} = \frac{25{,}400}{CN} - 254
$$

**Textual explanation:** $CN$ is the SCS Curve Number, depending on soil group and land use.

### Step 4 — Drainage Using Simple Bucket Overflow

$$
D = \max\left(0, \frac{S - S_{FC}}{t_{drain}}\right)
$$

where $S_{FC} = \theta_{FC} \cdot d_{root}$ is field capacity storage and $t_{drain}$ is the drainage time constant (1–3 days).

### Step 5 — Combine into a Discretized Update Equation

Using Euler forward difference with time step $\Delta t$:

$$
S(t + \Delta t) = S(t) + \left[P_{eff} + I - K_c ET_0 - D - R\right] \Delta t
$$

### Step 6 — Impose Physical Constraint

$$
0 \le S(t) \le S_{FC}
$$

If $S > S_{FC}$, excess becomes $D$. If $S < 0$, set $S = 0$ and deficit becomes an **irrigation demand signal** to the controller.

**Engineering utility:** This model is the **backbone of IoT-based smart irrigation controllers** (e.g., Jain Logic, Netafim).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Model Selection Guide

> [!TIP]
> Use this matrix during KTU viva or short-answer questions when asked "Which model is appropriate for scenario X?"

| Scenario | Recommended Model Type | Reason | Example Software |
|---|---|---|---|
| Predict rice yield under climate change | Dynamic, mechanistic, distributed | Need full-season biophysics | DSSAT, APSIM |
| Schedule drip irrigation in real time | Dynamic, empirical (lumped) | Speed, low compute, online | FAO-56 spreadsheet |
| Estimate tractor fuel use on a slope | Static, empirical, deterministic | One-shot calculation | ASABE D497 standard |
| Model fungal disease outbreak | Dynamic, stochastic, hybrid | Weather uncertainty | WHEATPEST, PROCULTURE |
| Optimize greenhouse ventilation | Dynamic, mechanistic, lumped | Multiphysics control | Simulink, Dymola |
| Robot weed detection in row crops | Discrete-event / agent-based | Decision logic per plant | ROS + Gazebo |
| Dairy barn climate control | Dynamic, lumped, 1st-principles | Real-time control | Modelica, EnergyPlus |
| Watershed runoff after storm | Dynamic, distributed, semi-empirical | Spatially varying rainfall | SWAT, HEC-HMS |
| Soil N leaching in monoculture | Dynamic, mechanistic, distributed | 1D advection-dispersion | HYDRUS-1D |
| Livestock herd growth | Dynamic, stochastic (age-structured) | Demographic randomness | AnyLogic |

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    A2 --> A3[MATHEMATICAL MODEL]
    A3 --> A4[COMPUTER IMPLEMENTATION]
    A4 --> A5[VERIFICATION]
    A5 --> A6[VALIDATION]
    A6 --> A7{DATA MATCH}
    A7 -- NO --> A2
    A7 -- YES --> A8[EXPERIMENTATION AND PREDICTION]
    A8 --> A9[DECISION SUPPORT]
```

**Interpretation:**
- The cycle is **iterative**: validation failure routes back to the conceptual stage.
- **Verification** precedes **Validation**.
- The output is **decision support**, not raw data.

```

**Interpretation:** This is a **closed-loop IoT mechatronic system** for the farm. The cloud layer hosts the **model + simulation engine**; the actuator layer **physically perturbs** the field. Feedback is continuous.

flowchart LR

    C5[FERTILIZER INPUT] --> C1
    C6[MINERALIZATION FLOW] --> C1

```

**Interpretation:** Soil N stock receives three positive flows (fertilizer, mineralization, deposition, residue return) and loses two (plant uptake, leaching, denitrification). Mass balance closure is verified by $\sum \text{flows} = 0$.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Question style and marks distribution strictly follow **KTU 2024 Scheme**: Part A = 3 marks each, Part B = 14 marks with internal choice (7 + 7).

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Differentiate between a static and a dynamic agricultural model with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Static Model | Dynamic Model |
|---|---|---|
| Time dependence | No explicit time variable | Time $t$ is an explicit argument |
| Mathematics | Algebraic equations | Ordinary or partial differential equations |
| Output | Single value for given inputs | Trajectory over time |
| Example | Allometric yield equation $Y = aX^b$ | Logistic pest growth $\dfrac{dP}{dt} = rP(1 - P/K)$ |
| Use | Quick estimation, design charts | Real-time control, season-long simulation |

*(1 mark per distinguishing row; example earns the third mark.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Define the term 'Light Use Efficiency' (LUE) as used in crop growth modeling. State its typical value for C3 and C4 crops.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
LUE ($\varepsilon_s$) is the amount of **above-ground dry matter produced per unit of photosynthetically active radiation (PAR) intercepted** by the canopy (Monteith, 1972).

$$
\varepsilon_s = \frac{\text{Biomass produced (g/m}^2)}{\text{Intercepted PAR (MJ/m}^2)}
$$

Typical values:
- **C3 crops** (wheat, rice, soybean): **1.0–1.5 g/MJ**
- **C4 crops** (maize, sugarcane, sorghum): **1.8–2.5 g/MJ**

*(1 mark definition + equation, 1 mark typical C3 value, 1 mark C4 value.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **five-phase modeling and simulation pipeline** used in agricultural systems. Mention **verification vs validation** explicitly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A farmer's paddy field receives 30 mm of rainfall. Soil is clay loam with $\theta_{FC} = 0.32$ and $\theta_{WP} = 0.18$. Root zone depth is 60 cm. Initial water content is at 70% of available water capacity. Compute:
   (i) Total available water storage capacity.
   (ii) Water deficit at the start.
   (iii) Whether the rain event is sufficient to refill the root zone to field capacity. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to A(a) — 7 Marks

The five-phase pipeline (per KTU Module 2 syllabus):

1. **Problem definition** (1 mark): Identify system boundary, objective, accuracy needs.
2. **Conceptual model** (1 mark): Influence diagram, list state variables $x(t)$, inputs $u(t)$, parameters $\theta$.
3. **Mathematical model** (1 mark): ODEs/PDEs/algebraic equations with boundary conditions.
4. **Computational implementation** (1 mark): Choice of numerical solver (Euler, RK4) and platform.
5. **Verification & Validation** (2 marks):
   - **Verification**: Are the equations solved correctly? Compare with analytical solutions, convergence tests.
   - **Validation**: Is the right model being used? Compare simulated output with field observations using RMSE, R², NSE.
6. **Experimentation** (1 mark): Run scenarios, sensitivity analysis, what-if studies, then decision support.

#### Model Solution to A(b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Total Available Water Capacity (AWC)** (2 marks):

$$
AWC = (\theta_{FC} - \theta_{WP}) \cdot d_{root}
$$

$$
AWC = (0.32 - 0.18) \cdot 600 \text{ mm} = 0.14 \cdot 600 = 84 \text{ mm}
$$

**(ii) Initial Water Deficit** (2 marks):

Current water content is at 70% of AWC above wilting point.

$$
\theta_{init} = \theta_{WP} + 0.7 \cdot AWC_{fraction} = 0.18 + 0.7 \cdot (0.32 - 0.18) = 0.18 + 0.098 = 0.278
$$

Initial storage:

$$
S_{init} = 0.278 \cdot 600 = 166.8 \text{ mm}
$$

Deficit to field capacity:

$$
\text{Deficit} = S_{FC} - S_{init} = (0.32 \cdot 600) - 166.8 = 192 - 166.8 = \mathbf{25.2 \text{ mm}}
$$

**(iii) Is 30 mm rain sufficient?** (3 marks):

Effective rainfall (assume 80% efficiency): $P_{eff} = 0.8 \cdot 30 = 24$ mm.

Drainage occurs only if storage exceeds $S_{FC}$:

$$
S_{final} = S_{init} + P_{eff} - ET_0 \cdot \Delta t
$$

Assuming $ET_0 = 5$ mm/day for 1 day: $S_{final} = 166.8 + 24 - 5 = 185.8$ mm.

Since $S_{FC} = 192$ mm, the field is **not yet at field capacity**. Deficit remaining:

$$
192 - 185.8 = \mathbf{6.2 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Conclusion:** The 30 mm rain event is **not sufficient**; a supplementary irrigation of **6.2 mm** is required to refill the root zone to field capacity.

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (Internal Choice Alternative)

**(a)** Derive the **closed-form solution of the logistic growth equation** as applied to a pest population. Define all symbols. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A drip-irrigation system is controlled by a **feedback loop** using a soil-moisture sensor. Draw the **state-space block diagram** and write the **state equations** for the soil moisture dynamics. Use the bucket model with rainfall $P = 2$ mm/day, $ET_c = 4$ mm/day, drainage constant $k_d = 0.3$ /day, and sensor gain $K_s = 0.5$ V per (m³/m³). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to B(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1** (1 mark): The governing ODE for logistic growth:

$$
\frac{dP}{dt} = r P \left(1 - \frac{P}{K}\right)
$$

where $P$ = pest population density (insects/ha), $r$ = intrinsic growth rate (1/day), $K$ = environmental carrying capacity (insects/ha).

**Step 2** (1 mark): Separation of variables:

$$
\int \frac{dP}{P(K - P)} = \int \frac{r}{K} dt
$$

**Step 3** (1 mark): Partial fractions:

$$
\frac{1}{P(K - P)} = \frac{1}{K}\left(\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{K - P}\right)
$$

**Step 4** (1 mark): Integration:

$$
\frac{1}{K}\left[\ln \vert P \vert - \ln \vert K - P \vert\right] = \frac{r}{K} t + C
$$

$$
\ln\left(\frac{P}{K - P}\right) = r t + C_1
$$

**Step 5** (1 mark): Exponentiation:

$$
\frac{P}{K - P} = A \, e^{rt}, \quad A = \frac{P_0}{K - P_0}
$$

**Step 6** (1 mark): Closed-form solution (Verhulst, 1838):

$$
\boxed{\,P(t) = \frac{K}{1 + A e^{-rt}} = \frac{K}{1 + \left(\dfrac{K - P_0}{P_0}\right) e^{-rt}}\,}
$$

**Step 7** (1 mark): Engineering interpretation: The S-curve shows slow start-up (lag phase), exponential middle, and saturation. Decision support: integrate $P(t)$ to find time-to-economic-threshold, which sets the re-spray interval.

#### Model Solution to B(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Define the State Variable** (1 mark):

Let $x(t) = \theta(t)$ be the volumetric soil moisture (m³/m³) in the root zone.

**Step 2 — Define Inputs and Outputs** (1 mark):

- Input $u(t) = I(t)$: irrigation rate (mm/day)
- Disturbance $d(t) = P(t) - ET_c(t)$: net atmospheric forcing (mm/day)
- Output $y(t) = K_s \cdot x(t)$: sensor voltage (V)

**Step 3 — State Equation (Bucket Model)** (2 marks):

$$
\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{1}{d_{root}}\left[P - ET_c - k_d \max(0, x - \theta_{FC}) + I\right]
$$

Assuming operating below field capacity (no drainage):

$$
\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{1}{d_{root}}\left[(P - ET_c) + I\right]
$$

In standard state-space form:

$$
\dot{x} = a \, x + b \, u + w
$$

With the sensor dynamics:

$$
y = c \, x
$$

**Step 4 — Insert Numerical Values** (2 marks):

Let $d_{root} = 0.5$ m (converted to 500 mm). With $P = 2$, $ET_c = 4$ mm/day, $k_d = 0.3$ /day:

- $a = -k_d / d_{root} = -0.3 / 500 = -6 \times 10^{-4}$ /day (lumped with drainage term)
- $b = 1 / d_{root} = 1/500 = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ per mm
- $c = K_s = 0.5$ V per (m³/m³)

$$
\dot{x} = -6\times 10^{-4} \, x + 2 \times 10^{-3} \, u + w
$$

$$
y = 0.5 \, x
$$

**Step 5 — State-Space Block Diagram** (1 mark):

```
       u(t)  d(t)
         |    |
         v    v
   +----[ b ]----(+ )----->[ 1/(s - a) ]-----> x(t) ---->[ c ]----> y(t)
                                        |
                                  (feedback -a*x)
```

A simple proportional controller $u = K_p (x_{ref} - y)$ closes the loop, yielding **closed-loop pole** at $s = a - b K_p c$, which is used to tune $K_p$ for desired settling time (typically 6 hours = 0.25 day).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks — Common KTU valuation mistakes:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Verification with Validation** (–2 marks in 7-mark Qs): Verification = "solving equations right"; Validation = "solving right equations." Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the absolute-value safeguard in Van Genuchten equation** when writing in tables (–1 mark): Use $\vert \psi \vert$ or $\mid \psi \mid$, never a single pipe.
> 
> 3. **Mixing units in Penman-Monteith**: $R_n$ must be in **MJ/m²/day**, not W/m². Wind $u_2$ at exactly **2 m height** (use logarithmic profile correction if measured at other heights).
> 
> 4. **Ignoring drainage constraint in soil water models**: When $x > \theta_{FC}$, drainage must be subtracted; otherwise mass balance is violated.
> 
> 5. **Omitting the rationale for choosing a model type** in 7-mark questions: Examiners want at least **one sentence** justifying *why* mechanistic vs empirical, dynamic vs static, for the given scenario.
> 
> 6. **Wrong symbol for ET**: It is **$ET$ or $ET_0$** (reference), **$ET_c$** (crop) — never plain $E$ (which means evaporation only).
> 
> 7. **Mermaid diagrams in answer sheets**: KTU accepts hand-drawn or textual block diagrams. Keep them simple and labeled.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-density 30-second revision before entering the exam hall:**

- [ ] A **model** is a representation; a **simulation** is its execution. The combination is a **digital twin**.
- [ ] Five model axes: **Static/Dynamic, Deterministic/Stochastic, Empirical/Mechanistic, Continuous/Discrete, Lumped/Distributed** — remember **S-D-E-C-L**.
- [ ] **Verification** = solving equations right (code). **Validation** = solving the right equations (field).
- [ ] **Logistic growth** (Verhulst 1838): $P(t) = K / [1 + A e^{-rt}]$ where $A = (K - P_0)/P_0$.
- [ ] **Exponential decay** for pesticide: $C(t) = C_0 e^{-kt}$; half-life $t_{1/2} = \ln 2 / k$.
- [ ] **FAO-56 Penman-Monteith** is the standard for $ET_0$; multiply by $K_c$ for $ET_c$.
- [ ] **Richards equation** governs soil water flow; reduce to **Darcy's law** for saturated conditions.
- [ ] **Van Genuchten** equation describes the soil-water retention curve $\theta(\psi)$.
- [ ] **Monteith LUE** says yield $\propto$ intercepted radiation; $Y = \varepsilon_s \int S_{t,i} \prod f_i \, dt$.
- [ ] **System Dynamics** = stocks (levels) and flows (rates) with feedback loops (Forrester, 1961).
- [ ] **Logistic ODE** $\dfrac{dP}{dt} = rP(1 - P/K)$ is the most-tested KTU derivation; know all seven steps.
- [ ] **Field Capacity** $\theta_{FC} \approx 0.30$ (loam); **Wilting Point** $\theta_{WP} \approx 0.12$ (loam).
- [ ] **Drainage constraint** $D = \max(0, (S - S_{FC})/t_{drain})$ must be enforced in bucket models.
- [ ] **SCS Curve Number** runoff equation: $Q = (P - 0.2 S)^2 / (P + 0.8 S)$.
- [ ] **Lambert-Beer** canopy light: $I = I_0 e^{-k LAI}$ with $k \approx 0.5$–$0.7$.
- [ ] **State-space** form $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu$, $y = Cx + Du$ is the KTU standard for control-coupled models.
- [ ] **C3** LUE = 1.0–1.5 g/MJ; **C4** LUE = 1.8–2.5 g/MJ.
- [ ] Numerical solvers in order of accuracy: **Euler < Heun < RK4**.
- [ ] Validating metrics: **RMSE, R², NSE, MAE, Willmott's d index**.
- [ ] Common software: **MATLAB/Simulink, DSSAT, APSIM, APSIM Next Gen, STELLA, Modelica/Dymola, AnyLogic, SWAT, HYDRUS**.
- [ ] Mechatronic agricultural system layers: **Field → Sensor → Edge → Communication → Cloud → Decision → Actuator** (closed loop).
- [ ] **Cyber-Physical Agricultural System (CPAS)** = sensor + model + actuator in real-time closed loop.
- [ ] **Monte Carlo simulation** handles stochasticity (e.g., rainfall variability, pest outbreaks).
- [ ] **Dimensional consistency** check (SI units) is mandatory before implementation.
- [ ] Always state **assumptions and limitations** in the closing paragraph of a 7-mark answer.
- [ ] Use **flow diagrams** (Mermaid or hand-drawn) to earn full visual-communication marks.
- [ ] Final 30-second tip: If the question says "explain with example," always show **numbers** — the KTU valuation scheme awards partial credit generously for worked numerical examples.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Precision Farming and Automated Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT864 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Precision Farming and Automated Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Precision Farming and Automated Control

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> **Precision Farming (PF)** is an integrated, information-based agricultural management system that uses site-specific sensing, monitoring, and actuation technologies to optimize field-level crop production by applying the **Right Input, at the Right Place, at the Right Time, and in the Right Quantity** — commonly abbreviated as the **4R Principle**.
> **Automated Control** in agriculture refers to the closed-loop feedback architecture of mechatronic systems (sensors → controllers → actuators) used to regulate variables such as soil moisture, temperature, humidity, nutrient concentration, and pH without continuous human intervention.

### Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are watering a garden with a single hose. You either soak everything uniformly or you walk plant-to-plant guessing their needs. Now, replace yourself with a **smart drip-irrigation network** where:

1. Tiny **soil-moisture probes** in every pot "report" the wetness level.
2. A **mini-controller** (the brain) compares that reading with the desired wetness target.
3. It opens or closes the **drip valve** for *that specific pot only*.

That is Precision Farming — **treating every square meter of the field as an individual patient**, instead of giving the whole field the same medicine.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Synergy:**
> PF + Mechatronics = **Sensor-driven decision making + Closed-loop actuation**. The "smartness" of the farm is fundamentally a mechatronic problem (Module 2 of your syllabus precisely fuses these two domains).

### Foundational Vocabulary (Board-Exam High-Frequency Terms)

| Term | Formal Meaning | Mechatronic Element |
|---|---|---|
| **SITE-SPECIFIC CROP MANAGEMENT (SSCM)** | Treating within-field variability as the primary management unit | Data acquisition layer |
| **GEOGRAPHIC INFORMATION SYSTEM (GIS)** | Software platform that maps spatially-referenced data onto field coordinates | Information processing |
| **GLOBAL POSITIONING SYSTEM (GPS)** | Satellite-based coordinate determination with accuracy **±2.5 m** (civilian L1) and **±0.01 m** (RTK-GPS) | Spatial reference |
| **VARIABLE RATE TECHNOLOGY (VRT)** | Hardware/software that varies input application rates on-the-go based on a prescription map | Actuation layer |
| **TELEMETRY** | Wireless transmission of field sensor data to a central base | Communication layer |
| **YIELD MAPPING** | Geo-referenced recording of crop yield during harvest | Feedback measurement |
| **PRESCRIPTION MAP** | Pre-generated digital map instructing VRT *what* to apply *where* | Control reference |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics to Memorize for Board Exams:**
> - GPS Civilian Accuracy = **±2.5 m**
> - RTK-GPS Accuracy = **±1–2 cm**
> - Typical Soil-Moisture Sensor Operating Range = **0–100 % VWC** (Volumetric Water Content)
> - DGPS (Differential GPS) Accuracy = **±1–3 m**
> - Standard Sprayer Droplet Size Classification (VMD) = **100–400 µm**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Functional Layers of a Precision Farming System

Any precision-farming mechatronic system can be decomposed into five modular layers. The KTU examiner frequently frames theory questions around this layered architecture.

1. **DATA ACQUISITION LAYER (Sensing)**
   - **How:** Distributed sensor array (soil, climate, crop-canopy) continuously digitizes physical/chemical variables.
   - **Why:** Without accurate data, the controller is "blind" — garbage in, garbage out.

2. **DATA PROCESSING & DECISION LAYER (Computing)**
   - **How:** Microcontroller / PLC / Embedded PC runs algorithms (threshold, PID, fuzzy, neural) on incoming data.
   - **Why:** Converts raw ADC counts into actionable control decisions.

3. **COMMUNICATION LAYER (Networking)**
   - **How:** Wired (RS-485, CAN-bus) or wireless (LoRa, ZigBee, Wi-Fi, GSM) protocols shuttle data.
   - **Why:** Enables centralized monitoring of distributed field nodes.

4. **ACTUATION LAYER (Output Devices)**
   - **How:** Solenoid valves, VFD-driven pumps, stepper/servo motors on VRT applicators.
   - **Why:** Delivers the physical corrective action (water, fertilizer, pesticide).

5. **HUMAN-MACHINE INTERFACE (HMI) LAYER**
   - **How:** Touchscreen panels, SCADA dashboards, mobile applications.
   - **Why:** Allows farmer supervision, override, and historical analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** When asked to "explain the block diagram of a precision-farming system," always sketch the **closed-loop** flow: **Sensor → Signal Conditioning → ADC → Controller → DAC/Driver → Actuator → Process (Field) → Sensor (feedback)**. Mention at least one example of each block to score full marks.

## 2.2 Sensors Used in Precision Farming (Board-Favourite Topic)

| Sensor | Variable Measured | Typical Output | Mechatronic Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **TDR (Time Domain Reflectometry)** | Soil moisture | Voltage (mV) | Irrigation scheduling |
| **Capacitive Soil Sensor** | Soil moisture | Frequency / Voltage | Low-cost IoT nodes |
| **Thermistor (NTC/PTC)** | Air / soil temperature | Resistance change | Greenhouse climate |
| **DHT22 / SHT31** | Humidity + Temperature | Digital (I²C) | Weather stations |
| **pH Electrode** | Soil/water pH | mV (Nernst) | Fertigation control |
| **EC Sensor** | Electrical conductivity (salinity) | mV / µS cm⁻¹ | Nutrient dosing |
| **NDVI Optical Sensor** | Crop canopy reflectance | Index (0–1) | Crop-health mapping |
| **Pyranometer** | Solar radiation (W m⁻²) | mV | Greenhouse shading |
| **LDR / Photodiode** | Light intensity | Resistance / current | Auto-daylength control |

## 2.3 The Nernst Equation (Foundational for pH Sensors)

The **pH sensor** in a fertigation system is a classic exam topic. The electrode develops a potential given by the **Nernst Equation**:

$$
E \;=\; E^{\circ} \;-\; \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q
$$

where:

- $E$ = measured cell potential (**Volts**)
- $E^{\circ}$ = standard electrode potential (**Volts**)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (**K**)
- $n$ = number of transferred electrons
- $F$ = Faraday constant = **96,485 C mol⁻¹**
- $Q$ = reaction quotient

At the standard temperature $T = 298\,\text{K}$, the thermal voltage becomes:

$$
\frac{RT}{F} \;=\; \frac{8.314 \times 298}{96485} \;\approx\; 0.02569 \text{ V}
$$

## 2.4 Variable Rate Technology (VRT) — Two Architectural Modes

- **MAP-BASED VRT** — A pre-loaded prescription map drives the actuator. Pros: deterministic, auditable. Cons: ignores real-time variability.
- **SENSOR-BASED VRT** — On-the-go optical sensors (e.g., GreenSeeker, Yara N-Sensor) sense crop reflectance in real time and modulate the actuator instantly.

## 2.5 Automated Greenhouse Control — PID Foundation

Greenhouse temperature / humidity control is universally implemented as a **PID (Proportional–Integral–Derivative)** loop. The control output is given by:

$$
u(t) \;=\; K_{p}\,e(t) \;+\; K_{i}\int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau \;+\; K_{d}\,\frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

| Term | Symbol | Effect | Consequence if too high |
|---|---|---|---|

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Volumetric Water Content (VWC) from Capacitance Reading

A capacitive soil-moisture sensor measures the **dielectric permittivity** of the surrounding soil. The relationship between capacitance and VWC can be derived starting from the parallel-plate model.

### Step 1 — Parallel-Plate Capacitance Equation

For a parallel-plate capacitor of area $A$ and plate separation $d$, the capacitance is given by:

$$
C \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_{r}\,\varepsilon_{0}\,A}{d}
$$

Here, $\varepsilon_{0} = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F m⁻¹ is the vacuum permittivity, and $\varepsilon_{r}$ is the relative permittivity (dielectric constant) of the soil medium.

**Logical step:** The capacitance is **directly proportional** to the dielectric constant of the material filling the gap between the plates. Therefore, changes in soil moisture (which change $\varepsilon_{r}$) translate linearly into changes in measured capacitance.

### Step 2 — Soil as a Three-Phase Dielectric Mixture

Soil is treated as a mixture of three phases: **soil solids**, **air**, and **water**. The bulk dielectric constant is governed by the Topp equation (the industry-standard empirical relationship):

$$
\theta \;=\; -5.3 \times 10^{-2} \;+\; 2.92 \times 10^{-2}\,\varepsilon_{r} \;-\; 5.5 \times 10^{-4}\,\varepsilon_{r}^{\,2} \;+\; 4.3 \times 10^{-6}\,\varepsilon_{r}^{\,3}
$$

where $\theta$ is the **Volumetric Water Content (VWC)** in m³ water per m³ soil.

### Step 3 — Converting Measured Capacitance to Dielectric Constant

The sensor electronics convert measured capacitance into a frequency $f$ using an **astable multivibrator** topology:

$$
f \;=\; \frac{1}{2\,R\,C\,\ln 2}
$$

Substituting $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{r}\varepsilon_{0}A}{d}$:

$$
f \;=\; \frac{d}{2\,R\,\varepsilon_{r}\,\varepsilon_{0}\,A\,\ln 2}
$$

**Logical step:** Rearranging for $\varepsilon_{r}$:

$$
\varepsilon_{r} \;=\; \frac{d}{2\,R\,\varepsilon_{0}\,A\,\ln 2 \cdot f}
$$

### Step 4 — Final Mapping Function (Sensor Output → VWC)

Substituting Step 3 into the Topp equation yields the complete end-to-end mapping from sensor frequency to VWC:

$$
\theta \;=\; -5.3 \times 10^{-2} \;+\; 2.92 \times 10^{-2}\!\left(\frac{d}{2R\varepsilon_{0}A\ln 2\,f}\right) \;-\; 5.5 \times 10^{-4}\!\left(\frac{d}{2R\varepsilon_{0}A\ln 2\,f}\right)^{2} \;+\; 4.3 \times 10^{-6}\!\left(\frac{d}{2R\varepsilon_{0}A\ln 2\,f}\right)^{3}
$$

This non-linear polynomial is typically **linearized in firmware** by the microcontroller to give the farmer a direct m³/m³ (or %) reading.

## 3.2 Derivation: PID Tuning for Greenhouse Temperature (Ziegler–Nichols Method)

Suppose a greenhouse heating loop has critical gain $K_{u}$ and oscillation period $P_{u}$. Applying the **Ziegler–Nichols First Method**, the PID parameters are computed from:

$$
K_{p} \;=\; 0.60\,K_{u}
$$

$$
K_{i} \;=\; \frac{2\,K_{p}}{P_{u}}
$$

$$
K_{d} \;=\; \frac{K_{p}\,P_{u}}{8}
$$

**Example:** Let $K_{u} = 8.0$ and $P_{u} = 60$ s (oscillation period observed at the ultimate gain).

$$
K_{p} = 0.60 \times 8.0 = 4.8
$$

$$
K_{i} = \frac{2 \times 4.8}{60} = 0.16 \;\text{s}^{-1}
$$

$$
K_{d} = \frac{4.8 \times 60}{8} = 36 \;\text{s}
$$

**Logical step:** Substitute these values into the canonical PID equation to obtain the final control law:

$$
u(t) \;=\; 4.8\,e(t) \;+\; 0.16\!\int_{0}^{t}\! e(\tau)\,d\tau \;+\; 36\,\frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Sensor for Which Variable?

| Variable | Best Sensor Choice | Rationale | Avoid Because |
|---|---|---|---|
| Soil moisture (deep) | TDR | High accuracy, robust in saline soils | Cost |
| Soil moisture (IoT) | Capacitive | Low cost, digital-ready | Needs field calibration |
| Air temperature | SHT31 | I²C, factory-calibrated | Bulky analog ICs |
| Crop canopy health | NDVI / Multispectral | Direct chlorophyll correlation | Requires sunlight |
| GPS position | RTK-GPS | ±1 cm accuracy for VRT | Needs base-station |
| Soil pH | Glass electrode (Nernst) | Standardized, lab-grade | Fragile, needs refill |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Closed-Loop Architecture of a Precision-Irrigation Node

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **block-level functional architecture** of a single mechatronic irrigation node. Note the explicit **feedback loop** from the soil back to the controller — this is the key signature of any precision-farming automation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Soil Field Process] --> B[Soil Moisture Sensor TDR or Capacitive]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning Amplifier and Filter]
    C --> D[ADC Analog to Digital Converter]
    D --> E[Microcontroller Decision Engine]
    E --> F[PWM Driver]
    F --> G[Solenoid Valve Actuator]
    G --> A
    E -.-> H[LoRa Wireless Link]
    H -.-> I[Central Gateway SCADA]
    I -.-> J[Cloud Dashboard Mobile App]
    E -.-> K[Local LCD Touch HMI]
```

**Reading guide (for exam answers):**
- Solid arrows = real-time closed-loop signal flow.
- Dashed arrows = supervisory communication (non-real-time).
- The cycle `A → B → C → D → E → F → G → A` is the **inner control loop** running at high frequency.
- The dashed branch to SCADA/cloud is the **outer monitoring loop** for analytics and historical logging.

## 4.2 Functional Layer Diagram of Precision Farming

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Layer 1 Sensing Field Sensors] --> L2[Layer 2 Processing Embedded Controller]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3 Communication Wireless Wired Bus]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4 Actuation Valves Pumps Motors]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5 HMI Touch SCADA Mobile App]
    L2 --> L6[GPS and GIS Module]
    L6 --> L3
    L5 --> L2
```

## 4.3 Variable Rate Technology Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[GPS Receiver Reads Position] --> B{Decision Mode}
    B -- Map Based --> C[Load Prescription Map from SD Card]
    B -- Sensor Based --> D[Read NDVI Optical Sensor Live]
    C --> E[Compute Target Rate kg per hectare]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Send PWM Command to Stepper Motor]
    F --> G[Stepper Rotates Metering Roller]
    G --> H[Fertilizer Drops at Variable Rate]
```

## 4.4 Greenhouse Climate Control — Multi-Loop Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    T1[Temperature Loop Sensor Thermistor] --> CTRL[Central PLC or Microcontroller]
    H1[Humidity Loop Sensor DHT22] --> CTRL
    C1[CO2 Loop Sensor NDIR] --> CTRL
    L1[Light Loop Sensor Pyranometer] --> CTRL
    CTRL --> ACT1[Heater and Fan]
    CTRL --> ACT2[Mister and Exhaust]
    CTRL --> ACT3[CO2 Solenoid]
    CTRL --> ACT4[Shade Net Motor]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why block-level (and not pictorial) diagrams?**
> Mechatronic precision-farming systems are highly distributed. Mermaid cannot render an accurate tractor, but it **can** render the control/data flow crisply — which is what examiners reward in theory answers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Precision Farming. State any four benefits.
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 — Model Paper Set B]**

**Model Answer:**

Precision farming is an integrated, site-specific crop management strategy that employs **GPS, GIS, remote sensing, and variable-rate technology** to apply agricultural inputs (water, fertilizer, pesticide) according to localized field conditions rather than uniform blanket rates.

**Four benefits:**
1. **Input optimization** — reduces fertilizer/water wastage by 20–40 %.
2. **Yield improvement** — typical yield gain of 10–15 % through targeted intervention.
3. **Environmental protection** — minimizes leaching and runoff of agro-chemicals.
4. **Economic viability** — higher profit margin per hectare despite higher initial CapEx.

---

### Q2. List the major components of a Variable Rate Technology (VRT) system.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Supplementary]**

**Model Answer:**

1. **DGPS / RTK-GPS receiver** — for real-time field position (±1–2 cm).
2. **Prescription map** — pre-loaded into the on-board computer.
3. **Field computer / controller** — executes the rate-lookup algorithm.
4. **Actuator** — typically a **stepper/servo motor** driving a metering roller.
5. **Flow sensor** — closed-loop verification of actual applied rate.
6. **Operator HMI** — touchscreen for calibration and live monitoring.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - When asked for a "block diagram," students often **omit the feedback arrow** from the process back to the sensor. This alone costs **2 marks**.
> - In PID-related questions, examiners check whether you have **defined $e(t)$** (error = set-point − measured). Skipping this earns a zero on the conceptual half.
> - Always include **units** for every numerical answer. A bare "36" without "seconds" loses a mark.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Draw and explain the **closed-loop block diagram of an automated drip-irrigation system** used in precision farming. Label every block clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A capacitive soil-moisture sensor has a parallel-plate geometry with $A = 50$ mm², $d = 3$ mm. The timer resistor $R = 10$ kΩ drives the sensor via an astable multivibrator. If the measured output frequency is **$f = 42$ kHz**, compute the **relative dielectric constant $\varepsilon_{r}$** of the soil. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a)** The block diagram is the **closed-loop irrigation loop** already given in Section 4.1. The student should reproduce it and explicitly annotate:

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| Soil Moisture Sensor | Converts soil water content into capacitance change |
| Signal Conditioner | Amplifies and low-pass filters the millivolt-level signal |
| ADC | Converts analog voltage to digital count for the MCU |
| Microcontroller | Compares count to set-point; computes error; generates PWM |
| Driver Stage | Boosts PWM logic-level to solenoid-rated current |
| Solenoid Valve | Opens/closes water flow |
| Soil (Process) | The plant root zone being irrigated |
| Feedback Path | Closes the loop from soil back to sensor |

**(b)** Computation:

Given:
- $A = 50 \text{ mm}^2 = 50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$
- $d = 3 \text{ mm} = 3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
- $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega = 10^{4} \text{ }\Omega$
- $f = 42 \text{ kHz} = 4.2 \times 10^{4} \text{ Hz}$

Using the relation derived in Section 3.1, Step 3:

$$
\varepsilon_{r} \;=\; \frac{d}{2\,R\,\varepsilon_{0}\,A\,\ln 2 \cdot f}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\varepsilon_{r} \;=\; \frac{3 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 10^{4} \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.693 \times 4.2 \times 10^{4}}
$$

Numerator: $3 \times 10^{-3}$.

Denominator (multiplied step-by-step):

$$
2 \times 10^{4} \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} = 1.7708 \times 10^{-7}
$$

$$
1.7708 \times 10^{-7} \times 50 \times 10^{-6} = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}
$$

$$
8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.693 = 6.136 \times 10^{-12}
$$

$$
6.136 \times 10^{-12} \times 4.2 \times 10^{4} = 2.577 \times 10^{-7}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\varepsilon_{r} \;=\; \frac{3 \times 10^{-3}}{2.577 \times 10^{-7}} \;\approx\; 1.164 \times 10^{4}
$$

A relative dielectric constant of ~11,640 is **physically unrealistic for bulk soil** (typical $\varepsilon_{r}$ of dry soil ≈ 3–5, wet soil ≈ 15–30). This indicates the assumed geometry yields an unrealistically high value — students should note that **field calibration** is mandatory, and the Topp equation (Section 3.1) is the practical bridge from $\varepsilon_{r}$ to VWC. **(Examiner commentary: full marks awarded if the numerical substitution is correct AND the student comments on the calibration requirement.)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the working principle of **GPS-guided Variable Rate Fertilization** with a neat sketch. Differentiate between **map-based** and **sensor-based** VRT. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A greenhouse heating system is modelled as a first-order plant with transfer function:

$$
G(s) \;=\; \frac{1}{20s + 1}
$$

Design a **PID controller** using the **Ziegler–Nichols first method** given that the ultimate gain $K_{u} = 25$ and ultimate period $P_{u} = 80$ s. Write the final control law in time domain. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a)** **Working Principle of GPS-Guided VRT:**

A tractor-mounted VRT applicator consists of:
1. **GPS antenna** mounted on the cab roof — receives satellite signals.
2. **DGPS / RTK receiver** corrects signal to ±1 cm accuracy.
3. **On-board field computer** — looks up the prescription rate for the tractor's current (x, y) coordinates.
4. **Controller card** — sends PWM commands proportional to the required rate.
5. **Stepper/servo motor** — rotates a metering roller inside the fertilizer hopper.
6. **Flow sensor** — provides closed-loop verification that the commanded rate is actually delivered.

**Differentiation:**

| Parameter | Map-Based VRT | Sensor-Based VRT |
|---|---|---|
| Data source | Pre-loaded prescription map | Real-time optical (e.g., NDVI) sensor |
| Latency | None (offline lookup) | Real-time |
| Cost | Lower | Higher (needs optical hardware) |
| Adaptability to season | Requires map re-issue | Auto-adapts |
| Best use case | Stable fields, contract farming | Highly variable fields, R&D |

**(b)** **PID Tuning Computation:**

Given:
- $K_{u} = 25$
- $P_{u} = 80 \text{ s}$

Applying Ziegler–Nichols first method:

$$
K_{p} \;=\; 0.60\,K_{u} \;=\; 0.60 \times 25 \;=\; 15
$$

$$
K_{i} \;=\; \frac{2\,K_{p}}{P_{u}} \;=\; \frac{2 \times 15}{80} \;=\; 0.375 \;\text{s}^{-1}
$$

$$
K_{d} \;=\; \frac{K_{p}\,P_{u}}{8} \;=\; \frac{15 \times 80}{8} \;=\; 150 \;\text{s}
$$

**Final control law in time domain:**

$$
u(t) \;=\; 15\,e(t) \;+\; 0.375\!\int_{0}^{t}\! e(\tau)\,d\tau \;+\; 150\,\frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

where $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ is the error between the set-point temperature $r(t)$ and the measured temperature $y(t)$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Do not confuse $K_{i}$ (integral gain in s⁻¹) with $K_{p}/T_{i}$ (where $T_{i}$ is the integral time constant). They are reciprocals. Many students write $K_{i} = 2K_{p}/P_{u}$ but then forget to invert it later. Keep the units clean: $K_{p}$ is dimensionless (or has plant-specific units), $K_{i}$ has units of s⁻¹, $K_{d}$ has units of s.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Precision Farming** = 4R Principle (Right Input, Right Place, Right Time, Right Quantity).
- [x] **Five-layer PF architecture** = Sense → Process → Communicate → Actuate → HMI.
- [x] **Closed-loop block diagram** must include the feedback arrow from the *process* back to the *sensor* — examiners deduct 2 marks if missing.
- [x] **GPS accuracy hierarchy:** Civilian GPS (2.5 m) → DGPS (1–3 m) → RTK-GPS (1–2 cm).
- [x] **VRT two modes:** Map-based (offline lookup) vs Sensor-based (real-time optical NDVI).
- [x] **Topp equation** is the canonical bridge from soil dielectric constant $\varepsilon_{r}$ to VWC $\theta$.
- [x] **Nernst equation** governs pH-electrode output: $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$.
- [x] **PID canonical form:** $u(t) = K_{p}\,e(t) + K_{i}\!\int e\,dt + K_{d}\,\dfrac{de}{dt}$.
- [x] **Ziegler–Nichols first method:** $K_{p} = 0.6 K_{u}$, $K_{i} = 2K_{p}/P_{u}$, $K_{d} = K_{p}P_{u}/8$.
- [x] **Mandatory units** on every numerical answer — bare numbers lose marks.
- [x] **Always state the error definition** $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ when writing a control law.
- [x] **Communication protocols** likely to appear: LoRa, ZigBee, CAN-bus, RS-485, GSM.
- [x] **Telemetry** = wireless remote monitoring; **SCADA** = centralized supervisory software.
- [x] **Yield map** = historical record; **Prescription map** = forward-looking control reference.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robotics in Agriculture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT864 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Robotics in Agriculture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robotics in Agriculture** is the integrated application of **mechatronic systems** — combining mechanical manipulators, electronic sensors, embedded controllers, and intelligent computer algorithms — to perform agricultural operations (sowing, weeding, spraying, harvesting, monitoring) with minimal human intervention, achieving the objectives of **Precision Agriculture (PA)** and **Automation of Field Operations (AFO)**.

According to the KTU 2024 Mechatronics syllabus, agricultural robotics sits at the intersection of three core pillars:

1. **Mechanical Subsystem** — Mobile platforms (UGV - Unmanned Ground Vehicle, or tracked/wheeled bases), robotic arms, and end-effectors (grippers, cutters, sprayers).
2. **Electronic & Sensor Subsystem** — Vision cameras (RGB, multispectral, hyperspectral), LiDAR, GPS/GNSS, IMU, soil moisture and pH probes, encoders.
3. **Control & Intelligence Subsystem** — Embedded microcontrollers, real-time path planning, computer vision pipelines, and AI-based crop/weed classification.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Smart Farm Hand" Analogy**
>
> Imagine a traditional farm labourer who, over 30 years, has learned to identify a ripe tomato by its slight red blush, pick it without bruising, and avoid stepping on the irrigation pipe. Now imagine transferring that *experience* into a **robotic arm mounted on a mobile rover** equipped with a camera "eye" (vision system) and a sensitive "hand" (force-controlled gripper). The rover acts as the labourer's "legs," the arm as the "torso," the gripper as the "hand," and the AI vision software as the "memory." This is, in essence, **Agricultural Robotics** — digitising the tactile skill of a farmer into a programmable, tireless, 24/7 mechatronic system.

The key performance metrics used in KTU-level evaluation of any agricultural robot are:

- **Field Capacity (ha/h)** — area covered per hour
- **Crop Detection Accuracy (%)** — vision system reliability
- **Operational Endurance (h)** — battery/runtime life
- **Path Tracking Error (cm)** — GPS/INS navigation precision

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (Bolded Constants & Units)

| Parameter | Standard Unit | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| GPS Positional Accuracy (RTK-GPS) | **centimetre (cm)** | **±2 cm** |
| Camera Resolution (precision ag) | **megapixel (MP)** | **5–20 MP** |
| Robotic Arm Payload (harvester) | **kilogram (kg)** | **2–15 kg** |
| Mobile Platform Speed | **metre/second (m/s)** | **0.5–2.5 m/s** |
| Spraying Flow Rate | **litre/hectare (L/ha)** | **50–200 L/ha** |
| Battery Endurance | **hour (h)** | **4–12 h** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module emphasises understanding *why* sensor fusion (GPS + IMU + Vision) is essential for autonomous navigation in unstructured, GPS-denied, or partially-shaded field environments (e.g., inside a polyhouse or orchard canopy).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown of an Agricultural Robot Mission

A typical agricultural robotic mission can be decomposed into the following **5 sequential logic steps**:

1. **Mission Planning (Off-board)**
   - The farmer uploads a **Geo-referenced Field Map (GFM)** generated from satellite/UAV imagery.
   - Waypoints, no-go zones (water bodies, rocks), and crop rows are encoded.
   - Path planner computes an optimal **Boustrophedon path** (back-and-forth, like an ox ploughing) to maximise coverage.

2. **Localisation & Navigation (On-board)**
   - The robot uses **GNSS (Global Navigation Satellite System)** for absolute position and **IMU + Wheel Odometry** for relative motion.
   - A **Kalman Filter (KF)** or **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** fuses these to estimate pose $\hat{x}_t = [x, y, \theta]^T$.

3. **Perception & Sensing**
   - Forward-facing RGB or multispectral camera captures images of crops.
   - A trained **CNN (Convolutional Neural Network)** classifies each pixel/object as *crop*, *weed*, *soil*, or *obstacle*.

4. **Decision & Actuation**
   - Based on classification, a control law activates the **end-effector**:
     - Crop → gripper harvests / records health.
     - Weed → precision sprayer / mechanical hoe deploys.
     - Obstacle → path replanner triggers.

5. **Data Logging & Telemetry**
   - Yield maps, weed density maps, and soil variability maps are uploaded to a **Farm Management Information System (FMIS)** for closed-loop precision agriculture.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these steps matter:** Without proper *sensor fusion* (Step 2), a GPS dropout under a tree canopy could cause the robot to drift into a crop row — destroying yield. This is a **favourite KTU question** on the *need for redundancy in mechatronic sensing*.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** The vertical pipe symbol `|` is replaced with `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula / Equation | LaTeX Form | Variables & Units | Real-World Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Field Capacity** | $C = \dfrac{W \cdot v}{10}$ | $C$: ha/h, $W$: width in m, $v$: speed in m/s | Predicts daily coverage of a harvester/sprayer |
| 2 | **Effective Field Capacity** | $C_{eff} = C \cdot \eta$ | $\eta$: field efficiency (0.6–0.85) | Accounts for turning, refilling, overlaps |
| 3 | **Theoretical Spray Volume** | $Q = \dfrac{f \cdot v \cdot W}{600}$ | $Q$: L/min, $f$: L/ha, $W$: m, $v$: km/h | Nozzle sizing & pump selection |
| 4 | **DH Forward Kinematics** | $T_n^0 = A_1(\theta_1) \cdot A_2(\theta_2) \cdots A_n(\theta_n)$ | $A_i$: $4 \times 4$ homogeneous transform | End-effector pose in world frame |
| 5 | **Single Joint DH Transform** | $A_i = \begin{bmatrix} c\theta_i & -s\theta_i c\alpha_i & s\theta_i s\alpha_i & a_i c\theta_i \\ s\theta_i & c\theta_i c\alpha_i & -c\theta_i s\alpha_i & a_i s\theta_i \\ 0 & s\alpha_i & c\alpha_i & d_i \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $\theta, d, a, \alpha$: DH params | Building arm model for a fruit picker |
| 6 | **Jacobian Velocity Mapping** | $\dot{x} = J(\theta) \dot{\theta}$ | $J$: $m \times n$ Jacobian | Cartesian speed of gripper |
| 7 | **GPS Dilution of Precision** | $HDOP = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2} \mid \sigma_{satellite}$ | $HDOP$: dimensionless | Predicts navigation error |
| 8 | **Camera Ground Sampling Distance** | $GSD = \dfrac{H \cdot p}{f}$ | $GSD$: m/pixel, $H$: altitude (m), $p$: pixel pitch (m), $f$: focal length (m) | UAV image resolution planning |
| 9 | **Weed Detection Accuracy** | $A_{cc} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | $TP, TN, FP, FN$: confusion matrix | Evaluates ML classifier in field |
| 10 | **Boustrophedon Path Length** | $L = N \cdot \left(W_{row} + R_{turn}\right)$ | $N$: rows, $W_{row}$: row length, $R_{turn}$: turning radius | Total mission distance estimation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** The *Field Capacity* and *DH Forward Kinematics* formulas are **most frequently asked** in 14-mark questions. Master their derivations and unit conversions.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Japan's Tomato Harvesting Robots (e.g., Panasonic, SPREAD):** Use 6-DOF arms with vision to harvest truss tomatoes in greenhouses, achieving **±2 mm** placement accuracy.
- **Weed-Specific Spot Spraying (e.g., John Deere See & Spray):** Uses CNNs + cameras to reduce herbicide use by up to **77%**, saving millions of litres globally.
- **Autonomous Tractors (e.g., CNH, AGCO):** Use **RTK-GPS** to maintain **±2.5 cm** row-to-row accuracy, enabling controlled traffic farming.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Effective Field Capacity from First Principles

The *Theoretical Field Capacity (TFC)* is the maximum possible area a machine can cover per unit time, assuming no idle periods. The *Effective Field Capacity (EFC)* is the actual coverage after accounting for **turning time, refilling, overlap, and obstacle avoidance**.

### Step 1: Define the area covered in one pass

If the implement has working width $W$ (metres) and the platform moves at constant forward speed $v$ (m/s), the area swept in time $t$ is:

$$A = W \cdot v \cdot t$$

### Step 2: Convert to hectares

Since $1 \text{ hectare} = 10{,}000 \text{ m}^2$, the area in hectares is:

$$A_{ha} = \dfrac{W \cdot v \cdot t}{10{,}000}$$

### Step 3: Derive Theoretical Field Capacity (TFC)

Setting $t = 3600$ s (one hour) gives the TFC in ha/h:

$$TFC = \dfrac{W \cdot v \cdot 3600}{10{,}000} = \dfrac{W \cdot v}{2.778}$$

But, conventional agricultural engineering simplifies by using $v$ in **km/h** instead of m/s. Since $1 \text{ km/h} = \dfrac{1}{3.6} \text{ m/s}$:

$$TFC = \dfrac{W \cdot (v_{km/h} / 3.6)}{2.778} = \dfrac{W \cdot v_{km/h}}{10}$$

$$\boxed{\therefore TFC = \dfrac{W \cdot v}{10} \quad \text{with } W \text{ in m, } v \text{ in km/h, result in ha/h}}$$

> This matches the standard formula taught in KTU Module 2 and verified by *ASABE Standards D497.7*.

### Step 4: Apply Field Efficiency $\eta$

The field efficiency accounts for the *time lost* in turning at row ends, overlapping, refilling spray tanks, and stopping for obstacles. The Effective Field Capacity is:

$$EFC = TFC \cdot \eta$$

For example, an autonomous weeding robot with $W = 3$ m, $v = 6$ km/h, and $\eta = 0.75$:

$$EFC = \dfrac{3 \cdot 6}{10} \cdot 0.75 = 1.8 \cdot 0.75 = 1.35 \text{ ha/h}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: DH Forward Kinematics for a 2-DOF Agricultural Picker's Arm

Consider a simple **2-DOF planar manipulator** used in a strawberry-picking robot. Joint 1 (shoulder) rotates about the vertical axis by $\theta_1$ and is offset vertically by $d_1$. Joint 2 (elbow) has link length $a_2$ and rotates by $\theta_2$.

### Step 1: Write the DH Parameter Table

| Joint $i$ | $a_i$ (m) | $\alpha_i$ | $d_i$ (m) | $\theta_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | $90^\circ$ | $d_1$ | $\theta_1$ |
| 2 | $a_2$ | $0^\circ$ | 0 | $\theta_2$ |

### Step 2: Build $A_1$ transformation

Using the standard DH matrix:

$$A_1 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_1 & 0 & \sin\theta_1 & 0 \\ \sin\theta_1 & 0 & -\cos\theta_1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & d_1 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 3: Build $A_2$ transformation

$$A_2 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_2 & -\sin\theta_2 & 0 & a_2 \cos\theta_2 \\ \sin\theta_2 & \cos\theta_2 & 0 & a_2 \sin\theta_2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 4: Multiply to obtain $T_2^0$

$$T_2^0 = A_1 \cdot A_2$$

The position of the end-effector $(x_e, y_e, z_e)$ is:

$$x_e = a_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$

$$y_e = a_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$

$$z_e = d_1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** $z_e = d_1$ means the gripper height above the ground is fixed by the vertical pedestal — useful for ground-level fruit pickers. $x_e, y_e$ trace a circle of radius $a_2$ as the arm sweeps.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing Sensors for an Agricultural Robot

| Sensor | Cost | Accuracy | Works Indoors | Works at Night | Detects Weed/Crop | Best Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **RTK-GPS** | High | **±2 cm** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | Open-field tractor auto-steering |
| **RGB Camera** | Low | Pixel-level | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (with ML) | Fruit/weed detection (daylight) |
| **Multispectral Camera** | Medium | Spectral | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅✅ Yes | Crop health (NDVI) |
| **LiDAR** | High | **±1 cm** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | Obstacle avoidance, canopy mapping |
| **IMU (9-DOF)** | Low | Drift ~1°/min | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | Short-term motion estimation |
| **Ultrasonic** | Very Low | **±3 cm** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | Low-cost ground proximity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** No single sensor is sufficient. **Sensor Fusion** (e.g., GPS + IMU + Vision) is the *mechatronic* answer to field variability. This is a high-yield 7-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Agricultural Robot

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete mechatronic signal flow of an autonomous weeding/harvesting robot.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PLANNING[Off-board Mission Planner]
        A1[Geo-referenced Field Map]
        A2[Boustrophedon Path Generator]
        A1 --> A2
    end

    subgraph NAV[On-board Navigation Stack]
        B1[GNSS Receiver]
        B2[IMU 9-DOF]
        B3[Wheel Odometry]
        B4[Extended Kalman Filter]
        B1 --> B4
        B2 --> B4
        B3 --> B4
        B4 --> B5[Pose Estimate x, y, theta]
    end

    subgraph PERCEPT[Perception and Vision]
        C1[RGB Camera]
        C2[Multispectral Sensor]
        C3[LiDAR Scanner]
        C4[CNN Classifier]
        C1 --> C4
        C2 --> C4
        C3 --> C4
        C4 --> C5[Object Class: Crop or Weed or Obstacle]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL[Real-time Controller]
        D1[STM32 / Jetson Nano SBC]
        D2[Path Re-planner]
        D3[Motion Controller PID]
        D1 --> D2
        D1 --> D3
    end

    subgraph ACTUATE[Actuation Layer]
        E1[BLDC Motor Drivers]
        E2[Steering Servo]
        E3[End-effector Gripper]
        E4[Precision Nozzle Sprayer]
        E1 --> E2
        E3 --> E5[Harvested Fruit Bin]
        E4 --> E6[Targeted Weed Zone]
    end

    subgraph TELEMETRY[Telemetry and FMIS]
        F1[WiFi / 4G Module]
        F2[Cloud Farm Management System]
        F1 --> F2
    end

    A2 --> B5
    B5 --> D2
    C5 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> E1
    D3 --> E3
    D3 --> E4
    C5 --> F1
```

## 4.2 Workflow Sequence — Mission Lifecycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant Op as Operator
    participant MIS as Farm MIS Cloud
    participant Bot as Agri Robot
    participant Vis as Vision System
    participant Arm as Robotic Arm
    participant Grip as Gripper

    Op->>MIS: Upload field boundary and crop map
    MIS-->>Bot: Send mission waypoints via 4G
    Bot->>Bot: Localise with GNSS + IMU
    Bot->>Vis: Capture image at waypoint
    Vis->>Bot: Return bounding box of ripe fruit
    Bot->>Arm: Command joint angles theta1, theta2
    Arm->>Grip: Open gripper
    Bot->>Grip: Close on fruit
    Bot->>Bot: Place in bin, log yield
    Bot-->>MIS: Upload yield map and weed map
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Sequence Diagram:** Each numbered arrow is a critical *handshake* between subsystems. A failure in any handshake (e.g., misclassified bounding box) cascades into a physical error. KTU questions often test the *consequence of a single-point failure* in such a mechatronic chain.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**Define Precision Agriculture. List any four mechatronic subsystems used in an autonomous agricultural robot.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Precision Agriculture (PA)** is an information-based, site-specific farm management strategy that uses **Mechatronic technologies** (sensors, controllers, actuators, and software) to optimise inputs (water, fertiliser, pesticide) and maximise yield, on a per-square-metre basis.

**Four mechatronic subsystems:**

1. **Sensing Subsystem** — GPS/GNSS, IMU, RGB/ Multispectral cameras, soil moisture probes.
2. **Control Subsystem** — Embedded microcontrollers (STM32, Arduino, Jetson Nano) running PID or AI inference.
3. **Actuation Subsystem** — BLDC drive motors, hydraulic steering, robotic arm grippers, sprayer solenoids.
4. **Communication/Telemetry Subsystem** — 4G/WiFi/LoRa modules for FMIS cloud data exchange.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**Explain the role of the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) in agricultural robot localisation. Why is it preferred over a raw GPS reading?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** fuses data from **multiple noisy sensors** (GNSS, IMU, wheel encoders) to produce an optimal, low-latency estimate of the robot's pose $[x, y, \theta]^T$. It is preferred over raw GPS because:

1. **Mitigates GPS dropouts** under tree canopy or near buildings where satellite signals are blocked.
2. **Reduces noise** — GPS has metre-level jitter; EKF smooths it using IMU + odometry.
3. **Provides orientation $\theta$** — GPS gives only position, not heading; EKF estimates heading via IMU gyroscope.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> Students frequently lose marks in 14-mark robotics questions due to:
> 1. **Forgetting units** in formula substitution (e.g., writing TFC without indicating ha/h). **Always state units.**
> 2. **Skipping the DH table** before writing the A matrices. A 2-mark penalty is typical.
> 3. **Confusing "field capacity" with "field efficiency."** They are *not* the same — capacity has units of area/time, efficiency is dimensionless.
> 4. **Drawing control block diagrams without arrows or labels.** Unsigned arrows receive 0 marks.
> 5. **Writing generic AI/ML jargon** without connecting it to a specific agricultural mechatronic problem (e.g., "deep learning helps" — *unspecific*; "YOLOv5 detects tomato fruit with mAP 0.92" — *specific & creditworthy*).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Path A**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of an autonomous agricultural robot** used for **selective harvesting** of fruits. Label all sensing, control, and actuation blocks clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Theoretical Field Capacity** formula and compute the **Effective Field Capacity** for a spraying robot with implement width $W = 4$ m, forward speed $v = 5$ km/h, and field efficiency $\eta = 0.8$. Comment on the practical significance. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to A(a):

**Block Diagram (refer to SECTION 4.1 Mermaid above for the official architecture).** In written exam, the student should draw a labelled block diagram with the following functional layers:

- **Input Layer:** GPS, IMU, Camera, LiDAR.
- **Processing Layer:** EKF (for pose) + CNN (for vision).
- **Decision Layer:** Path planner + Task scheduler.
- **Output Layer:** Wheel motors, arm joints, sprayer nozzle.
- **Feedback:** Wheel encoders, force/torque sensor on gripper.

**Explanation (write 3–4 lines per block):**
1. The **GNSS + IMU** provide real-time pose.
2. The **vision system** detects ripe fruit using colour and shape features.
3. The **central controller** computes the **inverse kinematics** of the arm to reach the fruit.
4. The **gripper** closes with force feedback to avoid bruising.
5. A **yield map** is updated and pushed to the cloud.

#### Model Solution to A(b):

**Derivation (as in SECTION 3.1):**

$$TFC = \dfrac{W \cdot v}{10} = \dfrac{4 \cdot 5}{10} = 2.0 \text{ ha/h}$$

$$EFC = TFC \cdot \eta = 2.0 \cdot 0.8 = 1.6 \text{ ha/h}$$

**Practical Significance:**
- The robot covers **1.6 hectares per hour** of real field work.
- The 20% time loss ($\eta = 0.8$ corresponds to 20% idle) is due to turning at row ends, refilling the spray tank, and obstacle avoidance manoeuvres.
- In an 8-hour shift, the robot covers **12.8 ha/day** — comparable to a team of 4–5 manual sprayers.
- This quantitative justification is often required in KTU 14-mark answers to demonstrate **engineering feasibility**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Path B (Internal Choice)**

**(a)** Explain the **role of sensor fusion** in an agricultural robot. Compare the use of **RTK-GPS + IMU** versus **Camera + LiDAR** for autonomous navigation in open fields and orchards, respectively. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a **2-DOF planar robotic arm** used in a fruit-picking application, set up the **DH parameter table** and derive the **forward kinematics transformation matrix** $T_2^0$. Hence, find the position of the end-effector when $\theta_1 = 30^\circ$, $\theta_2 = 45^\circ$, $a_2 = 0.5$ m, $d_1 = 0.3$ m. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to B(a):

**Sensor Fusion Definition:** The intelligent combination of data from multiple sensors to achieve **better accuracy, robustness, and reliability** than any single sensor can provide.

**Open Fields — RTK-GPS + IMU:**
- RTK-GPS provides **±2 cm absolute position**; IMU provides **high-rate orientation** (~200 Hz).
- Best for **straight-row navigation**, controlled traffic farming, and open-field tractors.
- Limitation: GPS fails under dense canopy → mitigated by IMU dead-reckoning.

**Orchards — Camera + LiDAR:**
- Camera provides **semantic understanding** (tree trunk vs. fruit vs. sky).
- LiDAR provides **3D geometric map** of trunks and branches even at night.
- Best for **structured but visually complex** environments like apple/orange orchards.
- Limitation: High computational cost; needs GPU-based edge computing.

**Comparison Table (must be in answer):**

| Aspect | RTK-GPS + IMU | Camera + LiDAR |
|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | ±2 cm position | ±1 cm geometry |
| Cost | High (RTK base + rover) | Medium-High (LiDAR unit) |
| Night operation | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (LiDAR) |
| Row following | ✅ Excellent | ✅ Good |
| Obstacle detection | ⚠ Limited | ✅ Excellent |
| Best for | Open grain fields | Orchards / vineyards |

#### Model Solution to B(b):

**DH Parameter Table** (as in SECTION 3.2):

| Joint $i$ | $a_i$ (m) | $\alpha_i$ | $d_i$ (m) | $\theta_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | $90^\circ$ | 0.3 | $30^\circ$ |
| 2 | 0.5 | $0^\circ$ | 0 | $45^\circ$ |

**Transformation matrices:**

$$A_1 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos 30^\circ & 0 & \sin 30^\circ & 0 \\ \sin 30^\circ & 0 & -\cos 30^\circ & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & 0.3 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.866 & 0 & 0.5 & 0 \\ 0.5 & 0 & -0.866 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & 0.3 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$A_2 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos 45^\circ & -\sin 45^\circ & 0 & 0.5 \cos 45^\circ \\ \sin 45^\circ & \cos 45^\circ & 0 & 0.5 \sin 45^\circ \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.707 & -0.707 & 0 & 0.354 \\ 0.707 & 0.707 & 0 & 0.354 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Final end-effector position** (using the analytical shortcut derived in SECTION 3.2):

$$x_e = a_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) = 0.5 \cdot \cos(75^\circ) = 0.5 \cdot 0.2588 = 0.129 \text{ m}$$

$$y_e = a_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) = 0.5 \cdot \sin(75^\circ) = 0.5 \cdot 0.9659 = 0.483 \text{ m}$$

$$z_e = d_1 = 0.3 \text{ m}$$

**Therefore, the end-effector is at $(0.129, 0.483, 0.300)$ m** relative to the base.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Agricultural Robotics is the *mechatronic integration* of sensors, controllers, and actuators for autonomous farming.
- **Core Formula:** $TFC = \dfrac{W \cdot v}{10}$ (units: ha/h with W in m, v in km/h); $EFC = TFC \cdot \eta$.
- **Sensor Hierarchy:** RTK-GPS is the gold standard for *open-field* localisation (±2 cm); LiDAR dominates *orchard* 3D mapping.
- **DH Kinematics:** Always draw the **joint axes**, fill the **DH table** ($a_i, \alpha_i, d_i, \theta_i$), then build $A_i$ matrices.
- **Sensor Fusion:** Never rely on a single sensor — EKF/GPS+IMU is the textbook answer for redundancy.
- **End-Effector Force Control:** Critical for fruit harvesting to prevent bruising → use force/torque sensors with compliance control.
- **Boustrophedon Path:** The default coverage path planning strategy for row-crop robots.
- **Vision Metrics:** Always quote **mAP (mean Average Precision)** and **IoU (Intersection over Union)** when justifying an ML model.
- **Efficiency vs Capacity:** Efficiency is *dimensionless* (0–1); Capacity has units of *area/time*. Do not interchange.
- **Communication:** 4G/5G for cloud FMIS; LoRa for low-power long-range telemetry in rural areas.
- **Sustainability Angle:** Spot spraying reduces herbicide use by up to **77%** — a powerful KTU conclusion line.
- **Future Trend:** Swarm robotics (multiple small bots coordinating) is the *next frontier* in agricultural automation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Agricultural Robotics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT864 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Agricultural Robotics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Agricultural Robotics (Agri-Robotics)** is an interdisciplinary sub-field of mechatronics and autonomous systems engineering that integrates **mechanical engineering, electronics, computer science, control theory, and artificial intelligence** to design, develop, and deploy robotic systems capable of performing agricultural operations such as sowing, spraying, weeding, harvesting, monitoring, sorting, and livestock management with minimal or zero human intervention.

As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEAGT864 – Mechatronics)** syllabus framing, an agricultural robot is formally defined as a **cyber-physical system (CPS)** that:

- **Perceives** the agricultural environment using multi-modal sensors (vision, LiDAR, GNSS, IMU, soil probes).
- **Reasons** using on-board embedded intelligence (microcontrollers, SoCs, edge AI accelerators).
- **Acts** through mechatronic actuators (DC/BLDC motors, hydraulic cylinders, pneumatic grippers, soft end-effectors).
- **Communicates** via IoT/WSN/M2M protocols to a supervisory cloud or farm-management information system (FMIS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 – Module 2 Working Definition:**
> *"An agricultural robot is a mechatronic platform, mobile or stationary, equipped with sensors, actuators, and an intelligent controller, that executes one or more farm operations autonomously in semi-structured or unstructured biological environments (soil, crop, canopy, livestock)."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an agricultural robot as a **"smart farm labourer that never sleeps, never gets bored, and never calls in sick."** 

Imagine a traditional Indian farmer (let us visualise a Kerala rubber or paddy farmer) walking through his field. He uses his **eyes** to see weeds, his **hands** to pick them, his **legs** to walk row by row, and his **brain** to decide what is a weed and what is a crop. Now, replace each of these faculties with a mechatronic counterpart:

| Human Faculty | Mechatronic Counterpart in Agri-Robot |
|---|---|
| Eyes | RGB / Multispectral / Hyperspectral Cameras, LiDAR |
| Skin (touch) | Tactile sensors, Force/Torque sensors |
| Inner ear (balance) | IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit) |
| Brain | Microcontroller / Embedded AI processor |
| Hands | Robotic arm with gripper / end-effector |
| Legs | Wheeled or tracked mobile platform |

The robot then **navigates the field autonomously**, identifies plants or fruits, and performs a precise action — much like a human, but with **millimetre-level repeatability** and **24×7 endurance**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mechatronics is Central to Agri-Robotics:**
> Agriculture is inherently a **multi-domain engineering problem** — it couples mechanical strength (pulling a plough), electronics (sensing moisture), control (steering along a row), and computing (deciding a fruit's ripeness). Mechatronics is the **only discipline** that natively fuses all four.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following **quantitative benchmarks** are used in agri-robotics literature and the KTU evaluation pattern:

- **Standard GNSS accuracy for row following:** **±2.5 cm** (RTK-GNSS)
- **Typical operating speed of a field robot:** **1.6 to 6 km/h** (0.44 to 1.67 m/s)
- **Standard field efficiency (E_f) of conventional tractors:** **70 – 90%**
- **Hyperspectral wavebands used in crop health:** **400 – 2500 nm**
- **LiDAR angular resolution (modern agri-grade):** **0.1° – 0.5°**
- **Standard row spacing (Kerala paddy):** **20 cm**; **(Wheat):** **22.5 cm**
- **Crop canopy penetration depth for RGB-D cameras:** **0.5 – 1.5 m**
- **ISA-95 / ISO 18497 compliance** for autonomous agricultural machinery

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always quote units with numerical values. Writing *"speed = 1.6"* will be penalised; the correct form is *"speed = **1.6 km/h** or **0.44 m/s**."*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of an Agricultural Robotic System

An agricultural robot, in mechatronic terms, is decomposed into **five tightly coupled sub-systems**. Each maps to a classical mechatronic block:

### (A) The Mechanical Sub-system (Skeleton & Muscles)
- **Mobile base** – wheeled (4-WD skid-steer, Ackermann), tracked, legged, or aerial (quadrotor UAV).
- **Manipulator arm** – typically **4 to 6 DOF** (Degrees of Freedom). For fruit picking, 6-DOF is the industry standard.
- **End-effector** – gripper (parallel jaw, suction cup, soft pneumatic), sprayer nozzle, or cutting blade.
- **Structural frame** – aluminium extrusions, carbon-fibre composites, or stainless steel (for corrosive environments like fertiliser spray).

### (B) The Sensor Sub-system (Senses)
