- **Exteroceptive sensors:** GNSS/GPS, LiDAR, RADAR, RGB cameras, multispectral & hyperspectral cameras, ultrasonic range finders.
- **Proprioceptive sensors:** Wheel encoders, IMU (accelerometer + gyroscope + magnetometer), joint encoders, current sensors.
- **Physio-chemical sensors:** Soil moisture probes, NPK sensors, pH sensors, leaf chlorophyll fluorescence sensors.

### (C) The Actuator Sub-system (Muscles)
- **BLDC motors** for wheel drives (high torque density, sealed against dust).
- **Servo / Stepper motors** for joint articulation.
- **Solenoid valves** for pneumatic/hydraulic end-effectors.
- **Peristaltic / diaphragm pumps** for precision spraying (flow rates from **0.1 to 5 L/min**).

### (D) The Controller Sub-system (Brain)
- **Low-level real-time controller** (Arduino, STM32, PLC) – runs at **1 kHz** for motor control loops.
- **Mid-level processor** (NVIDIA Jetson, Raspberry Pi, Snapdragon) – runs perception algorithms at **10–30 Hz**.
- **High-level decision engine** (Edge AI accelerator, e.g., Google Coral TPU) – runs deep-learning inference.

### (E) The Communication & Power Sub-system (Nervous System & Energy)
- **Wireless:** Wi-Fi, 4G/5G, LoRaWAN, Zigbee for farm-wide telemetry.
- **Wired:** CAN bus, RS-485, Ethernet for intra-robot communication.
- **Power:** Li-ion / LiFePO₄ battery packs (**24 V, 48 V, 96 V**); optional solar hybrid; fuel-cell prototypes in research.

## 2.2 Why Each Step Matters — The "Why & How"

1. **Why a mobile base?** Because crops are spatially distributed across hectares; the robot must *go to the crop*, not bring the crop to the robot.
2. **Why multi-modal sensors?** A single sensor is fragile in agriculture — dust fogs cameras, canopy occludes GNSS, so redundancy is *engineered in*, not optional.
3. **Why edge AI?** Farm fields often have **no stable internet**; on-board inference (edge computing) is mandatory for real-time response.
4. **Why 6-DOF arms?** A fruit in 3D space has 6 degrees of freedom; the arm must match this to grasp from any orientation (forward + inverse kinematics).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The table below is the ONLY reference you need for numerical problems in Module 2 Agri-Robotics.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Theoretical Field Capacity | $C_T = \dfrac{W \cdot V}{10}$ | ha/h | $W$ in m, $V$ in km/h |
| 2 | Effective Field Capacity | $C_E = C_T \cdot E_f$ | ha/h | $E_f$ is decimal (e.g. 0.85) |
| 3 | Field Efficiency | $E_f = \dfrac{T_p}{T_p + T_l} \times 100$ | % | $T_p$ = productive time, $T_l$ = loss time |
| 4 | Number of Passes | $N = \left\lceil \dfrac{W_f}{W} \right\rceil$ | – | $W_f$ = field width, $W$ = implement width |
| 5 | Time per Pass | $T_p = \dfrac{L}{V}$ | s or h | $L$ = pass length, $V$ = velocity |
| 6 | Total Field Time | $T_{tot} = N \cdot T_p + T_{turn}(N-1)$ | h | Includes turning at headlands |
| 7 | Forward Kinematics (2-D) | $x = x_0 + \sum_{i} L_i \cos\theta_i$ | m | Standard DH-chain |
| 8 | Workspace Area (planar) | $A_{ws} = \pi \cdot R^2$ | m² | For a holonomic disk |
| 9 | Coverage Rate (Robotic) | $\dot{A} = W \cdot v$ | m²/s | Equivalent to $C_T$ in SI |
| 10 | Spray Application Rate | $Q = \dfrac{q \cdot v \cdot w}{600}$ | L/min | $q$ in L/ha, $v$ in km/h, $w$ in m |
| 11 | Battery Endurance | $T_b = \dfrac{E_{bat}}{P_{avg}}$ | h | $E$ in Wh, $P$ in W |
| 12 | Turning Radius (Ackermann) | $R = \dfrac{L_{wb}}{\tan\delta}$ | m | $L_{wb}$ = wheelbase, $\delta$ = steer angle |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In Formula 1, the divisor is **10** (not 10000). This is because $1 \text{ ha} = 10000 \text{ m}^2$ and $1 \text{ km/h} = 1000/3600 \text{ m/s}$, so the conversion $W \cdot V$ in m·(km/h) divided by 10 directly gives ha/h. Memorise this!

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Precision Agriculture:** Robots apply fertiliser/pesticide *only* where needed, cutting chemical use by up to **80%**.
- **Labour Shortage Mitigation:** Critical in ageing-farmer demographies (e.g., Japan's average farmer age = **67 years**).
- **24/7 Operations:** Harvesting strawberries at night preserves sugar content.
- **Data-Driven Yield Optimisation:** Robots generate georeferenced crop data, feeding digital twins of the farm.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **exhaustive, line-by-line derivations** of the most important agri-robotics formulas from the KTU Module 2 syllabus. Every algebraic transition is written out, with a textual justification of the physics/mathematics behind it.

---

## Derivation 1: Theoretical Field Capacity ($C_T$) and Effective Field Capacity ($C_E$)

### Problem Context
A precision spraying robot of **working width $W$ = 6 m** moves along a straight pass at a **constant ground speed $V$ = 5 km/h**. Compute the theoretical field capacity, and then the effective field capacity given an **assumed field efficiency $E_f$ = 0.80** (80%).

### Step 1 — Establish the definition of Capacity
The *theoretical field capacity* $C_T$ is defined as the **area a machine would cover per unit time if it operated continuously at its full working width, without any idle, overlap, or turning time**.

$$
C_T = \frac{\text{Area covered per unit time}}{}
$$

### Step 2 — Express Area Covered per Unit Time
In a small time interval $dt$, the robot advances a distance $V \, dt$ along the row, and the working width $W$ is constant. Hence the elemental area swept is:

$$
dA = W \cdot V \, dt
$$

### Step 3 — Form the ratio $\dfrac{dA}{dt}$
Dividing both sides by $dt$ gives the instantaneous coverage rate:

$$
\frac{dA}{dt} = W \cdot V
$$

Here, $W$ must be in **metres (m)** and $V$ in **metres per second (m/s)** to obtain $dA/dt$ in m²/s. However, in agricultural engineering, it is conventional to express the result in **hectares per hour (ha/h)**.

### Step 4 — Unit Conversion to ha/h
We use:
- $1 \text{ ha} = 10{,}000 \text{ m}^2$
- $1 \text{ h} = 3600 \text{ s}$
- $1 \text{ km/h} = \dfrac{1000}{3600} \text{ m/s}$

Substitute $V_{\text{(m/s)}} = V_{\text{(km/h)}} \cdot \dfrac{1000}{3600} = V_{\text{(km/h)}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{3.6}$ into $dA/dt$:

$$
C_T \big|_{\text{m}^2/\text{s}} = W \cdot \frac{V_{\text{km/h}}}{3.6}
$$

Now convert m²/s → ha/h:

$$
C_T \big|_{\text{ha/h}} = \frac{W \cdot \frac{V_{\text{km/h}}}{3.6}}{10{,}000} \cdot 3600
$$

### Step 5 — Simplify the constant factor

$$
\frac{3600}{3.6 \times 10{,}000} = \frac{3600}{36{,}000} = \frac{1}{10}
$$

Therefore:

$$
C_T = \frac{W \cdot V}{10} \quad \text{(ha/h)}
$$

### Step 6 — Numerical Evaluation

Given $W = 6$ m and $V = 5$ km/h:

$$
C_T = \frac{6 \times 5}{10} = \frac{30}{10} = 3 \text{ ha/h}
$$

### Step 7 — Effective Field Capacity

$$
C_E = C_T \cdot E_f = 3 \times 0.80 = 2.4 \text{ ha/h}
$$

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{C_T = 3 \text{ ha/h}, \qquad C_E = 2.4 \text{ ha/h}}
$$

---

## Derivation 2: Total Time to Cover a Rectangular Field (including turning)

### Problem Context
A weeding robot must cover a rectangular field of **length $L_f$ = 400 m** and **width $W_f$ = 120 m**. Its working width is $W$ = 4 m, ground speed $V$ = 2 m/s, and at each headland it executes a U-turn that consumes $T_{turn}$ = 15 s. Compute the **total time to complete the operation**.

### Step 1 — Number of Passes
The number of parallel passes required is:

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{W_f}{W} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{120}{4} \right\rceil = \lceil 30 \rceil = 30 \text{ passes}
$$

### Step 2 — Time for a Single Productive Pass
$$
T_p = \frac{L_f}{V} = \frac{400}{2} = 200 \text{ s}
$$

### Step 3 — Number of Turns
Between 30 passes, there are $N - 1 = 29$ headland turns (the last pass does not require a turn at the end because the field is fully covered).

$$
\text{Total turn time} = (N - 1) \cdot T_{turn} = 29 \times 15 = 435 \text{ s}
$$

### Step 4 — Total Time

$$
T_{tot} = N \cdot T_p + (N - 1) \cdot T_{turn}
$$

$$
T_{tot} = 30 \times 200 + 29 \times 15
$$

$$
T_{tot} = 6000 + 435 = 6435 \text{ s}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to Hours

$$
T_{tot} = \frac{6435}{3600} \approx 1.7875 \text{ h} \approx 1 \text{ h } 47 \text{ min } 15 \text{ s}
$$

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{T_{tot} \approx 1.79 \text{ hours}}
$$

---

## Derivation 3: Spray Application Rate

### Problem Context
A precision sprayer must deliver $q$ = **200 L/ha** at a forward speed $V$ = **8 km/h** with a boom width $w$ = **12 m**. Compute the required **per-nozzle flow rate** if the boom has 24 equal nozzles.

### Step 1 — Standard Formula

$$
Q = \frac{q \cdot V \cdot w}{600}
$$

where $Q$ is total boom flow in L/min, and the constant 600 is the combined unit-conversion factor.

### Step 2 — Substitute values

$$
Q = \frac{200 \times 8 \times 12}{600} = \frac{19{,}200}{600} = 32 \text{ L/min}
$$

### Step 3 — Per-Nozzle Flow

$$
Q_{nozzle} = \frac{Q}{N_{nozzles}} = \frac{32}{24} = 1.333 \text{ L/min} \approx 1.33 \text{ L/min}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to mL/s

$$
1.333 \text{ L/min} = \frac{1.333 \times 1000}{60} \text{ mL/s} \approx 22.22 \text{ mL/s}
$$

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{Q_{nozzle} \approx 1.33 \text{ L/min} \approx 22.22 \text{ mL/s}}
$$

---

## Analytical Matrix: Sensor–Task Mapping in Agri-Robotics

| Agricultural Task | Primary Sensor | Secondary Sensor | Actuator Used | Typical AI/Algorithm |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Row following | GNSS-RTK | IMU, Wheel Encoder | Steer-by-wire motor | PID / Stanley controller |
| Weed detection | Multispectral camera | RGB camera | Sprayer / Laser | CNN (e.g., YOLOv8) |
| Fruit picking | RGB-D camera | Tactile sensor | 6-DOF arm + gripper | Pose estimation + IK |
| Soil moisture mapping | Capacitive probe | Thermal camera | – | Kriging interpolation |
| Autonomous mowing | LiDAR | Ultrasonic | BLDC cutter motor | Random walk / Boustrophedon |
| Livestock health | Thermal IR | Acoustic mic | – | Anomaly detection |
| Drone spraying | LiDAR + GNSS | Barometer | Centrifugal nozzle | Coverage path planning |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

This section presents **two Mermaid diagrams** that visualise the architecture of an agricultural robotic system. Per the protocol, complex physical drawings have been adapted as **block-level functional architecture flows**.

---

## Diagram 1: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic Agricultural Robot

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Power Source Li-ion Battery Pack 48V] --> B[Power Management and DC-DC Converters]
    B --> C[Low Level Real Time Controller STM32 PLC]
    B --> D[Mid Level Processor NVIDIA Jetson]
    B --> E[Edge AI Accelerator Google Coral TPU]
    C --> F[Motor Drivers BLDC H-Bridge]
    F --> G[Wheel Actuators 4WD Drive]
    F --> H[Steering Actuator Ackermann]
    D --> I[Perception Pipeline]
    I --> J[GNSS RTK Receiver]
    I --> K[IMU 9 Axis]
    I --> L[LiDAR 2D 360 Degree]
    I --> M[RGB-D and Multispectral Camera]
    I --> N[Ultrasonic and Wheel Encoders]
    E --> O[Decision Engine Path Planning Task Scheduling]
    D --> P[Manipulator Controller 6 DOF Arm]
    P --> Q[End Effector Gripper Sprayer Cutter]
    O --> R[Communication Module 4G 5G LoRa CAN Bus]
    R --> S[Cloud FMIS Farm Management Information System]
    M --> I
    L --> I
    N --> I
    K --> I
    J --> I
```

**Reading the diagram:** The arrows show **power and data flow**. The left side is the *actuation chain*; the right side is the *sensing and intelligence chain*; both meet at the central processor. The cloud connection (S) represents the **Cyber-Physical-Cloud integration** central to modern precision agriculture.

---

## Diagram 2: Sense–Plan–Act Loop in an Agri-Robot (Closed-Loop Control)

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[Environment Crop Field Soil] --> S2[Sensor Suite GNSS LiDAR Camera IMU]
    S2 --> S3[Signal Conditioning and Filtering]
    S3 --> S4[Perception Module Feature Extraction Object Detection]
    S4 --> S5[Localization and Mapping SLAM GPS Fusion]
    S5 --> S6[Path Planner Boustrophedon A Star RRT]
    S6 --> S7[Task Scheduler Mission FSM]
    S7 --> S8[Motion Controller PID Stanley Pure Pursuit]
    S8 --> S9[Actuator Commands Motor PWM Servo PWM]
    S9 --> S10[Physical Action Move Spray Pick Weed]
    S10 --> S1
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is a **closed-loop Sense → Plan → Act** cycle. Every action of the robot changes the environment, which is re-sensed, and the cycle repeats at the controller's sample rate (typically **10–100 Hz** for the motion loop).

---

## Diagram 3: Classification Tree of Agricultural Robots

```mermaid
graph TD
    root[Agricultural Robots]
    root --> aero[Aerial UAV Drones]
    root --> ground[Ground UGV]
    root --> manip[Stationary Manipulators]
    aero --> a1[Spraying Drones]
    aero --> a2[Monitoring and Mapping Drones]
    aero --> a3[Pollination Drones Research]
    ground --> g1[Autonomous Tractors]
    ground --> g2[Robotic Weeders]
    ground --> g3[Harvesting Robots]
    ground --> g4[Seeding and Planting Robots]
    ground --> g5[Livestock Robots Milking Grazing]
    manip --> m1[Greenhouse Manipulators]
    manip --> m2[Sorting and Grading Arms]
    manip --> m3[Post Harvest Processing Robots]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

This section is structured exactly per the KTU 2024 Scheme **End Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern for a 14-mark Module question with internal choice. It contains **Part A** (3-mark short answers) and **Part B** (14-mark long answers with two alternative choices).

---

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define Mechatronics. List any **four** major subsystems of an agricultural robot.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Mechatronics** is the synergistic integration of mechanical engineering, electronics, computer science, control engineering, and information technology to design and manufacture smart products and processes.

The four major subsystems of an agricultural robot are:

1. **Mechanical Sub-system** – Mobile base, manipulator arm, end-effector, frame.
2. **Sensor Sub-system** – GNSS, LiDAR, camera, IMU, encoders, soil sensors.
3. **Actuator Sub-system** – BLDC motors, servo motors, hydraulic cylinders, pneumatic pumps.
4. **Controller Sub-system** – Microcontroller, edge AI processor, PLC, with associated software (ROS, embedded C, Python).

*(Award 1 mark for the definition; 0.5 mark × 4 = 2 marks for subsystems. Total 3 marks.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] What is the significance of **RTK-GNSS** in agricultural robotics? Mention its typical accuracy.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**RTK (Real-Time Kinematic) GNSS** is a high-precision satellite positioning technique that uses a **base station** and a **rover** to correct satellite signal errors in real time, achieving **centimetre-level positioning accuracy** (typically **±2 cm to ±2.5 cm** horizontally).

**Significance in agricultural robotics:**
1. Enables **row-following** with cm-level accuracy, essential for inter-row weeding and precision spraying.
2. Supports **autonomous navigation** without reliance on buried guide wires.
3. Provides **georeferencing** for digital farm maps and variable-rate technology.
4. Allows **repeatable path planning** across multiple operations (sowing, spraying, harvesting).

*(1 mark for definition; 1 mark for accuracy value; 1 mark for any 2 significance points. Total 3 marks.)*

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **ESE Pattern Note:** In KTU 2024, Module 2 carries a 14-mark question with internal choice. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

#### (a) With a neat block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of an agricultural robot**, explaining the role of each block. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

An agricultural robot can be decomposed into the following functional blocks (refer to Diagram 1 in Section 4):

**(1) Power Block (1 mark):** Typically a **Li-ion / LiFePO₄ battery pack** (24 V, 48 V, or 96 V) feeding DC-DC converters that step down to logic-level voltages (5 V, 3.3 V) for the controller and sensors.

**(2) Sensor Block (2 marks):**
- *Exteroceptive* sensors perceive the external world — GNSS-RTK for global position, LiDAR for obstacle/terrain mapping, RGB-D cameras for object detection, multispectral cameras for crop-health assessment.
- *Proprioceptive* sensors measure the robot's internal state — IMU (roll, pitch, yaw), wheel encoders (odometry), joint encoders (arm pose), current sensors (motor load).

**(3) Controller / Processing Block (2 marks):**
- *Low-level real-time controller* (STM32, PLC) runs motor control loops at **1 kHz** with deterministic latency.
- *Mid-level processor* (Jetson, Raspberry Pi) runs perception pipelines at **10–30 Hz** using ROS (Robot Operating System).
- *High-level AI engine* (Coral TPU, Intel OpenVINO) executes deep-learning inference for plant classification, fruit detection, and decision making.

**(4) Actuator Block (1 mark):** BLDC motors for wheel drives (sealed against dust and water — IP65 rating), servo motors for manipulator joints, pneumatic/hydraulic actuators for high-force tasks (e.g., tree-shaking for fruit harvesting).

**(5) Communication & Cloud Block (1 mark):** CAN bus for intra-robot communication, 4G/5G/LoRa for telemetry to a **Farm Management Information System (FMIS)**, enabling remote monitoring, fleet coordination, and over-the-air updates.

*(Marks distribution: block diagram 2 marks, role explanation 5 marks. Total 7 marks.)*

---

#### (b) A robotic weeder operates with working width $W$ = 3 m at a forward speed $V$ = 4 km/h in a field of length 500 m and width 90 m. Each headland turn takes 12 s. Compute: (i) theoretical field capacity, (ii) effective field capacity assuming $E_f$ = 0.78, (iii) total time to cover the field. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Theoretical Field Capacity (2 marks):**

$$
C_T = \frac{W \cdot V}{10} = \frac{3 \times 4}{10} = 1.2 \text{ ha/h}
$$

**(ii) Effective Field Capacity (2 marks):**

$$
C_E = C_T \cdot E_f = 1.2 \times 0.78 = 0.936 \text{ ha/h}
$$

**(iii) Total Time to Cover the Field (3 marks):**

Number of passes:

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{W_f}{W} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{90}{3} \right\rceil = 30 \text{ passes}
$$

Time per productive pass:

$$
T_p = \frac{L_f}{V} = \frac{500 \text{ m}}{4 \text{ km/h}} = \frac{500}{4000/3600} = \frac{500 \times 3600}{4000} = 450 \text{ s}
$$

Number of turns (between 30 passes, there are 29 turns):

$$
T_{turns} = 29 \times 12 = 348 \text{ s}
$$

Total time:

$$
T_{tot} = N \cdot T_p + T_{turns} = 30 \times 450 + 348 = 13{,}500 + 348 = 13{,}848 \text{ s}
$$

Converting to hours:

$$
T_{tot} = \frac{13{,}848}{3600} \approx 3.847 \text{ h} \approx 3 \text{ h } 50 \text{ min } 48 \text{ s}
$$

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] — *ALTERNATIVE TO Question A*

#### (a) Discuss the **classification of agricultural robots** with examples, and explain the **sensors used in each category**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Agricultural robots are classified into **three broad categories** based on mobility and operating environment:

**(1) Aerial Robots / UAVs (Drones) (2 marks):**
- *Examples:* Multirotor spraying drones (e.g., DJI Agras T40), fixed-wing mapping drones (e.g., senseFly eBee).
- *Sensors:* GNSS (primary), barometer (altitude), downward multispectral/RGB camera, occasionally LiDAR.
- *Tasks:* Crop health mapping, variable-rate spraying, livestock counting, pesticide dispersion.

**(2) Ground Mobile Robots / UGVs (3 marks):**
- *Examples:* Autonomous tractors (John Deere 8R), robotic weeders (Naio Technologies Oz, Ecorobotix ARA), strawberry harvesters (Agrobot E-Series), seeding robots (FarmDroid FD20).
- *Sensors:* GNSS-RTK + IMU + wheel encoders (for navigation), LiDAR and ultrasonic (for obstacle avoidance), RGB-D cameras (for crop/weed detection), multispectral cameras (for selective spraying).
- *Tasks:* Mechanical weeding, precision spraying, autonomous seeding, selective harvesting, soil sampling.

**(3) Stationary Manipulators (2 marks):**
- *Examples:* Greenhouse tomato-picking arms, post-harvest sorting robots, automated milking systems (Lely Astronaut).
- *Sensors:* Force/torque sensors (for grip control), RGB-D cameras (for fruit localization), capacitive sensors (for milk quality).
- *Tasks:* Greenhouse picking, sorting/grading by size/colour/ripeness, robotic milking, packaging.

**Justification of sensor choice:** Aerial robots use lightweight, low-power sensors (since payload is limited). Ground robots can carry heavier multi-modal sensor stacks. Manipulators use *tactile* and *force* sensors because they perform physical contact tasks.

---

#### (b) An autonomous tractor ploughs a field of 5 hectares. Its working width is 2.5 m and forward speed is 6 km/h. If the headland turn time per pass is 20 s and field efficiency is 75%, determine: (i) the theoretical field capacity, (ii) the number of passes required assuming field length is 250 m, (iii) the **effective time** to complete the operation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Theoretical Field Capacity (2 marks):**

$$
C_T = \frac{W \cdot V}{10} = \frac{2.5 \times 6}{10} = 1.5 \text{ ha/h}
$$

**(ii) Number of Passes (2 marks):**

Field area $A = 5$ ha $= 50{,}000 \text{ m}^2$. The width of one pass is $W = 2.5$ m. Total width covered by $N$ passes is $N \cdot W$. For the field, $W_f = \dfrac{A}{L_f} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{250} = 200 \text{ m}$.

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{W_f}{W} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{200}{2.5} \right\rceil = 80 \text{ passes}
$$

**(iii) Effective Time (3 marks):**

Time per productive pass:

$$
T_p = \frac{L_f}{V} = \frac{250 \text{ m}}{6 \text{ km/h}} = \frac{250 \times 3600}{6000} = 150 \text{ s}
$$

Total productive time for 80 passes:

$$
T_{prod} = 80 \times 150 = 12{,}000 \text{ s}
$$

Total turn time for 79 turns:

$$
T_{turns} = 79 \times 20 = 1580 \text{ s}
$$

Total clock time:

$$
T_{clock} = T_{prod} + T_{turns} = 12{,}000 + 1580 = 13{,}580 \text{ s} \approx 3.772 \text{ h}
$$

The **effective time** (using field efficiency as a check) is given by:

$$
T_{eff} = \frac{A}{C_E} = \frac{5 \text{ ha}}{1.5 \times 0.75} = \frac{5}{1.125} \approx 4.444 \text{ h}
$$

> **Note on the apparent discrepancy:** The $T_{clock}$ computed by discrete-pass summation equals the effective time **only if** the loss time is *exactly* the turn time. Here, an additional 4.444 − 3.772 = 0.672 h of unaccounted loss exists (e.g., refuelling, overlapping, operator breaks). For the KTU exam, the **direct clock-time answer (3.77 h) is preferred** when individual pass/turn times are given.

$$
\boxed{T_{clock} \approx 3.77 \text{ h}, \quad T_{eff} \approx 4.44 \text{ h}}
$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in Module 2 Agri-Robotics questions:**
> 1. **Unit-conversion mistakes** — Forgetting the divisor of **10** in $C_T = \dfrac{W \cdot V}{10}$. This is the single most common error. *Always* check whether $W$ is in m and $V$ is in km/h before applying the formula.
> 2. **Number of turns = (N − 1), not N.** The last pass does **not** require a turn at the end of the field. Students who write $N \cdot T_{turn}$ lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Misclassification of sensors** — Conflating LiDAR (active optical) with RADAR (radio waves). Examiners will deduct 0.5 mark per such error.
> 4. **Omitting units** — Numerical answers without units (e.g., "$C_T = 1.2$" instead of "$C_T = 1.2$ **ha/h**") attract a 0.5 mark penalty per KTU 2024 scheme.
> 5. **Confusing theoretical vs. effective capacity** — $C_T$ ignores all losses; $C_E$ includes them. State explicitly which you are computing.
> 6. **Spelling "Boustrophedon" wrongly** — Yes, examiners notice. It means "ox-turning" (Greek). Spell it as: **B-O-U-S-T-R-O-P-H-E-D-O-N**.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Definition:** Agri-Robotics = Mechatronics + Agriculture; an autonomous CPS for farm operations.
- ✅ **Five Sub-systems:** Mechanical, Sensor, Actuator, Controller, Communication/Power.
- ✅ **Three Categories:** Aerial (UAV), Ground (UGV), Stationary Manipulator.
- ✅ **Key Sensors:** GNSS-RTK (±2.5 cm), IMU, LiDAR, RGB-D camera, Multispectral camera, encoders.
- ✅ **Key Actuators:** BLDC motor (wheels), Servo motor (joints), Pneumatic (grippers), Hydraulic (high force).
- ✅ **Three Formulas to Memorise Cold:**
  - $C_T = \dfrac{W \cdot V}{10}$ (ha/h)
  - $E_f = \dfrac{T_p}{T_p + T_l} \times 100$ (%)
  - $T_{tot} = N \cdot T_p + (N-1) \cdot T_{turn}$ (h)
- ✅ **RTK-GNSS accuracy = ±2.5 cm** (favourite KTU short-answer value).
- ✅ **Field efficiency of conventional tractors = 70–90%**.
- ✅ **Standard sensors for crop health: 400–2500 nm (multispectral/hyperspectral).**
- ✅ **ROS = Robot Operating System** is the de-facto software framework.
- ✅ **Sense–Plan–Act** is the canonical control loop.
- ✅ **Path planning patterns:** Boustrophedon (back-and-forth), Spiral, Coverage patterns.
- ✅ **Slip in wheels = error in odometry** ⇒ use sensor fusion (IMU + GNSS) for accurate localization.
- ✅ **IP65/IP67** = ingress protection ratings needed for agri-robots (dust + water jet).
- ✅ **End-effector choice** depends on crop: suction for apples, gripper for cucumbers, blade for weeds.
- ✅ **KTU exam mantra:** *Show units. Show conversions. Show every step. Write legibly.*

---

*End of Notes — Introduction to Agricultural Robotics, Module 2, PEAGT864 Mechatronics, KTU 2024 Scheme.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Agricultural Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT864 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Agricultural Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Agricultural Robots (Agri-bots / Agribots)** are mechatronic systems that integrate mechanical structures, sensors, actuators, embedded controllers, and intelligent algorithms to perform farming operations with minimal or no direct human intervention. According to the KTU 2024 Mechatronics syllabus (Module 2), agricultural robots are classified based on **operational function** (tillage, sowing, spraying, weeding, harvesting, livestock management) and **mobility platform** (ground-based wheeled/tracked, aerial-UAV, legged, rail-mounted).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** An agricultural robot is a **cyber-physical system (CPS)** whose perception–decision–action loop is closed in real time by onboard sensors and microcontrollers, executing agronomic tasks with **positional accuracy ≤ 2 cm (GNSS-RTK)** and **operational reliability > 90%**.

The taxonomy recognized by KTU for Module 2 evaluation consists of **five primary functional classes**:

| Class | Primary Function | KTU Mapping |
|---|---|---|
| Driverless / Autonomous Tractors | Tillage, haulage, primary traction | PEAGT864-M2-LO1 |
| Aerial Agri-Drones (UAVs) | Spraying, mapping, crop health imaging | PEAGT864-M2-LO2 |
| Robotic Harvesters & Pickers | Selective fruit/vegetable collection | PEAGT864-M2-LO3 |
| Weeding & Inter-row Robots | Mechanical/laser weed elimination | PEAGT864-M2-LO4 |
| Livestock & Dairy Robots | Milking, feeding, health monitoring | PEAGT864-M2-LO5 |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Farm-as-Factory" Analogy**
> Imagine a modern hospital: doctors (human farmers) diagnose, but surgical robots (agri-robots) execute with precision no human can sustain for 14 hours under the sun. The farm becomes a **production line** — sensors are the "nervous system," GNSS is the "GPS of the body," actuators are the "muscles," and the ECU is the "brain." Where a human hand can pick 100 apples/hour, a robotic arm with computer vision (e.g., FFR — *Fresh Fruit Recognition*) can pick **800–1000 apples/hour** at uniform ripeness, 24/7, without fatigue.

## 1.3 Why Mechatronics Fits Agriculture

Agriculture is the **last major industry** to undergo robotic automation because of its **unstructured environment** (varying soil, weather, light, biological variability). Mechatronics solves this through the **MCSE loop** — **M**echanics + **C**ontrol + **S**ensors + **E**lectronics — coupled with AI-based perception.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Metric for KTU:** Modern precision agriculture targets a **reduction in agrochemical use by 30–60%**, achievable only through **site-specific robot action** rather than broadcast spraying.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Systematic Classification of Agricultural Robots

### A. Driverless / Autonomous Tractors
- **Platform:** Heavy 4WD tractor retrofitted with **RTK-GNSS**, **IMU**, **LIDAR**, and **steer-by-wire actuators**.
- **Function:** Soil preparation, plowing, seeding, fertilizing, haulage.
- **Control architecture:** Stanley / Pure-Pursuit path-tracking with lateral error feedback.
- **Example (industry reference):** John Deere 8R 410 AutoTrac™, CNH Industrial Autonomous Tractor Concept.

### B. Aerial Agricultural Drones (UAVs)
- **Platform:** Multi-rotor (quad/hex/octocopter) or fixed-wing UAVs.
- **Function:** Multispectral/hyperspectral imaging, variable-rate spraying (VRS), seeding broadcast.
- **Sensors:** RGB-D camera, NDVI multispectral sensor, LiDAR, mmWave radar.
- **Example:** DJI Agras T50, XAG V40, Parrot Bluegrass Fields.

### C. Robotic Harvesters & Pickers
- **Platform:** Mobile manipulator with multi-DOF robotic arm (often 6-DOF or 7-DOF).
- **Function:** Selective harvesting of apples, strawberries, tomatoes, grapes, sweet peppers.
- **Key sub-systems:**
  - **Computer vision:** YOLO/CNN-based ripeness detection.
  - **End-effector:** Soft gripper, suction cup, scissor cutter.
- **Example:** Tevel Aerobotics (TIGER), Octinion Rubion, FFRobot.

### D. Weeding & Inter-row Robots
- **Platform:** Small autonomous ground vehicle with RTK + camera row-following.
- **Function:** Mechanical tillage between rows, laser weeding (e.g., Carbon Robotics LaserWeeder), spot-spraying.
- **Power:** Electric (Li-ion) with solar assist.
- **Example:** Naio Technologies Oz, Ecorobotix ARA, FarmDroid FD20.

### E. Livestock / Dairy Robots
- **Platform:** Stationary or mobile units inside barns.
- **Function:** Automated milking (AMS), automatic feed pushers, manure scrapers, health monitoring collars.
- **Example:** Lely Astronaut A5, DeLaval VMS™ V300, GEA DairyRobot R9500.

### F. Seeding / Planting Robots
- **Function:** Precision seed placement at variable depth/density using vacuum-metered singulators.
- **Example:** FarmDroid FD20 (solar-powered, RTK-based sowing + weeding).

### G. Sorting & Grading Robots
- **Function:** Post-harvest conveyor vision systems for size, color, defect classification.
- **Example:** TOMRA Spectrim, Ellips Elisam.

### H. Greenhouse / Phenotyping Robots
- **Function:** Indoor crop monitoring, plant phenotyping (height, biomass, leaf area index).
- **Example:** PhenoBot, PlantEye, Thorvald.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **directly examinable** in KTU ESE Part A/B questions.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Theoretical Field Capacity | $C_T = \dfrac{S \times W}{10}$ | $S$ = speed (km/h), $W$ = width (m), $C_T$ = ha/h | Tractor / weeder productivity |
| 2 | Effective Field Capacity | $C_E = C_T \times \eta_f$ | $\eta_f$ = field efficiency (0.60–0.85) | Real-world throughput |
| 3 | UAV Spray Discharge | $Q = v \times w \times q_a$ | $v$ = flight speed (m/s), $w$ = swath width (m), $q_a$ = application rate (L/m²) | Drone spraying |
| 4 | Battery Endurance (Multirotor) | $T = \dfrac{E_{bat}}{P_{hover} + P_{payload}}$ | $E_{bat}$ = energy (Wh), $P$ = power (W) | Drone mission planning |
| 5 | RTK Positional Accuracy | $\sigma_{RTK} \le 2\,\text{cm} + 1\,\text{ppm}$ | baseline-corrected | Auto-steer tractors |
| 6 | Pure-Pursuit Lookahead | $\delta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{2L\sin\alpha}{l_d}\right)$ | $L$ = wheelbase, $\alpha$ = heading error, $l_d$ = lookahead distance | Path tracking |
| 7 | NDVI (Crop Health) | $\text{NDVI} = \dfrac{\text{NIR} - \text{Red}}{\text{NIR} + \text{Red}}$ | reflectance bands | Drone imaging |
| 8 | Number of Drones Needed | $N = \dfrac{A}{v \times w \times T_{flight} \times \eta_o}$ | $A$ = area (m²), $\eta_o$ = operational efficiency | Fleet sizing |
| 9 | Robotic Arm Reach (Workspace) | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i \ge R_{task}$ | $L_i$ = link length | Harvester arm design |
| 10 | Vacuum Gripper Suction | $F = \Delta P \times A_{cup}$ | $\Delta P$ = pressure diff, $A_{cup}$ = cup area | Soft fruit picking |

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

- **Precision agriculture:** Reduces agrochemical input cost (target: **30–60% saving**).
- **Labor substitution:** Addresses rural labor shortage in Kerala's plantation sector (rubber, coconut, cardamom).
- **Data-driven farming:** Drone-acquired NDVI maps enable **variable rate technology (VRT)**.
- **Sustainability:** Solar-powered agri-bots (e.g., FarmDroid FD20) achieve **carbon-neutral operations**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Effective Field Capacity of an Autonomous Tractor

**Problem Statement:** A driverless tractor operates at forward speed $S = 8 \text{ km/h}$ with an implement of working width $W = 4 \text{ m}$. Field efficiency $\eta_f = 0.80$. Compute (a) Theoretical Field Capacity $C_T$, (b) Effective Field Capacity $C_E$, and (c) the time $t$ required to cover a 12-hectare field.

**Step 1 — Convert speed to consistent units:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= 8 \;\text{km/h} \\[2pt]
&= \dfrac{8 \times 1000}{3600} \;\text{m/s} \\[2pt]
&= 2.222 \;\text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Theoretical Field Capacity $C_T$:**

The standard agronomic relation is $C_T = \dfrac{S \times W}{10}$ when $S$ is in km/h and $W$ in m, yielding ha/h.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_T &= \dfrac{S \times W}{10} \\[2pt]
&= \dfrac{8 \times 4}{10} \\[2pt]
&= \dfrac{32}{10} \\[2pt]
&= 3.2 \;\text{ha/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Field Efficiency to obtain Effective Capacity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_E &= C_T \times \eta_f \\[2pt]
&= 3.2 \times 0.80 \\[2pt]
&= 2.56 \;\text{ha/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Time for 12 hectares:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \dfrac{A}{C_E} = \dfrac{12 \;\text{ha}}{2.56 \;\text{ha/h}} \\[2pt]
&= 4.6875 \;\text{h} \\[2pt]
&\approx 4 \;\text{h}\;41 \;\text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** Field efficiency accounts for **turning time, overlap, headland turns, and refilling**. A 5-mark KTU question typically requires the above 4 steps.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Number of Drones Required for Spraying a Paddy Field

**Problem Statement:** A 50-hectare paddy field must be sprayed by multirotor UAVs with:
- Flight speed $v = 6 \;\text{m/s}$,
- Spray swath width $w = 6 \;\text{m}$,
- Flight endurance $T_{flight} = 12 \;\text{min} = 720 \;\text{s}$,
- Operational efficiency $\eta_o = 0.70$ (includes refill + turnaround).

Compute the **minimum number of drones** $N$ to finish the spray in **one working day (8 h)**.

**Step 1 — Coverage Rate per Drone (m²/s):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= v \times w \\[2pt]
&= 6 \times 6 \\[2pt]
&= 36 \;\text{m}^2/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Effective Coverage per Drone per Day (accounting for endurance + efficiency):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{eff} &= R \times T_{flight} \times \eta_o \\[2pt]
&= 36 \times 720 \times 0.70 \\[2pt]
&= 18144 \;\text{m}^2/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert 50 ha to m²:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 50 \times 10000 = 500000 \;\text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Number of Drones:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \dfrac{A}{R_{eff}} = \dfrac{500000}{18144} \\[2pt]
&= 27.56
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $N$ must be an integer and we must **complete the task**, round **up**:

$$
N = 28 \;\text{drones}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Real fleet planning includes **battery-swap logistics**; each drone typically requires **3–4 battery sets** to avoid idle time.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — RTK Lookahead Steering Angle for Auto-Tractor

**Problem Statement:** A tractor with wheelbase $L = 3 \text{ m}$ follows a curved path with heading error $\alpha = 5°$ and lookahead distance $l_d = 5 \text{ m}$. Find the steering angle $\delta$ using the **Pure Pursuit** law.

**Step 1 — Pure-Pursuit Equation:**

$$
\delta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{2L\sin\alpha}{l_d}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta &= \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{2 \times 3 \times \sin 5°}{5}\right) \\[2pt]
&= \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{6 \times 0.0872}{5}\right) \\[2pt]
&= \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{0.5230}{5}\right) \\[2pt]
&= \arctan(0.1046) \\[2pt]
&= 5.98° \approx 6°
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:** A small heading error (5°) produces a modest corrective steer angle (6°). Pure pursuit is **geometrically stable** but **lags** in tight turns.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Agri-Robot Classes

| Feature | Driverless Tractor | Agri-Drone | Robotic Harvester | Weeding Robot | Dairy Robot |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mobility | Ground (wheeled) | Aerial | Ground + arm | Ground (small) | Stationary/Mobile |
| Primary Sensor | GNSS-RTK + IMU | RGB + Multispectral | RGB-D + Force | RGB + IMU | Thermal + Camera |
| Power Source | Diesel/Electric hybrid | Li-Po Battery | Diesel + Battery | Battery + Solar | Mains AC |
| Working Speed | 6–12 km/h | 5–8 m/s | 0.5–2 km/h | 1–3 km/h | Cow-paced |
| Cost Range (USD) | 200k–500k | 15k–60k | 100k–300k | 30k–150k | 100k–250k |
| Human Replaced | 1 operator | 1–2 scouts | 5–10 pickers | 1–2 weeders | 1 milker |
| KTU Module Mapping | M2-LO1 | M2-LO2 | M2-LO3 | M2-LO4 | M2-LO5 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Agricultural Robots

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AGRICULTURAL ROBOTS] --> B[GROUND BASED]
    A --> C[AERIAL BASED]
    A --> D[STATIONARY]

    B --> B1[Driverless Tractors]
    B --> B2[Robotic Harvesters]
    B --> B3[Weeding Robots]
    B --> B4[Seeding Robots]

    B1 --> B1a[RTK GNSS Auto Steer]
    B1 --> B1b[Path Tracking via Pure Pursuit]

    B2 --> B2a[Apple Picker]
    B2 --> B2b[Strawberry Picker]
    B2 --> B2c[Tomato Harvester]

    B3 --> B3a[Mechanical Tillage]
    B3 --> B3b[Laser Weeding]
    B3 --> B3c[Spot Spraying]

    C --> C1[Multirotor UAV]
    C --> C2[Fixed Wing UAV]
    C1 --> C1a[Spraying Drone]
    C1 --> C1b[NDVI Mapping Drone]
    C1 --> C1c[Seeding Drone]

    D --> D1[Automatic Milking System AMS]
    D --> D2[Feed Pusher Robot]
    D --> D3[Sorting and Grading Conveyor]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Mechatronic Architecture of an Agri-Robot (e.g., Driverless Tractor)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SENS[SENSOR LAYER] --> CTRL[CONTROL LAYER]
    CTRL --> ACT[ACTUATOR LAYER]
    ACT --> CROP[FIELD ACTUATION]

    SENS --> S1[RTK GNSS Receiver]
    SENS --> S2[IMU Gyro Accelerometer]
    SENS --> S3[Wheel Encoder]
    SENS --> S4[LIDAR Scanner]
    SENS --> S5[Soil Moisture Sensor]

    CTRL --> C1[ECU Microcontroller]
    CTRL --> C2[Path Planning Module]
    CTRL --> C3[PID Controller]
    CTRL --> C4[CAN Bus Communication]

    ACT --> A1[Steer by Wire Motor]
    ACT --> A2[Throttle Actuator]
    ACT --> A3[Hydraulic Valve Block]
    ACT --> A4[Implement Hitch Control]

    CROP --> OUT1[Variable Rate Seeding]
    CROP --> OUT2[Site Specific Spraying]
    CROP --> OUT3[Autonomous Tillage]
```

## 4.3 Operational Sequence — Robotic Apple Harvester (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant V as Vision Module
    participant C as Central ECU
    participant A as Robotic Arm
    participant G as Gripper End Effector
    participant B as Storage Bin

    V->>C: Detect ripe apple and 3D coordinates
    C->>A: Compute inverse kinematics joint angles
    A->>G: Move to apple pose
    G->>G: Apply vacuum suction F equals dP times A cup
    G->>A: Twist and detach stem
    A->>B: Transport to bin
    C->>V: Reacquire next target
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture — Drone Spraying System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MISSION_PLANNING
        MP1[GIS Map Input]
        MP2[Boundary Detection]
        MP3[Flight Path Grid]
    end

    subgraph PERCEPTION
        P1[Multispectral Camera]
        P2[NDVI Calculation]
        P3[Obstacle LiDAR]
    end

    subgraph FLIGHT_CONTROL
        F1[PX4 Flight Stack]
        F2[GPS Position Hold]
        F3[Failsafe RTL Return to Launch]
    end

    subgraph ACTUATION
        AC1[Brushless Motors ESC]
        AC2[Peristaltic Pump]
        AC3[Nozzle Array PWM Modulated]
    end

    MISSION_PLANNING --> FLIGHT_CONTROL
    PERCEPTION --> FLIGHT_CONTROL
    FLIGHT_CONTROL --> ACTUATION
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**List any three types of agricultural robots with one example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Driverless Tractors** — Used for autonomous tillage and sowing. Example: John Deere 8R 410 with AutoTrac RTK guidance.
2. **Agricultural Drones (UAVs)** — Used for crop health imaging and variable-rate spraying. Example: DJI Agras T50.
3. **Robotic Harvesters** — Used for selective fruit picking. Example: Tevel Aerobotics tethered flying robots for apple harvesting.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for correct category + 1 mark for function + 1 mark for valid example)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the role of RTK-GNSS in agricultural robots?**

**Model Answer:**
RTK-GNSS (Real-Time Kinematic Global Navigation Satellite System) provides centimeter-level positional accuracy (typically ≤ 2 cm + 1 ppm) by using a base-station-rover differential correction. In agricultural robots, RTK enables **row-following precision for autonomous tractors**, **repeatable inter-row weeding paths**, and **geo-tagged crop health mapping** for variable-rate technology (VRT). It is the **backbone sensor** of precision agriculture.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for accuracy + 1 mark for application)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Classify the different types of agricultural robots based on their **mobility platform** and **operational function**. Discuss **any four categories in detail** with at least one commercial example and one key sensor per category. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A driverless tractor operates at $S = 7 \text{ km/h}$ with an implement width of $W = 3.5 \text{ m}$. Field efficiency $\eta_f = 0.75$.
- (i) Calculate the **theoretical field capacity** and **effective field capacity**.
- (ii) How many **hours** are required to cover **20 hectares**?
- (iii) If the same task is performed by **autonomous weeding robots** of width 1.2 m operating at 2.5 km/h with $\eta_f = 0.85$, compare the time required and comment on the **trade-off**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Classification & Discussion (7 marks):**

Agricultural robots are classified into **Ground-based, Aerial, and Stationary** categories.

**1. Driverless Tractors (Ground-based):**
- Function: Primary and secondary tillage, sowing, haulage.
- Key sensor: **RTK-GNSS** + **IMU** for closed-loop steering.
- Commercial example: **John Deere 8R AutoTrac**.

**2. Agricultural Drones — Aerial:**
- Function: Multispectral imaging, variable-rate spraying, seeding.
- Key sensor: **RGB + Multispectral camera** (NDVI computation).
- Commercial example: **DJI Agras T50**.

**3. Robotic Harvesters (Ground-based manipulator):**
- Function: Selective fruit picking (apple, strawberry, tomato).
- Key sensor: **RGB-D camera** for fruit detection + pose estimation.
- Commercial example: **Tevel Aerobotics / Octinion Rubion**.

**4. Weeding Robots (Small ground-based):**
- Function: Mechanical or laser-based inter-row weed control.
- Key sensor: **Vision camera + IMU** for row following.
- Commercial example: **Carbon Robotics LaserWeeder / Ecorobotix ARA**.

**5. Dairy Robots (Stationary / Barn-mobile):**
- Function: Automated milking, feed pushing, health monitoring.
- Key sensor: **Thermal + RFID tags** on cattle.
- Commercial example: **Lely Astronaut A5**.

*(Mark split: 1 for classification logic, 4 × 1.5 = 6 for the four categories — function, sensor, example)*

---

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Field Capacities:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_T &= \dfrac{S \times W}{10} = \dfrac{7 \times 3.5}{10} = 2.45 \;\text{ha/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_E &= C_T \times \eta_f = 2.45 \times 0.75 = 1.8375 \;\text{ha/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Time for 20 ha (Tractor):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{tractor} &= \dfrac{20}{1.8375} = 10.88 \;\text{h} \approx 10 \;\text{h}\;53 \;\text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Weeder Robot Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{T,w} &= \dfrac{2.5 \times 1.2}{10} = 0.30 \;\text{ha/h} \\[2pt]
C_{E,w} &= 0.30 \times 0.85 = 0.255 \;\text{ha/h} \\[2pt]
t_{weeder} &= \dfrac{20}{0.255} = 78.43 \;\text{h} \approx 78 \;\text{h}\;26 \;\text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Trade-off Comment:**
Although the weeding robot is **~7.2 times slower**, it offers **targeted action** (no crop damage), **lower soil compaction** (lighter weight), and **30–60% herbicide savings**. For **selective operations** like weeding, the slower but precise approach is agronomically superior. For **bulk tillage**, the tractor remains optimal.

*(Mark split: 2 for (i), 2 for (ii), 2 for (iii) calculation + 1 for trade-off comment)*

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **mechatronic architecture of an autonomous agricultural drone** used for spraying. Identify **perception, planning, control, and actuation** sub-systems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A multirotor spraying drone has the following specifications:
- Flight speed $v = 5 \text{ m/s}$,
- Effective swath $w = 5 \text{ m}$,
- Battery capacity $E_{bat} = 1000 \text{ Wh}$,
- Hover power $P_{hover} = 1500 \text{ W}$,
- Payload power $P_{payload} = 500 \text{ W}$,
- Operational efficiency $\eta_o = 0.75$.

Calculate:
- (i) **Flight endurance** in minutes.
- (ii) **Area covered per flight** (in hectares).
- (iii) **Number of drones** required to spray a **40-hectare** field in **one 8-hour shift**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Block Diagram & Sub-systems (7 marks):**

The autonomous agri-drone comprises four mechatronic layers:

| Layer | Sub-system | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Perception | Multispectral Camera, LiDAR, IMU | Detect crop health, obstacles, attitude |
| Planning | Mission Planner (QGroundControl), GIS Map | Generate Boustrophedon spray path |
| Control | PX4 / Ardupilot flight stack, PID loops | Stabilize roll/pitch/yaw, hold altitude |
| Actuation | BLDC motors + ESCs, Peristaltic pump, Nozzles | Generate thrust + dispense variable spray |

The closed-loop sequence is: **Sensor → ECU → Flight Controller → ESC → Motor + Pump → Field → Feedback**.

*(Mark split: 2 for diagram, 4 × 1 mark for sub-systems, 1 for closed-loop logic)*

---

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Flight Endurance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \dfrac{E_{bat}}{P_{hover} + P_{payload}} \\[2pt]
&= \dfrac{1000}{1500 + 500} = \dfrac{1000}{2000} = 0.5 \;\text{h} = 30 \;\text{minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Area per Flight:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{flight} &= v \times w \times T \times \eta_o \\[2pt]
&= 5 \times 5 \times (30 \times 60) \times 0.75 \\[2pt]
&= 25 \times 1800 \times 0.75 \\[2pt]
&= 33750 \;\text{m}^2 = 3.375 \;\text{ha}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Number of Drones for 40 ha in 8 hours:**

Drones per drone in 8 h (with battery swaps assumed instantaneous in this simplified model):

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{per\_drone\_per\_shift} &= 3.375 \;\text{ha/flight} \times \dfrac{8 \;\text{h}}{0.5 \;\text{h/flight}} \\[2pt]
&= 3.375 \times 16 = 54 \;\text{ha}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
N = \left\lceil \dfrac{40}{54} \right\rceil = 1 \;\text{drone}
$$

However, factoring in **refill time, weather contingency, and battery cooling**, the practical recommendation is **2–3 drones**.

*(Mark split: 2 + 2 + 3 for the three sub-parts)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Mixing units:** Always convert km/h → m/s **before** applying the formula $C = v \times w$. Wrong unit conversions cost 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting $\eta_f$:** Many students compute only $C_T$ and skip $C_E$ — KTU explicitly tests both.
> 3. **Drone vs. Tractor confusion:** Drones compute area per **flight cycle**, not per hour directly; always multiply by $T_{flight}$ first.
> 4. **NDVI formula sign errors:** NDVI is bounded in $[-1, +1]$; do not confuse NIR with Red in numerator.
> 5. **Classification completeness:** For 7-mark classification answers, **mention sensor + example** for each class; bare lists score only 3–4 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Five core agri-robot classes** for KTU: Driverless Tractors, Agri-Drones, Harvesters, Weeders, Dairy Robots.
- ✅ **Mobility platforms:** Ground, Aerial, Stationary.
- ✅ **Field Capacity formulas:** $C_T = SW/10$, $C_E = C_T \eta_f$.
- ✅ **Drone coverage formula:** $A = v \times w \times T_{flight} \times \eta_o$.
- ✅ **Battery endurance:** $T = E_{bat}/(P_{hover} + P_{payload})$.
- ✅ **RTK accuracy:** $\sigma \le 2 \text{ cm} + 1 \text{ ppm}$ — backbone of precision.
- ✅ **Pure Pursuit:** $\delta = \arctan(2L\sin\alpha / l_d)$.
- ✅ **NDVI:** $(\text{NIR} - \text{Red})/(\text{NIR} + \text{Red})$ — key crop-health index.
- ✅ **Soft-gripper force:** $F = \Delta P \times A_{cup}$.
- ✅ **Sustainability target:** 30–60% agrochemical reduction via precision.
- ✅ **Always state assumptions** on $\eta_f$ and $\eta_o$ in numerical answers.
- ✅ **Draw block diagrams** for any 7-mark architecture question — KTU awards 2 marks purely for neat diagrams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robotic Vision Systems in Agriculture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT864 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Robotic Vision Systems in Agriculture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Robotic Vision Systems in Agriculture** refer to the integrated electro-optical and computational subsystems mounted on autonomous or semi-autonomous agricultural platforms (ground-based rovers, UAVs/drones, tractor-mounted booms, or stationary gantries) that acquire, process, and interpret visual imagery of crops, soil, weeds, pests, and fruits to enable perception-driven actuation (targeted spraying, selective harvesting, weeding, navigation, and yield estimation).

According to the KTU 2024 Mechatronics syllabus (Module 2 – Sensors and Vision Systems), this topic falls under the umbrella of *Machine Vision in Field Robotics*, where the vision system acts as the **non-contact analog of the human eye** and is treated as a feedback sensor feeding into the closed-loop mechatronic controller.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> Robotic vision in agriculture = *“A machine-vision-based sensing architecture comprising illumination, image capture (2-D / 3-D / hyperspectral), pre-processing, segmentation, feature extraction, classification, and decision output, interfaced with the actuator block of an agricultural mechatronic system.”*

### 1.2 System-Level Composition

A robotic agricultural vision system consists of the following functional blocks:

| Block | Function | Agricultural Example |
|-------|----------|----------------------|
| **Illumination Unit** | Provides controlled, shadow-free lighting | LED ring for night-time fruit detection |
| **Image Sensor (CMOS/CCD)** | Converts light into electrical pixels | Color CMOS on a strawberry-picking robot |
| **Optics (Lens)** | Focuses scene onto sensor with desired FOV | Varifocal lens for variable crop-row height |
| **Frame Grabber / Interface** | Digitizes analog signal or streams digital data | GigE Vision / USB3 Vision link |
| **Processing Unit (GPU/Embedded)** | Runs CV algorithms (OpenCV, TensorRT) | Jetson Xavier NX onboard a weeding robot |
| **Decision/Actuation Layer** | Translates vision output to action | PWM signal to solenoid valve for spot-spray |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Farmer's Eye on a Chip"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:**
> Imagine a skilled farmer walking through a field at sunrise. The farmer's eye (a) senses reflected sunlight, (b) separates the green weeds from the brown soil, (c) judges fruit ripeness by hue and size, and (d) directs the hand to pluck only the ripe ones.
>
> The **Robotic Vision System** performs the same four functions — but with cameras (a), segmentation algorithms (b), color/shape classifiers (c), and servo/robotic arms (d). It replaces the *biological retina–brain–hand* chain with a *sensor–processor–actuator* chain.

### 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (Must be Memorized)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry-Standard Metrics:**
> - **Spatial Resolution:** measured in **megapixels (MP)** or **pixels per mm at the object plane**.
> - **Spectral Resolution:** number of bands (RGB = 3, Multispectral ≈ 5–10, Hyperspectral > 100).
> - **Temporal Resolution / Frame Rate:** **30 fps** (real-time) to **120 fps** (high-speed phenotyping).
> - **Field of View (FOV):** **20°–60°** for row-crop navigation, **90°+** for wide-area scouting.
> - **Working Distance (WD):** typically **0.3 m – 5 m** for hand-held/ground robots.
> - **Depth Accuracy (for stereo/ToF):** ± **2 mm at 1 m** for harvesting tasks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Pipeline (The "Vision Chain")

The agricultural vision system executes a deterministic 6-stage pipeline. Each stage has a definitive engineering purpose:

1. **Image Acquisition**
   - *Why:* Capture raw intensity data $I(x, y, \lambda)$ where $x, y$ are spatial coordinates and $\lambda$ is wavelength.
   - *How:* Synchronize shutter trigger with GPS/encoder pulse so each image is geo-tagged.

2. **Pre-processing**
   - *Why:* Sensor noise (photon shot noise, dark current), motion blur, and uneven illumination degrade downstream accuracy.
   - *How:* Apply a **Gaussian low-pass filter** for noise suppression and **histogram equalization** or **retinex** for lighting normalization.

3. **Segmentation**
   - *Why:* Isolate Region of Interest (ROI) — e.g., green vegetation from soil background.
   - *How:* Thresholding in HSV/Lab color space, or learned semantic segmentation (U-Net, DeepLab).

4. **Feature Extraction**
   - *Why:* Convert ROI into a compact, discriminative descriptor (shape, texture, color histogram).
   - *How:* **HOG (Histogram of Oriented Gradients)**, **SIFT/SURF**, or CNN feature maps.

5. **Classification / Detection**
   - *Why:* Assign semantic labels: *crop*, *weed*, *ripe*, *unripe*, *diseased*.
   - *How:* SVM, Random Forest, or deep detectors (**YOLOv8**, Faster-RCNN).

6. **Decision Output / Actuation**
   - *Why:* Translate detected class into control commands.
   - *How:* Send coordinates to robotic arm or trigger a precision nozzle via GPIO/CAN bus.

### 2.2 Pinhole Camera Model — The Foundation of All Vision Math

A pixel $(u, v)$ on the image plane originates from a 3-D world point $(X_w, Y_w, Z_w)$. The pinhole projection establishes a projective mapping:

$$s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} f_x & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}_{\text{Intrinsic Matrix } K} \begin{bmatrix} R & \vert & t \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

where $s$ is the scalar depth, $f_x = f / d_x$ and $f_y = f / d_y$ are the focal lengths in pixel units, $(c_x, c_y)$ is the principal point, and $[R \vert t]$ is the extrinsic rigid-body transform from world to camera frame.

### 2.3 Lens Distortion Correction (Mandatory in Agricultural Cameras)

Agricultural robots operate close to plants, so **radial and tangential distortion** must be corrected:

$$x_{distorted} = x \left( 1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6 \right) + \left[ 2 p_1 x y + p_2 \left( r^2 + 2 x^2 \right) \right]$$

$$y_{distorted} = y \left( 1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6 \right) + \left[ p_1 \left( r^2 + 2 y^2 \right) + 2 p_2 x y \right]$$

where $r^2 = x^2 + y^2$, and $k_1, k_2, k_3, p_1, p_2$ are the radial and tangential distortion coefficients determined via **Zhang's checkerboard calibration**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — Memorize Before Exam**

| # | Formula / Equation | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---------------------|-------------------|----------------|
| 1 | $FOV = 2 \arctan\!\left( \dfrac{d}{2 f} \right)$ | Field of View from sensor size $d$ and focal length $f$ | degrees |
| 2 | $GSD = \dfrac{H \cdot p}{f}$ | Ground Sampling Distance (pixel footprint) at altitude $H$, pixel pitch $p$ | cm/pixel |
| 3 | $u = f_x \dfrac{X_w}{Z_w} + c_x$ | Pinhole x-projection | pixels |
| 4 | $v = f_y \dfrac{Y_w}{Z_w} + c_y$ | Pinhole y-projection | pixels |
| 5 | $Z = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{d}$ | Stereo depth from baseline $B$ and disparity $d$ | meters |
| 6 | $ExG = 2 g - r - b$ | Excess Green Index (vegetation segmentation) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $NDVI = \dfrac{NIR - Red}{NIR + Red}$ | Normalized Difference Vegetation Index | $-1$ to $+1$ |
| 8 | $Hue = \arctan\!\left( \dfrac{\sqrt{3}(G - B)}{2R - G - B} \right)$ | HSV Hue from RGB | degrees |
| 9 | $\nabla I = \sqrt{I_x^2 + I_y^2}$ | Image gradient magnitude (edge detection) | intensity |
| 10 | $C = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Classification precision (weed/crop detection) | ratio |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - Formula 2 (GSD) is critical for UAV-based crop monitoring — e.g., a drone at $H = 50$ m altitude with a 12 MP camera and $f = 8$ mm lens achieves $GSD \approx 1.5$ cm/pixel, sufficient for weed detection but not for individual fruit sizing.
> - Formula 6 (ExG) is the **single most-used** vegetation index in KTU board questions for crop/weed segmentation.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Mechatronics

- **Precision Spraying:** Bosch/Bonirob and Ecorobotix use vision-guided boom sprayers to reduce herbicide usage by up to **95%**.
- **Selective Harvesting:** DOGtooth (UK strawberry picker) and FFRobotics (apple picker) rely on stereo vision to localize fruit within ±5 mm.
- **Autonomous Navigation:** John Deere's autonomous tractor uses **RTK-GPS + stereo vision** for row-following with sub-cm accuracy.
- **Phenotyping:** LemnaTec field phenotyping platforms use hyperspectral vision to predict yield and drought stress.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pinhole Projection from World to Pixel Coordinates

**Goal:** Show how a 3-D world point $(X_w, Y_w, Z_w)$ is mapped to 2-D pixel $(u, v)$.

#### Step 1 — Express the world point in camera coordinates
Apply the rigid-body transformation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \end{bmatrix} &= R \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \end{bmatrix} + t
\end{aligned}
$$

where $R \in SO(3)$ is the $3 \times 3$ rotation matrix encoding camera orientation (roll, pitch, yaw), and $t$ is the $3 \times 1$ translation vector (camera position in world frame).

#### Step 2 — Apply the pinhole projection (similar triangles)
The ideal pinhole projects points from the camera frame onto the image plane at distance $f$ (focal length). By the principle of similar triangles:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_i &= f \cdot \dfrac{X_c}{Z_c} \\
y_i &= f \cdot \dfrac{Y_c}{Z_c}
\end{aligned}
$$

Here $x_i, y_i$ are coordinates in the *image plane* (units: meters).

#### Step 3 — Convert from metric image coordinates to pixel coordinates
Pixel spacing $d_x, d_y$ (meters/pixel) along horizontal and vertical axes, and the principal point offset $(c_x, c_y)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u &= \dfrac{x_i}{d_x} + c_x = f_x \cdot \dfrac{X_c}{Z_c} + c_x \\
v &= \dfrac{y_i}{d_y} + c_y = f_y \cdot \dfrac{Y_c}{Z_c} + c_y
\end{aligned}
$$

where we define the focal length in pixel units as $f_x = f / d_x$ and $f_y = f / d_y$.

#### Step 4 — Compact homogeneous form
Introducing a depth scalar $s = Z_c$, the final projective equation is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} f_x & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **intrinsic matrix $K$** premultiplying the camera-frame coordinates. Combined with the extrinsic matrix $[R \mid t]$, it forms the complete camera projection equation shown in Section 2.2.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Insight:** KTU board questions frequently ask to derive the relationship between image pixel and world coordinates. Memorizing Steps 1–4 verbatim will fetch full marks (typically 7 marks).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stereo Depth (Disparity) for Fruit Localization

**Goal:** Show how a stereo pair of cameras computes the 3-D depth of an agricultural target (e.g., a fruit).

#### Step 1 — Geometric setup
Consider two cameras with parallel optical axes separated by a horizontal baseline $B$. Let the same world point $P$ project to pixel $(u_L, v)$ in the **left** camera and $(u_R, v)$ in the **right** camera (the row coordinate $v$ is identical under a rectified stereo pair).

#### Step 2 — Apply pinhole projection in both cameras

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_L &= f_x \cdot \dfrac{X}{Z} + c_x \\
u_R &= f_x \cdot \dfrac{X - B}{Z} + c_x
\end{aligned}
$$

where $X, Z$ are the lateral and depth coordinates of $P$ relative to the left camera.

#### Step 3 — Compute disparity
Disparity $d$ is the horizontal pixel shift between the two matched image points:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= u_L - u_R = f_x \cdot \dfrac{X}{Z} - f_x \cdot \dfrac{X - B}{Z} = f_x \cdot \dfrac{B}{Z}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4 — Solve for depth $Z$
Rearranging the disparity equation to isolate $Z$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z &= \dfrac{f_x \cdot B}{d}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **stereo depth formula** in Formula Sheet entry 5. As disparity $d$ increases (object closer), depth $Z$ decreases — the correct inverse behavior.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Example (KTU-typical):**
> Given $f_x = 800$ pixels, $B = 120$ mm, and $d = 96$ pixels:
>
> $$ Z = \frac{800 \times 120}{96} = 1000 \text{ mm} = 1.0 \text{ m} $$
>
> Thus the fruit lies exactly **1.0 m** from the left camera — a typical picking-arm working distance.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Excess Green Index (ExG) Thresholding

**Goal:** Derive the thresholded binary map used to isolate green vegetation from soil.

#### Step 1 — Normalize RGB channels
Convert integer pixel values $R_i, G_i, B_i \in [0, 255]$ to floating-point:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= \dfrac{R_i}{R_i + G_i + B_i} \\
g &= \dfrac{G_i}{R_i + G_i + B_i} \\
b &= \dfrac{B_i}{R_i + G_i + B_i}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2 — Compute the Excess Green Index
The ExG index amplifies the green channel while suppressing red and blue:

$$
\begin{aligned}
ExG(x, y) &= 2 g(x, y) - r(x, y) - b(x, y)
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 — Apply Otsu-style threshold
A pixel is classified as vegetation iff $ExG(x, y) > T$, where $T$ is determined by Otsu's method that maximizes inter-class variance between vegetation and background:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_B^2(T) &= \omega_0(T) \, \omega_1(T) \left[ \mu_0(T) - \mu_1(T) \right]^2
\end{aligned}
$$

with $T^* = \arg\max_T \sigma_B^2(T)$. The resulting binary mask $B(x, y)$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B(x, y) &= \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } ExG(x, y) > T^* \quad \text{(vegetation)} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \quad \text{(soil/background)} \end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Justification:**
> ExG is robust to *varying illumination* because the RGB-to-chromaticity normalization cancels out multiplicative lighting changes — a property critical for outdoor agricultural deployment where sunlight intensity fluctuates throughout the day.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Selection for Common Tasks

| Agricultural Task | Recommended Algorithm | Key Parameter | Real-World Sensor |
|-------------------|------------------------|---------------|--------------------|
| Crop row detection | Hough Line Transform on edge map | $\rho$, $\theta$ resolution | B&W CMOS @ 30 fps |
| Weed/crop segmentation | ExG + Otsu threshold | $T^*$ | RGB camera, 1 MP |
| Fruit counting | Blob detection / watershed | Min blob area | Stereo RGB-D |
| Disease detection | CNN (ResNet-50) on leaf image | Confidence threshold | Hyperspectral 450–850 nm |
| Soil moisture mapping | NIR + thermal fusion | NDVI + surface $T$ | Multispectral + thermal |
| Canopy volume estimation | Voxel carving from multi-view | Voxel resolution | Multi-camera rig |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Functional Architecture of an Agricultural Robotic Vision System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Crop Field Scene] --> B[Illumination Unit LED Ring]
    B --> C[Optical Lens Varifocal]
    C --> D[CMOS Image Sensor 5 MP RGB]
    D --> E[Frame Grabber GigE Vision Link]
    E --> F[Embedded Processor Jetson Xavier]
    F --> G[Preprocessing Gaussian + CLAHE]
    G --> H[Segmentation ExG and Otsu]
    H --> I[Feature Extraction HOG or CNN]
    I --> J[Classifier YOLO or SVM]
    J --> K{Decision Logic}
    K --> L[Spray Nozzle PWM Trigger]
    K --> M[Robotic Arm Servo Cmd]
    K --> N[Telemetry Uplink MQTT]
    L --> O[Actuator Block]
    M --> O
    N --> P[Farm Cloud Dashboard]
    O --> Q[Field Action]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - The pipeline flows from *Scene → Sensor → Processor → Decision → Actuator*, which mirrors the canonical mechatronics closed-loop architecture (sensing → control → actuation).
> - The `{Decision Logic}` diamond node represents a software conditional that dispatches outputs to one of three actuator paths — a key KTU concept tied to **multi-modal actuation**.

### 4.2 Image Processing Sub-Pipeline (Internal View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Image Frame] --> B[Noise Filtering Gaussian 5x5]
    B --> C[Color Space Convert RGB to HSV]
    C --> D[Vegetation Mask ExG Greater Than T]
    D --> E[Morphological Cleanup Opening then Closing]
    E --> F[Connected Components Labeling]
    F --> G[Feature Vector HOG Descriptor]
    G --> H[Classifier YOLO Class Index]
    H --> I[Output Bounding Box and Confidence]
    I --> J[Coordinate Transform Pixel to World]
    J --> K[Send to Motion Controller CAN Bus]
```

### 4.3 Closed-Loop Mechatronic Integration View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Reference Setpoint Target Fruit Coordinates] --> C[Vision Controller]
    FB[Vision Feedback Pixel Position] --> C
    C --> ACT[Actuator Robotic Arm PWM]
    ACT --> PL[Plant and Fruit]
    PL --> S2[Sensor Image Capture]
    S2 --> PROC[Image Processing Pipeline]
    PROC --> FB
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a Closed-Loop Diagram?**
> This is exactly the **Mechatronics Quadrant** representation that KTU expects students to draw when asked *"Explain the role of vision in a mechatronic agricultural system."* Always show the *feedback arrow* from sensor back to controller — examiners explicitly award 2 marks for it.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**
>
> **Q1.** Define *Robotic Vision System* as applied to precision agriculture. List any **four** functional blocks of such a system.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> A Robotic Vision System in agriculture is a computer-vision-based sensing subsystem that automatically interprets crop, soil, weed, and fruit imagery to enable perception-driven actuation in autonomous field robots.
>
> The four functional blocks are:
> 1. **Illumination Unit** — provides controlled lighting (e.g., LED ring, strobe).
> 2. **Image Sensor (CMOS/CCD)** — captures the 2-D intensity field of the scene.
> 3. **Processing Unit** — runs segmentation, feature extraction, and classification.
> 4. **Decision/Actuation Layer** — converts vision output into control signals (PWM, CAN, GPIO) for nozzles, arms, or vehicle steering.

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**
>
> **Q2.** State the **Excess Green Index (ExG)** formula and explain its significance in crop/weed segmentation.
>
> **Model Answer:**
>
> $$ ExG = 2g - r - b $$
>
> where $r, g, b$ are the chromaticity-normalized red, green, and blue values.
>
> **Significance:**
> - ExG amplifies the green channel relative to red and blue, producing a high response on vegetation and a low response on soil, senescent leaves, and residue.
> - It is **illumination-invariant** (due to chromaticity normalization), making it robust to outdoor lighting variations — a critical property for agricultural deployment.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 2 Internal Choice Pattern]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the **pinhole camera model** and derive the relationship between a 3-D world point $(X_w, Y_w, Z_w)$ and its image-plane pixel coordinate $(u, v)$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain how **stereo vision** is used in an agricultural fruit-picking robot. Derive the depth formula $Z = \dfrac{f_x \cdot B}{d}$ and compute the depth of an apple if $f_x = 600$ pixels, baseline $B = 80$ mm, and measured disparity $d = 48$ pixels. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**Solution (a):**

The **pinhole camera model** assumes light rays from a scene converge through an infinitesimally small aperture onto an image plane. The schematic is:

```
        Z_c ↑
             |        Image Plane
             |       _________________
             |      |                 |
   P(.) -----+-----|------→ X_i      |
             |      |       (.)       |
             |      |_________________|
             |     / Pinhole O
             |    /
             |   /
        Y_c  ↓  /
```

**Derivation Steps:**

1. Express world point $(X_w, Y_w, Z_w)$ in camera frame via extrinsic matrix:
   $$\begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \end{bmatrix} = R \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \end{bmatrix} + t$$
2. Apply pinhole similar-triangle projection:
   $$x_i = f \cdot \frac{X_c}{Z_c}, \quad y_i = f \cdot \frac{Y_c}{Z_c}$$
3. Convert to pixel coordinates using pixel pitch and principal point:
   $$u = \frac{x_i}{d_x} + c_x = f_x \cdot \frac{X_c}{Z_c} + c_x$$
   $$v = \frac{y_i}{d_y} + c_y = f_y \cdot \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} + c_y$$
4. The final homogeneous form is the standard camera matrix equation:
   $$s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} [R \mid t] \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Solution (b):**

In a stereo fruit-picking robot, two cameras with parallel optical axes and baseline $B$ capture the same apple from slightly different viewpoints. After **epipolar rectification**, the apple's projections have the same vertical coordinate $v$, and the horizontal shift (disparity) $d = u_L - u_R$ encodes its depth.

**Derivation:**

Applying the pinhole equation to both cameras:
$$\begin{aligned}
u_L &= f_x \cdot \frac{X}{Z} + c_x \\
u_R &= f_x \cdot \frac{X - B}{Z} + c_x
\end{aligned}$$

Subtracting:
$$d = u_L - u_R = f_x \cdot \frac{B}{Z}$$

Solving for $Z$:
$$Z = \frac{f_x \cdot B}{d}$$

**Numerical Computation:**
Given $f_x = 600$ pixels, $B = 80$ mm, $d = 48$ pixels:
$$Z = \frac{600 \times 80}{48} = 1000 \text{ mm} = 1.0 \text{ m}$$

Thus the apple is located **1.0 m** from the left camera — a typical working distance for a picking arm.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**(a)** Discuss the role of **hyperspectral and multispectral imaging** in agricultural robotics. Compare RGB, multispectral (5 bands), and hyperspectral (>100 bands) sensors in terms of spectral resolution, cost, and application. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Excess Green Index (ExG)** algorithm for crop/weed segmentation. With a clear threshold-based decision rule, show how a binary vegetation mask is generated. Mention any **two limitations** of ExG in real field conditions. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

**Solution (a):**

Multispectral and hyperspectral cameras capture reflected light at multiple discrete wavelengths, enabling the robot to detect physiological traits of plants invisible to RGB cameras.

**Comparison Table:**

| Sensor Type | No. of Bands | Spectral Range | Cost (USD) | Typical Agricultural Application |
|-------------|--------------|-----------------|-------------|-----------------------------------|
| **RGB** | 3 (R, G, B) | 400–700 nm | 50–500 | Basic weed detection, fruit color |
| **Multispectral** | 5–10 | 400–2500 nm | 5,000–25,000 | NDVI-based crop health, nitrogen status |
| **Hyperspectral** | 100–300 | 400–2500 nm | 25,000–100,000+ | Disease detection, water stress, biochemical content |

**Role in Agricultural Robotics:**
- **Multispectral:** UAV-mounted MicaSense RedEdge senses Red-Edge band (~720 nm), enabling NDVI computation for variable-rate fertilization.
- **Hyperspectral:** Onboard a phenotyping robot, it identifies specific pathogen signatures (e.g., late blight in potato) via spectral angle mapper (SAM) classification.
- **RGB + NIR fusion:** Low-cost variant for NDVI computation without a full multispectral rig.

**Solution (b):**

The **ExG (Excess Green Index)** algorithm isolates green vegetation pixels by leveraging the strong green reflectance of chlorophyll-rich leaves against soil backgrounds.

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. **Normalize RGB channels** to chromaticity:
   $$r = \frac{R}{R+G+B}, \quad g = \frac{G}{R+G+B}, \quad b = \frac{B}{R+G+B}$$
2. **Compute ExG index** for every pixel:
   $$ExG(x, y) = 2g(x, y) - r(x, y) - b(x, y)$$
3. **Determine threshold $T^*$** via Otsu's method (maximizes inter-class variance):
   $$\sigma_B^2(T) = \omega_0(T) \omega_1(T) [\mu_0(T) - \mu_1(T)]^2$$
4. **Generate binary vegetation mask**:
   $$B(x, y) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } ExG(x, y) > T^* \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Two Limitations of ExG:**
1. **Confusion with senescent/yellow leaves:** As chlorophyll degrades, the green signal weakens, causing ExG to misclassify drying crops as background — a critical issue during harvest season.
2. **Sensitivity to specular highlights:** Under direct sunlight, reflective waxy leaf surfaces produce saturated pixels that distort chromaticity normalization, leading to fragmented vegetation masks.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Omitting the baseline explanation in stereo depth derivation:** Students often write $Z = f B / d$ directly without showing the pinhole equations for both cameras. KTU examiners deduct up to **3 marks** if the derivation steps are missing.
> 2. **Confusing intrinsic and extrinsic matrices:** Always state clearly that $K$ is *intrinsic* (sensor-specific) and $[R \vert t]$ is *extrinsic* (pose-specific). Mixing them up loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting units in numerical problems:** When solving for $Z$, express the final answer in **meters (or mm)** with explicit unit conversion — a frequent 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **No diagram in pinhole camera question:** Even a hand-drawn schematic showing the aperture and image plane earns **2 marks** as part of the 7-mark sub-question.
> 5. **Using RGB mean intensity for ExG:** ExG requires *chromaticity-normalized* $r, g, b$, not raw $R, G, B$. This single error invalidates the segmentation logic and forfeits the full 7 marks in Part (b) of Question B.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets:**
> - **Robotic Vision = Sensor + Processor + Actuator** — it is the mechatronic *sensing layer* of an agricultural robot.
> - The **pinhole model** is the mathematical foundation; memorize $s [u, v, 1]^T = K [R \mid t] [X_w, Y_w, Z_w, 1]^T$.
> - **Intrinsic matrix $K$** contains $f_x, f_y, c_x, c_y$; **extrinsic** $[R \mid t]$ encodes camera pose.
> - **Stereo depth** $Z = f_x B / d$: closer objects → larger disparity.
> - **GSD** (Ground Sampling Distance) = $H \cdot p / f$: critical for UAV imaging; lower is better for detection.
> - **ExG** = $2g - r - b$: the workhorse algorithm for crop/weed segmentation; pair with **Otsu's threshold**.
> - **NDVI** = $(NIR - Red)/(NIR + Red)$: vegetation health indicator for multispectral sensing.
> - **Lens distortion correction** uses 5 coefficients $(k_1, k_2, k_3, p_1, p_2)$ — calibrate using **Zhang's checkerboard method**.
> - **RGB** = 3 bands, **Multispectral** = 5–10 bands, **Hyperspectral** = 100+ bands; cost scales with spectral resolution.
> - **Pipeline order:** Acquisition → Pre-processing → Segmentation → Feature Extraction → Classification → Actuation.
> - Always include a **closed-loop feedback arrow** in mechatronic block diagrams — KTU examiners reward it.
> - In numerical problems, **always state units** (mm, m, pixels, fps) explicitly.
> - **Two major algorithms to master:** Pinhole projection derivation and stereo depth derivation (these appear in nearly every KTU board exam for Module 2).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robot Manipulators and Grippers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT864 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Robot Manipulators and Grippers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Robot Manipulator — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme)**
> A **Robot Manipulator** is a programmable, multi-axis mechanical arm composed of a series of rigid bodies (**links**) connected by powered articulations (**joints**), terminating in an **end-effector** (tool or gripper), designed to perform industrial tasks such as material handling, welding, assembly, and inspection by executing programmed trajectories in 3D Cartesian space.

The structure replicates a human arm:
- **Base** → acts as the shoulder anchor (mounted to floor/pedestal)
- **Links** → analogous to upper arm, forearm, palm (rigid members)
- **Joints** → analogous to shoulder, elbow, wrist (provide relative motion)
- **End-effector / Gripper** → analogous to the human hand (performs the task)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Arm

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture:** Imagine your own arm picking up a cup from a table.

| Human Arm Component | Robot Manipulator Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
