<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
**Define Precision Agriculture. List any four enabling technologies.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Precision Agriculture (PA)** is an integrated farm management approach that uses information technology, GPS, sensors, and variable-rate actuators to deliver site-specific crop inputs based on localized field conditions.

The "**4 Rs**" principle underpins PA: the **Right input**, in the **Right amount**, at the **Right time**, in the **Right place**.

**Four enabling technologies:**
1. **GNSS / RTK-GPS** for cm-level positioning of tractors and drones.
2. **Soil and crop sensors** (NPK, moisture, multispectral) for real-time data acquisition.
3. **Variable Rate Technology (VRT)** for spatially modulated application of inputs.
4. **Geographic Information Systems (GIS)** for spatial data analysis and prescription map generation.

*(Valid 3-mark answer: definition 1 mark + 4 technologies ½ mark each = 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**What is NDVI? State its significance and the range of values for bare soil, healthy crop, and water bodies.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NDVI (Normalized Difference Vegetation Index)** is a remote-sensing metric that quantifies vegetation vigour using the difference between Near-Infrared (NIR) and Red reflectance, normalized by their sum.

$$
\text{NDVI} = \frac{\text{NIR} - \text{Red}}{\text{NIR} + \text{Red}}
$$

**Range of values:**

| Surface | Approx. NDVI |
|---|---|
| **Water body** | $< 0$ (typically $-0.2$ to $-0.1$) |
| **Bare soil** | $\approx 0$ to $0.15$ |
| **Sparse vegetation** | $0.2$ to $0.4$ |
| **Healthy dense crop** | $0.6$ to $0.9$ |

**Significance:** NDVI is used to monitor crop health, detect stress (drought, disease, nutrient deficiency), and generate prescription maps for variable-rate fertilization and irrigation.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split into two sub-parts: **(a) = 7 marks** and **(b) = 7 marks**. Students must answer **one full question** (with internal choice between Q-A and Q-B) per module in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

---

### **Question A — [14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern Question]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **architecture of a precision agriculture system**. Explain the role of GPS, GIS, and VRT in modern farming. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **smart irrigation system** uses a capacitive soil moisture sensor interfaced to an ESP32 microcontroller. Explain:
- (i) The working principle of the capacitive sensor. **(3 marks)**
- (ii) The control logic used to actuate the solenoid valve. **(2 marks)**
- (iii) How LoRaWAN can be integrated for long-range field communication. **(2 marks)**

#### Model Answer for Q-A (a):

The precision agriculture architecture consists of **four layers** — Sensing, Processing, Actuation, and Decision.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1["SOIL SENSOR"] --> p1["MICROCONTROLLER"]
    s2["WEATHER SENSOR"] --> p1
    s3["GPS MODULE"] --> p1
    p1 --> a1["VRT SPRAYER"]
    p1 --> a2["AUTOMATED VALVE"]
    p1 --> c1["CLOUD DASHBOARD"]
    c1 --> p1
```

**Role of GPS:** Provides **real-time geolocation** of the tractor/implement. With **RTK corrections**, accuracy reaches **±2 cm**, enabling autosteer and avoiding overlap (>10% to <2% overlap). This data is geo-tagged for mapping.

**Role of GIS:** A **Geographic Information System** stores, analyses, and visualizes spatially-referenced data. It overlays yield maps, soil maps, and elevation models to generate **prescription maps** that guide variable-rate operations.

**Role of VRT:** **Variable Rate Technology** modulates the application rate of inputs (seed, fertilizer, water, pesticide) in real time as the machine traverses zones of differing need. For example, a VRT sprayer may apply 200 L/ha in nutrient-deficient zones and 100 L/ha elsewhere, reducing total consumption by **30–50%**.

*(Marks distribution: diagram 2, GPS 2, GIS 1.5, VRT 1.5 = 7 marks.)*

#### Model Answer for Q-A (b):

**(i) Capacitive sensor working principle (3 marks):**
A capacitive soil moisture sensor uses **two conductive electrodes** embedded in the soil. The soil acts as the **dielectric medium** of a parallel-plate capacitor. The capacitance is:

$$
C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r \cdot A}{d}
$$

Since water has a high dielectric constant ($\varepsilon_r \approx 80$) compared to dry soil ($\varepsilon_r \approx 3$ to $5$), an increase in soil moisture increases $\varepsilon_r$ and therefore $C$. The microcontroller measures the frequency shift of an RC oscillator whose capacitance is the sensor, converts it to a digital value, and applies a **calibration equation** to estimate volumetric water content $\theta_v$.

**(ii) Control logic for solenoid valve (2 marks):**
The ESP32 reads $\theta_v$ every 30 minutes. If $\theta_v < \theta_{\text{threshold}}$ (e.g., 20% for loam) AND no rain is forecast in the next 6 hours, a digital HIGH signal is sent to a relay module driving the **12 V DC solenoid valve**, opening water flow. Once $\theta_v$ reaches the upper threshold (e.g., 30%), the valve closes. A **maximum run-time safeguard** (e.g., 45 minutes) prevents over-irrigation in case of sensor failure.

**(iii) LoRaWAN integration (2 marks):**
For large farms (>500 m radius), Wi-Fi is insufficient. A **LoRaWAN module (e.g., SX1276, Ra-02)** operating at **433 MHz / 868 MHz / 915 MHz** ISM bands provides **2–15 km line-of-sight** communication at ultra-low power (~10–100 mW). The ESP32 sends soil data packets via SPI to the LoRa transceiver. A **LoRa gateway** at the farm office forwards packets to a cloud server (e.g., **The Things Network → MQTT → AWS IoT**), where dashboards and SMS alerts are generated.

---

### **Question B — [14 Marks, Alternative Choice]**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern Question]**

**(a)** Explain the **role of UAVs (drones) in modern agriculture**. Describe the multispectral imaging technique and the computation of NDVI with an example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **mechatronic design of an autonomous tractor** covering:
- (i) Sensors required (GNSS, IMU, LiDAR) and their functions. **(3 marks)**
- (ii) Path planning and obstacle avoidance strategy. **(2 marks)**
- (iii) Actuator and controller selection. **(2 marks)**

#### Model Answer for Q-B (a):

**Role of UAVs in agriculture:**
- **Aerial crop scouting** — high-resolution RGB/multispectral imagery in hours, not days.
- **NDVI / variable-rate prescription maps** — identify stressed zones for targeted action.
- **Pesticide / fertilizer spraying** — drones (e.g., DJI Agras T40) carry 40 L tanks, spray at **5–10 m/s**, and reduce chemical use by **30–50%**.
- **Plant counting and germination analysis** — computer vision pipelines on stitched orthomosaics.
- **Disaster assessment** — flood, hail, and drought damage mapping within hours.

**Multispectral imaging technique:**
A multispectral camera (e.g., **MicaSense RedEdge, Parrot Sequoia**) captures reflectance in discrete bands: **Blue (475 nm), Green (560 nm), Red (660 nm), NIR (840 nm)**, and **Red Edge (717 nm)**. The camera is mounted on a drone flying at **50–120 m altitude** with **75–85% front/side overlap** for photogrammetric stitching. Each pixel records the radiance reflected by vegetation.

**NDVI computation example:**
Suppose a pixel records $\text{NIR} = 0.50$ and $\text{Red} = 0.08$.

$$
\text{NDVI} = \frac{0.50 - 0.08}{0.50 + 0.08} = \frac{0.42}{0.58} \approx 0.724
$$

This indicates **dense, healthy vegetation** (likely at peak growth stage).

*(Marks: UAV roles 2, multispectral 2, NDVI derivation + example 3 = 7 marks.)*

#### Model Answer for Q-B (b):

**(i) Sensors and functions (3 marks):**
- **GNSS / RTK-GPS receiver** (e.g., u-blox ZED-F9P): provides **±2 cm** absolute position; double antenna gives **heading**.
- **IMU** (e.g., BMI088, MPU-9250): 6-axis accelerometer + gyroscope for **roll, pitch, yaw** estimation; combines with GPS in an **EKF (Extended Kalman Filter)** for robust state estimation.
- **LiDAR** (e.g., Velodyne VLP-16, SICK LMS511): 360° 2D/3D scanning to detect obstacles up to **100 m** ahead; provides geometry for SLAM.

**(ii) Path planning & obstacle avoidance (2 marks):**
The tractor follows a **Boustrophedon (back-and-forth) pattern** parallel to crop rows. The **A\*** or **DWA (Dynamic Window Approach)** algorithm generates waypoints. When LiDAR detects an obstacle (e.g., a person or animal), a **safety bubble of 3 m** is enforced, and the planner re-routes around it. If the obstacle persists > 5 seconds, the tractor **stops and alarms the operator**.

**(iii) Actuator and controller selection (2 marks):**
- **Steering actuator:** A brushless DC motor with a planetary gear reducer coupled to the steering column, controlled by a **PID loop** that minimizes **cross-track error (XTE)** from the reference path.
- **Throttle and brake:** Electronic throttle via CAN bus; brake via proportional hydraulic valve.
- **Master controller:** An **industrial ECU** (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson Orin + STM32) running **ROS 2 Humble**, with a **Model Predictive Controller (MPC)** running at **50 Hz** for longitudinal/lateral control.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in Agricultural Mechatronics Questions**
>
> 1. **Writing formulas without units or substituting numbers** — KTU examiners deduct up to 1 mark per question for missing units in numericals.
> 2. **Confusing NDVI range** — many students incorrectly state NDVI ranges from 0 to 1, but it actually ranges from **−1 to +1**. Water gives negative values.
> 3. **Skipping the block diagram** — in 7-mark sub-parts, **a labelled block diagram is mandatory** (carries 1.5–2 marks). Drawing it on the left margin and labelling on the right is the safest strategy.
> 4. **Vague "GPS gives position"** — be specific: **"RTK-GPS gives ±2 cm position at 10 Hz update rate"**.
> 5. **No mention of power/battery** for drones and IoT nodes — examiners often look for a line on **"powered by LiPo battery, 25 V, 16000 mAh, 20 min flight time"**.
> 6. **Ignoring safety in autonomous systems** — always mention **emergency stop, obstacle detection, and geofencing** in tractor/robot answers.
> 7. **Writing essays without structure** — KTU expects **numbered sub-points, tables, and short paragraphs**, not running prose.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**
>
> - **Precision Agriculture (PA):** Right input, Right amount, Right time, Right place. Backbone of modern farming.
> - **Key technologies:** GNSS/RTK, GIS, VRT, multispectral imaging, IoT, AI/ML.
> - **NDVI range:** **−1 to +1**; healthy vegetation **> 0.6**, bare soil **~ 0**, water **< 0**.
> - **Field capacity formula:** $C_e = \dfrac{\eta \cdot v \cdot w}{10}$ (in ha/h).
> - **RTK-GPS accuracy:** **±2 cm** (vs. standard GPS **±2–5 m**).
> - **Capacitive soil sensor:** Exploits water's high dielectric constant ($\varepsilon_r \approx 80$) to infer moisture.
> - **WUE definition:** Crop yield per unit of water consumed; smart drip irrigation achieves **> 90%** WUE.
> - **UAV applications:** Scouting, NDVI mapping, spraying (DJI Agras), plant counting.
> - **Autonomous tractor sensors:** RTK-GNSS, IMU, LiDAR, stereo camera.
> - **Harvesting robot success rate:** **> 85%** for apples, strawberries, tomatoes.
> - **Communication protocols:** LoRaWAN (long range, low power), Wi-Fi (short range, high bandwidth), ZigBee (mesh, medium range).
> - **Cloud platforms:** AWS IoT, Azure FarmBeats, The Things Network, custom MQTT brokers.
> - **Hydroponics sensors:** **pH (ideal 5.5–6.5)**, **EC (1.2–2.4 mS/cm)**, **Dissolved Oxygen (> 5 mg/L)**.
> - **Greenhouse control:** PID / fuzzy logic; sensors: **DHT22 (T, RH), CO₂, PAR**.
> - **VRT savings:** **30–50%** fertilizer, **40–60%** water, **> 80%** pesticide overlap reduction.
> - **Energy metric:** $P = T \cdot \omega$ (mechanical power from torque and speed).
> - **Topp equation:** Empirical cubic relation between $\varepsilon_r$ and $\theta_v$ used in TDR sensors.
> - **Safety-first:** Always include emergency stop, geofencing, and obstacle detection in autonomous designs.
> - **AI/ML in agriculture:** Disease detection (**Plantix**, **>90%** accuracy), yield prediction (LSTM/Transformer models), weed recognition (YOLO-based CNNs).

---

**End of Module 4 — Emerging Trends in Mechatronics for Agriculture**

*Prepared in accordance with the KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels (Remember → Understand → Apply → Analyze → Evaluate).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Internet of Things (IoT) in Agriculture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT864 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Internet of Things (IoT) in Agriculture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Internet of Things (IoT) in Agriculture** refers to the integrated network of physical devices, sensors, actuators, communication systems, and computational platforms deployed in agricultural environments to collect real-time field data, transmit it through wireless protocols, process it via cloud or edge computing, and autonomously execute precision actions — thereby enabling data-driven decision-making, resource optimization, and enhanced crop yield with minimal human intervention.

In KTU 2024 Mechatronics parlance, **Smart Agriculture (or Smart Farming)** is the cyber-physical convergence of:
- **Sensing Layer** (soil, weather, crop, livestock sensors)
- **Communication Layer** (LPWAN, ZigBee, Wi-Fi, LoRa, NB-IoT, 5G)
- **Processing Layer** (Cloud/Edge analytics, AI/ML inference)
- **Actuation Layer** (smart irrigation valves, drones, robotic harvesters)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Farmer's Sixth Sense"**
> 
> Imagine a farmer named *Ravi* who, instead of physically walking through his **5-acre** paddy field every 2 hours, simply checks his **smartphone**. The phone tells him:
> - "Soil moisture in Zone B is **18%** — below the critical **25%** threshold."
> - "Pump #2 has been activated automatically. **450 liters** of water delivered."
> - "Nitrogen deficiency detected in Sector 4 — drone spraying scheduled at **6:15 AM**."
> 
> IoT in agriculture is essentially giving the farmer a **digital nervous system** spread across the entire field. Just as your body's nerves continuously send signals to your brain, the field's sensors continuously send data to the cloud, and the cloud acts as the "brain" that triggers the "muscles" (actuators). The farmer becomes a **strategic supervisor** rather than a manual laborer.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Parameters

The following key performance and operational metrics are **standardized in KTU 2024 Mechatronics evaluation** for IoT-agriculture systems:

- **Soil Moisture Content (SMC)** — measured in **% VWC (Volumetric Water Content)**, range **0–50%**
- **Soil pH** — range **0–14**, optimal for most crops: **5.5–7.5**
- **EC (Electrical Conductivity)** — measured in **dS/m** (deciSiemens per meter), range **0–5 dS/m**
- **Atmospheric Temperature** — **°C**, optimal **20–30°C**
- **Relative Humidity (RH)** — **%**, optimal **40–70%**
- **PAR (Photosynthetically Active Radiation)** — **μmol/m²/s**, optimal **300–800**
- **CO₂ Concentration** — **ppm**, ambient **400 ppm**
- **NDVI (Normalized Difference Vegetation Index)** — dimensionless, range **0–1**
- **Latency** — wireless transmission delay, target **< 2 seconds** for control loops
- **Power Budget** — node battery life target **> 2 years** (for LPWAN nodes)

## 1.4 Why IoT in Agriculture — The KTU Perspective

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module-4 Learning Outcome (Implied):**
> The student shall be able to describe the architecture, components, and applications of IoT-enabled mechatronic systems in the agricultural domain, and evaluate the role of sensor fusion, wireless protocols, and automated actuation in precision agriculture.

**India-Specific Context (KTU Examiner Favourite):**
- Agriculture supports **~58%** of India's population (economic survey data)
- Average landholding size in Kerala: **< 0.5 hectares** — small/marginal farmers dominate
- Water scarcity + climate variability = **urgent need** for precision resource management
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Layers of IoT in Agriculture

The standard **4-layer IoT-Agri architecture** (per KTU 2024 Mechatronics syllabus) is:

### Layer 1 — Perception / Sensing Layer
- **Function:** Analog/digital capture of physical, chemical, and biological variables
- **Components:** Soil moisture probes, DHT11/DHT22, NPK sensors, pH electrodes, leaf wetness sensors, GPS modules, RGB/multispectral cameras, accelerometers (for livestock)
- **Why this layer:** Without accurate transduction, every downstream decision is flawed — the principle of **Garbage-In-Garbage-Out (GIGO)**

### Layer 2 — Network / Communication Layer
- **Function:** Reliable, low-power data transport from field to gateway/cloud
- **Protocols:**
  - **Short-range:** ZigBee (IEEE 802.15.4), BLE, Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11)
  - **Long-range LPWAN:** LoRaWAN, Sigfox, NB-IoT (Cat-M1)
  - **Satellite:** For remote/rural coverage
- **Key selection criteria:** Range, bandwidth, battery life, node density

### Layer 3 — Processing / Edge-Cloud Layer
- **Function:** Data aggregation, analytics, ML inference, decision logic
- **Sub-layers:**
  - **Edge:** On-board microcontrollers (ESP32, STM32) for local decisions
  - **Fog:** Local gateways (Raspberry Pi, Jetson Nano)
  - **Cloud:** AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT for storage + AI

### Layer 4 — Application / Actuation Layer
- **Function:** Human-Machine Interface (HMI) + automated physical actions
- **Components:** Mobile dashboards, SMS/voice alerts, solenoid valves, motor-driven pumps, UAVs (drones), robotic sprayers, climate-control fans

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic (Smart Irrigation Example)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical KTU 14-mark question pattern** for Module-4 IoT.

1. **Soil-moisture sensor** reads SMC at depth **15 cm** and **30 cm**
2. Sensor output (e.g., **0–3.3 V** analog) is conditioned via **signal conditioning circuit** (amplifier + RC filter)
3. **ADC** on microcontroller (e.g., ESP32 12-bit) converts analog → digital
4. Firmware compares reading against **threshold** (say, **25% VWC**)
5. If reading < threshold → microcontroller drives a **relay/MOSFET** → activates **solenoid valve** → water flows
6. Sensor continuously samples every **60 seconds**; when SMC ≥ threshold → valve closes
7. All events timestamped and uploaded via **MQTT protocol** to cloud
8. Farmer views trends on **Grafana/ThingSpeak dashboard**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Units | Application / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Volumetric Water Content (TDR method) | $\theta_v = \dfrac{C \cdot K_a}{2}$ | % or m³/m³ | Soil-moisture sensor calibration |
| 2 | NDVI (Vegetation Index) | $\text{NDVI} = \dfrac{\text{NIR} - \text{Red}}{\text{NIR} + \text{Red}}$ | dimensionless, range [-1, 1] | Crop health from satellite/drone imagery |
| 3 | ETo Reference Evapotranspiration (Hargreaves) | $ETo = 0.0023 \cdot (T_{mean} + 17.8) \cdot \sqrt{T_{max} - T_{min}} \cdot R_a$ | mm/day | Irrigation scheduling |
| 4 | Penman-Monteith (FAO-56) | $ET_c = ETo \cdot K_c$ | mm/day | Crop water requirement |
| 5 | LoRa Path Loss (Log-distance model) | $PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10n \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{d}{d_0}\right) + X_\sigma$ | dB | Wireless range estimation (n = 2–4) |
| 6 | ADC Resolution | $V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{N} - 1}$ | V | Sensor quantization (N = bits) |
| 7 | Soil pH (Nernst equation) | $E = E^0 - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln[H^+]$ | V | pH probe voltage |
| 8 | Link Budget | $P_{RX} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} - PL - L_{sys} + G_{RX}$ | dBm | Communication feasibility |
| 9 | Battery Life Estimate | $T_{life} = \dfrac{C_{batt}}{I_{avg}}$ | hours | Node power budgeting |
| 10 | Duty Cycle (Energy Saving) | $DC = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{sleep}}$ | dimensionless, 0–1 | Sleep-mode optimization |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always quote units. A numerical answer without units in Module-4 IoT problems typically loses **1 mark**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Precision Irrigation:** Saves **30–50%** water compared to flood irrigation
- **Yield Prediction:** ML models on NDVI time-series predict harvest **2–4 weeks** in advance
- **Pest Early Warning:** Image-classification CNNs detect pest attack at **> 90% accuracy**
- **Supply Chain:** IoT-enabled cold-chain monitoring reduces post-harvest losses (currently **~5–16%** in India)
- **Livestock Tracking:** GPS + accelerometer collars detect **lameness, oestrus, theft** in real time
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — NDVI Computation from Multispectral Imagery

The **Normalized Difference Vegetation Index** is the single most-tested formula in KTU Module-4 IoT-agriculture questions.

### Step 1 — Identify Spectral Bands
A typical multispectral sensor (e.g., Sentinel-2, Parrot Sequoia) captures:
- **Red band (R)** — wavelength ≈ **660 nm**
- **Near-Infrared band (NIR)** — wavelength ≈ **840 nm**

### Step 2 — Define Reflectance Values
For a given pixel, the satellite records reflectance $\rho_{NIR}$ and $\rho_{Red}$ as digital numbers. After radiometric calibration:

$$
\rho_{NIR} = \dfrac{DN_{NIR}}{DN_{max,NIR}}
$$

$$
\rho_{Red} = \dfrac{DN_{Red}}{DN_{max,Red}}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the NDVI Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NDVI} &= \dfrac{\rho_{NIR} - \rho_{Red}}{\rho_{NIR} + \rho_{Red}} \\
&= \dfrac{\rho_{NIR} - \rho_{Red}}{\rho_{NIR} + \rho_{Red}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation Boundaries
- $\text{NDVI} < 0$ → Water body
- $0 < \text{NDVI} < 0.2$ → Bare soil / sparse vegetation
- $0.2 < \text{NDVI} < 0.5$ → Moderate vegetation
- $0.5 < \text{NDVI} < 0.8$ → Dense, healthy crop canopy
- $\text{NDVI} > 0.8$ → Very dense tropical forest / peak growth

### Step 5 — Worked Example
A pixel has $DN_{NIR} = 4500$ and $DN_{Red} = 1200$ on a 12-bit sensor (max DN = 4095 — saturated, indicating strong NIR reflectance).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{NIR} &= 4500 / 4095 = 1.099 \text{ (clipped to } 1.0\text{)} \\
\rho_{Red} &= 1200 / 4095 = 0.293 \\
\text{NDVI} &= \dfrac{1.000 - 0.293}{1.000 + 0.293} = \dfrac{0.707}{1.293} = 0.547
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** NDVI = **0.547** indicates **dense, healthy crop canopy** — typical of mature paddy or wheat.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — ADC Resolution and Sensor Quantization

Given a capacitive soil-moisture sensor outputting **0–3.3 V** and an **ESP32 12-bit ADC**:

### Step 1 — Resolution Formula

$$
V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{N} - 1}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{LSB} &= \dfrac{3.3 \, \text{V}}{2^{12} - 1} \\
&= \dfrac{3.3}{4095} \\
&= 8.06 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{V} \\
&= 0.806 \, \text{mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Map ADC Counts to Soil Moisture
Assume the sensor outputs:
- **0.0 V** at **0% VWC** (dry)
- **3.0 V** at **50% VWC** (saturated, field capacity)

**Linearity assumption** (valid for capacitive sensors in mid-range):

$$
\theta_v = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{FS}} \times \theta_{max}
$$

where $V_{FS} = 3.0$ V and $\theta_{max} = 50\%$.

For an ADC reading of **2.1 V**:

$$
\theta_v = \dfrac{2.1}{3.0} \times 50\% = 35\% \, \text{VWC}
$$

This corresponds to a **healthy mid-range moisture** for most crops.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — LoRa Path-Loss Estimation

To determine if a sensor node can communicate with a gateway at distance $d$:

### Step 1 — Reference Path Loss

$$
PL(d_0) = 20\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{4\pi d_0}{\lambda}\right)
$$

For $f = 868$ MHz (Indian LoRa band) → $\lambda = c/f = 3 \times 10^8 / 868 \times 10^6 = 0.345$ m.
At $d_0 = 1$ m:

$$
PL(d_0) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{4\pi \cdot 1}{0.345}\right) = 31.2 \, \text{dB}
$$

### Step 2 — Path Loss at Distance d

$$
PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10n \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{d}{d_0}\right)
$$

For $d = 2000$ m and $n = 2.5$ (crop canopy, leafy environment):

$$
\begin{aligned}
PL(d) &= 31.2 + 10 \cdot 2.5 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{2000}{1}\right) \\
&= 31.2 + 25 \cdot 3.301 \\
&= 31.2 + 82.5 \\
&= 113.7 \, \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Receive Power Check

$$
P_{RX} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - PL
$$

If $P_{TX} = +14$ dBm (LoRa SX1276), $G_{TX} = 2$ dBi, $G_{RX} = 6$ dBi:

$$
P_{RX} = 14 + 2 + 6 - 113.7 = -91.7 \, \text{dBm}
$$

Since LoRa sensitivity at SF12, BW 125 kHz is approximately **−137 dBm**, the link has a **margin of 45.3 dB** — highly reliable.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Communication Protocol Selection

| Parameter | Wi-Fi | ZigBee | LoRaWAN | NB-IoT | Satellite |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Range** | 50–100 m | 10–100 m | 2–15 km | 1–10 km | Global |
| **Data Rate** | 10–100 Mbps | 250 kbps | 0.3–50 kbps | 26 kbps DL / 62 kbps UL | 1–100 kbps |
| **Power** | High | Low | Very Low | Medium | High |
| **Node Cost** | ₹600+ | ₹400 | ₹800 | ₹1200 | ₹3000+ |
| **Best for** | Greenhouse | Intra-field | Wide-area farms | Cellular regions | Remote/rural |
| **KTU Application** | Lab demos | Cluster sensing | Kerala rubber/paddy | Coastal agri | Hilly tracts |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Weightage:** The selection of appropriate communication protocol carries **3–5 marks** in 14-mark Module-4 questions. Always justify using **range vs. power vs. cost** trade-off.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End IoT Agriculture System — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FIELD["FIELD SENSING LAYER"]
        S1["Soil Moisture Sensor"]
        S2["Soil pH and EC Sensor"]
        S3["DHT22 Air Temp and Humidity"]
        S4["NPK Nutrient Sensor"]
        S5["Multispectral Camera"]
        S6["GPS Module"]
        S7["Leaf Wetness Sensor"]
    end

    subgraph EDGE["EDGE PROCESSING LAYER"]
        MCU["ESP32 Microcontroller"]
        ADC["12 bit ADC and Signal Conditioning"]
        LOGIC["Local Decision Logic"]
    end

    subgraph COMMS["COMMUNICATION LAYER"]
        LORA["LoRaWAN SX1276"]
        WIFI["Wi-Fi 802.11 b/g/n"]
        NB["NB-IoT Module"]
        MQTT["MQTT Broker"]
    end

    subgraph CLOUD["CLOUD AND AI LAYER"]
        SERVER["AWS IoT Core"]
        DB["Time Series Database"]
        ML["ML Model for Yield Prediction"]
        DASH["Grafana Dashboard"]
    end

    subgraph ACT["ACTUATION LAYER"]
        V1["Solenoid Valve for Drip"]
        V2["Fertilizer Injector Pump"]
        DR["Agricultural Drone"]
        FAN["Greenhouse Exhaust Fan"]
        ALERT["SMS and Mobile App Alert"]
    end

    S1 --> ADC
    S2 --> ADC
    S3 --> MCU
    S4 --> ADC
    S5 --> MCU
    S6 --> MCU
    S7 --> ADC
    ADC --> MCU
    MCU --> LOGIC
    LOGIC --> LORA
    LOGIC --> WIFI
    LOGIC --> NB
    LORA --> MQTT
    WIFI --> MQTT
    NB --> MQTT
    MQTT --> SERVER
    SERVER --> DB
    DB --> ML
    DB --> DASH
    LOGIC --> V1
    LOGIC --> V2
    ML --> DR
    DASH --> FAN
    DASH --> ALERT
```

## 4.2 Smart Irrigation Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["System Boot and Wi-Fi Connect"] --> B["Read Soil Moisture Sensor"]
    B --> C{"Moisture less than Threshold?"}
    C -->|"Yes"| D["Read Weather API for Rain Forecast"]
    D --> E{"Rain Expected in 6 hours?"}
    E -->|"No"| F["Open Solenoid Valve via Relay"]
    F --> G["Run Pump for Calculated Duration"]
    G --> H["Re-check Moisture every 60 s"]
    H --> C
    E -->|"Yes"| I["Skip Irrigation, Log Event"]
    C -->|"No"| I
    I --> J["Upload Telemetry to Cloud via MQTT"]
    J --> B
```

## 4.3 Livestock Monitoring Sub-system

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph COW["ON-COW MODULE"]
        A1["3-Axis Accelerometer"]
        A2["Body Temperature Sensor"]
        A3["Heart Rate Sensor"]
        A4["GPS Receiver"]
        A5["LoRa Transmitter"]
    end

    subgraph GW["GATEWAY"]
        G1["LoRa Gateway at Farm House"]
        G2["Edge AI for Lameness Detection"]
    end

    subgraph CLOUD["CLOUD"]
        C1["Animal Health Database"]
        C2["SMS Alert to Veterinarian"]
        C3["Mobile App Dashboard"]
    end

    A1 --> A5
    A2 --> A5
    A3 --> A5
    A4 --> A5
    A5 --> G1
    G1 --> G2
    G2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note (for KTU Board Exam):** A block diagram should clearly separate **sensing → communication → processing → actuation**. If any of the four blocks is missing, marks are deducted. Always label arrow directions and signal types (analog/digital/packet).

## 4.4 IoT Agriculture Data Flow Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sensor as Soil Sensor
    participant MCU as ESP32 MCU
    participant GW as LoRa Gateway
    participant Cloud as AWS IoT
    participant App as Mobile App
    participant Valve as Solenoid Valve

    Sensor->>MCU: Analog voltage 0 to 3.3 V
    MCU->>MCU: ADC conversion to digital
    MCU->>MCU: Compare with threshold 25% VWC
    alt Moisture below threshold
        MCU->>Valve: GPIO HIGH, relay energised
        Valve->>Sensor: Water delivered, moisture rises
    end
    MCU->>GW: LoRa packet with sensor ID, value, timestamp
    GW->>Cloud: MQTT publish to topic farm/zone1/moisture
    Cloud->>App: WebSocket push to dashboard
    App->>Farmer: Notification Irrigation Event at 14:30
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define IoT in Agriculture. List any four sensors used in smart farming.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
> IoT in Agriculture is the integration of sensors, communication networks, and cloud/edge computing to monitor and control agricultural parameters in real-time, enabling precision farming and resource optimization.

**Four Sensors (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
1. **Soil Moisture Sensor** — measures volumetric water content (capacitive/resistive type)
2. **DHT22** — measures air temperature and relative humidity
3. **NPK Sensor** — measures soil Nitrogen, Phosphorus, Potassium ion concentrations
4. **Multispectral / RGB Camera** — captures crop imagery for NDVI computation and disease detection

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Explain the role of LPWAN technologies in agricultural IoT. Why is LoRaWAN preferred over Wi-Fi for large farms?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Role of LPWAN (1.5 marks):**
LPWAN (Low-Power Wide-Area Network) technologies like **LoRaWAN, Sigfox, and NB-IoT** enable long-range (**2–15 km**), low-power (**battery life > 2 years**) communication of small data packets (**0.3–50 kbps**) from thousands of in-field sensors.

**LoRaWAN vs Wi-Fi (1.5 marks):**

| Parameter | LoRaWAN | Wi-Fi |
|---|---|---|
| Range | 2–15 km | 50–100 m |
| Battery Life | 2–10 years | Hours to days |
| Hops/Relays needed | None | Many APs required |
| Cost per node | Moderate | High (with infrastructure) |

For large farms spanning **> 100 hectares**, LoRaWAN requires **one gateway** instead of **dozens of Wi-Fi access points**, drastically reducing CAPEX and OPEX.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **four-layer architecture of IoT in agriculture**. Describe the function of each layer in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A farmer wishes to monitor soil moisture in a **2-hectare** coconut plantation. Design an IoT-based system specifying: **(i)** sensor choice, **(ii)** communication protocol, **(iii)** power source, and **(iv)** actuation strategy for drip irrigation. Justify each choice. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

The four-layer architecture is:

**1. Perception Layer (1.75 marks):**
- Consists of physical sensors: soil-moisture probes, temperature/humidity sensors, pH electrodes, NPK sensors, leaf wetness sensors, GPS modules, and multispectral cameras
- Function: Transduce physical, chemical, and biological parameters into electrical signals (analog voltage, current, or digital I²C/SPI data)
- Sensors are typically mounted on **PCB-based motes** with weatherproof IP65/IP67 enclosures

**2. Network / Communication Layer (1.75 marks):**
- Forwards sensor data to gateways/cloud via **wireless protocols**
- Short-range: ZigBee, BLE, Wi-Fi (for greenhouses / indoor farms)
- Long-range LPWAN: LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, Sigfox (for open fields)
- Selection criteria: **range, bandwidth, node density, battery life**

**3. Processing Layer (1.75 marks):**
- **Edge:** Local microcontrollers (ESP32, STM32) handle time-critical decisions
- **Fog:** Local gateways run ML models for image-based pest detection
- **Cloud:** AWS IoT, Azure IoT, ThingSpeak for storage, analytics, dashboarding
- Performs **data fusion, anomaly detection, threshold-based triggers**

**4. Application / Actuation Layer (1.75 marks):**
- HMI: Mobile apps, web dashboards, SMS/voice alerts
- Actuators: Solenoid valves, motor pumps, drone sprayers, climate-control fans
- Closes the **feedback loop** between sensing and physical action

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**System Design for 2-hectare Coconut Plantation:**

**(i) Sensor Choice — Capacitive Soil Moisture Sensor (v1.5):**
- **Justification:** Capacitive sensors are **corrosion-resistant** (unlike resistive), accurate (±2% VWC), and have **long lifespan (> 5 years)**
- Install at **two depths (15 cm and 30 cm)** to profile root-zone moisture
- DHT22 for canopy micro-climate

**(ii) Communication Protocol — LoRaWAN (v1.5):**
- **Justification:** Range 2–15 km with single gateway; battery life **> 3 years** on 2× AA cells; 2 hectares fits within **one LoRa cell**; no monthly SIM cost (unlike NB-IoT)

**(iii) Power Source — 6V/5W Solar PV + 18650 Li-ion (v1.5):**
- **Justification:** Field nodes are off-grid; solar ensures **continuous operation**; lithium cell buffers for nighttime; estimated daily energy: 0.5 Wh (sleep mode) to 5 Wh (active)

**(iv) Actuation Strategy — 12V DC Solenoid Valve + Relay + 0.5 HP Pump (v1.5):**
- **Justification:** Drip irrigation requires **low-pressure (< 1.5 bar)** valves; relay (5V coil, 12V/230V contact) isolates MCU from pump; firmware uses **PID control** to maintain target VWC

**Block Diagram Justification (1 mark):**
Draw a 4-layer block diagram with sensors → LoRa → cloud → valves.

---

### QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Describe the working of a **smart irrigation system** using IoT. Explain how soil moisture threshold, weather API, and actuator control are integrated. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A multispectral camera captures a pixel with $DN_{NIR} = 3850$ and $DN_{Red} = 980$ on a **12-bit** sensor (max DN = 4095). Calculate the **NDVI** and interpret the crop health. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Smart Irrigation System — Working Principle (7 marks):**

**1. Sensing Subsystem (2 marks):**
- Capacitive soil moisture sensors installed at multiple zones (Zone A, B, C) of the field
- Each sensor connected to **ESP32 microcontroller** with 12-bit ADC
- ADC resolution: $V_{LSB} = 3.3 / 4095 = 0.806$ mV
- Sampling rate: **once per 60 seconds**

**2. Threshold-Based Logic (2 marks):**
- Firmware compares reading with crop-specific threshold (e.g., **25% VWC** for paddy)
- If reading < threshold → microcontroller sets GPIO HIGH → relay energizes → solenoid valve opens
- If reading ≥ threshold → valve closes (hysteresis of 2% to avoid chattering)
- **PID controller** fine-tunes valve timing for precision

**3. Weather API Integration (1.5 marks):**
- ESP32 queries **OpenWeatherMap API** every 6 hours
- If rain forecast within next 6 hours → irrigation is **skipped** (saves water)
- Otherwise, irrigation proceeds with calculated run-time based on ETo (Reference Evapotranspiration)

**4. Cloud and Dashboard (1 mark):**
- All events published via **MQTT** to ThingSpeak/AWS IoT
- Farmer receives **SMS + mobile-app push notification**
- Historical data visualized as **time-series charts and heat-maps**

**5. Closed-Loop Feedback (0.5 marks):**
- System continuously self-corrects — typical water savings: **30–50%** compared to flood irrigation

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Given Data:**
- Sensor: 12-bit (max DN = 4095)
- $DN_{NIR} = 3850$
- $DN_{Red} = 980$

**Step 1 — Compute Reflectance Values (2 marks):**

$$
\rho_{NIR} = \dfrac{3850}{4095} = 0.9402
$$

$$
\rho_{Red} = \dfrac{980}{4095} = 0.2393
$$

**Step 2 — Apply NDVI Formula (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NDVI} &= \dfrac{\rho_{NIR} - \rho_{Red}}{\rho_{NIR} + \rho_{Red}} \\
&= \dfrac{0.9402 - 0.2393}{0.9402 + 0.2393} \\
&= \dfrac{0.7009}{1.1795} \\
&= 0.5943
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation (2 marks):**

$\text{NDVI} = 0.5943$ falls in the range **0.5 < NDVI < 0.8**, which indicates **dense, healthy crop canopy** with strong photosynthetic activity. This is consistent with a well-irrigated, disease-free mid-growth-stage paddy or wheat field.

**No irrigation deficiency indicated.** Farmer may maintain current schedule without intervention.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in IoT-Agriculture Questions (KTU 2024):**
> 
> 1. **Missing units** — losing 1 mark (e.g., writing "NDVI = 0.59" without stating it is dimensionless)
> 2. **Confusing NDVI formula** — writing (NIR + Red)/(NIR - Red) — wrong sign in numerator; full 3 marks lost
> 3. **Omitting communication protocol justification** — listing LoRaWAN without explaining why over Wi-Fi = −2 marks
> 4. **Forgetting power-budgeting** in field-node design — sensor choice without energy analysis = −1 mark
> 5. **Drawing block diagram without arrow labels** — unlabelled flow direction = −1 mark
> 6. **Not mentioning MQTT/HTTP protocol** when describing cloud upload — partial 1 mark lost
> 7. **Conflating perception and network layers** — the sensor's *output* is a signal, *not* data on the network; examiners penalize this

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ IoT in Agriculture = **Smart Farming / Precision Agriculture / AgriTech 4.0**
- ✅ Standard architecture: **Perception → Network → Processing → Application (4 layers)**
- ✅ Key sensors: **Soil moisture, DHT22, NPK, pH, EC, GPS, multispectral camera**
- ✅ Key communication: **LoRaWAN** (long-range), **ZigBee** (intra-field), **Wi-Fi** (greenhouse)
- ✅ Key protocols: **MQTT** (lightweight pub/sub), **HTTP/HTTPS** (REST), **CoAP** (constrained)
- ✅ Key formula: **NDVI = (NIR − Red) / (NIR + Red)**, range **−1 to +1**
- ✅ Key formula: **ADC $V_{LSB}$ = $V_{ref}$ / $(2^N − 1)$**
- ✅ Smart irrigation saves **30–50%** water
- ✅ LPWAN battery life: **> 2 years** typical
- ✅ Cloud platforms: **AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT, ThingSpeak**
- ✅ Kerala-specific relevance: **coconut, rubber, paddy, cardamom, pepper, areca nut** — all suitable for IoT pilots
- ✅ Government initiative: **PMKSY** (Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchayee Yojana) supports precision-irrigation IoT deployment
- ✅ Draw block diagrams with **clearly labelled arrows** in every 14-mark answer
- ✅ Always mention **threshold + hysteresis** in control loops (avoids relay chattering)
- ✅ For KTU viva: be ready to discuss **edge vs. cloud computing trade-offs**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Artificial Intelligence in Agricultural Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT864 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Artificial Intelligence in Agricultural Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Artificial Intelligence (AI) in Agricultural Systems** refers to the integration of computational intelligence techniques—including **machine learning (ML)**, **deep learning (DL)**, **computer vision**, **fuzzy logic**, and **expert systems**—with mechatronic hardware (sensors, actuators, microcontrollers, and robotic platforms) to enable autonomous perception, reasoning, decision-making, and actuation across the agricultural value chain (soil → crop → harvest → post-harvest).

The system architecture follows a classical **perception–cognition–action** triad:

$$
\text{AI Agricultural System} = f(\text{Sensors}, \text{Data Layer}, \text{Decision Engine}, \text{Actuators})
$$

Where the **Decision Engine** encapsulates AI algorithms that map noisy, high-dimensional sensor inputs to optimal agronomic actions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Farm Manager"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine hiring an agronomist who works **24/7**, never sleeps, observes **every plant** through a camera-eye, consults a memory of **10 million past harvests**, speaks **every language** (weather data, soil chemistry, satellite imagery), and commands a fleet of robotic arms to fix problems instantly. That is exactly what an **AI-driven agricultural system** does — it is the *digital brain* of **Precision Agriculture (PA)**.

Just as a human farmer walks the field, sees a yellowing leaf, infers nitrogen deficiency, and applies fertilizer — an AI system:

| Human Farmer Action | AI Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Visually inspects crop | **Camera + CNN image classifier** |
| Recalls past experience | **Trained ML model on historical data** |
| Decides fertilizer dose | **Regression / Reinforcement Learning policy** |
| Sprays the field | **Variable-rate actuator / drone swarm** |

## 1.3 Core Sub-Domains of AI in Agriculture (KTU High-Yield)

1. **Precision Agriculture (PA)** — site-specific crop management.
2. **Computer Vision Systems** — weed, disease, pest, and maturity detection.
3. **Predictive Analytics** — yield forecasting and weather modeling.
4. **Autonomous Robotics** — self-driving tractors, robotic harvesters, UAVs.
5. **Smart Irrigation** — AI-controlled drip/micro-jet systems.
6. **Decision Support Systems (DSS)** — expert-system-based agronomic advisory.
7. **IoT–AI Edge Computing** — real-time field analytics.

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

- **Classification Accuracy:** $A = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$
- **Precision:** $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$
- **Recall (Sensitivity):** $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$
- **F1-Score:** $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$
- **Mean Average Precision (mAP):** Standard for object detection in crop imaging.
- **Intersection over Union (IoU):** $\text{IoU} = \dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$

> [!NOTE]
> The standard dataset benchmark for plant disease classification is **PlantVillage**, containing **54,306 images** across **38 disease classes** across **14 crop species**. It is the de-facto KTU reference benchmark for CNN-based agricultural vision.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mechatronic Closed-Loop Pipeline in Smart Farming

A mechatronic agricultural AI system operates as a **closed-loop feedback control system**. The pipeline is:

1. **Perception Layer (Sensors)**
   - Multi-spectral / hyper-spectral cameras.
   - **NDVI (Normalized Difference Vegetation Index)** sensors.
   - Soil moisture probes (capacitive / TDR — Time Domain Reflectometry).
   - Weather stations (temperature, humidity, wind, rainfall).
   - LiDAR and ultrasonic ranging for obstacle detection.

2. **Data Layer (Acquisition + Preprocessing)**
   - Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC) at **12-bit or 16-bit** resolution.
   - Noise filtering: Kalman filter, moving average, wavelet denoising.
   - Data fusion using **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** or **Particle Filter**.

3. **Cognition Layer (AI Engine)**
   - Supervised learning: SVM, Random Forest, CNN, ResNet, YOLO.
   - Unsupervised learning: K-Means, DBSCAN for weed clustering.
   - Reinforcement learning: irrigation scheduling, autonomous navigation.
   - Fuzzy Logic Controllers (FLC) for non-linear, rule-based decisions.

4. **Action Layer (Actuators)**
   - Variable-rate fertilizer applicators.
   - Drip irrigation solenoid valves.
   - Robotic manipulators (e.g., 6-DOF arm for fruit picking).
   - UAVs / drones for pesticide spraying.

## 2.2 Artificial Neural Network (ANN) — The Brain of the System

An **Artificial Neural Network (ANN)** is a computational model inspired by biological neurons. The mathematical model of a single artificial neuron:

$$
y = \phi\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b\right)
$$

Where:
- $x_i$ = input features (e.g., soil moisture, temperature, leaf color).
- $w_i$ = synaptic weights (learned during training).
- $b$ = bias term.
- $\phi(\cdot)$ = activation function (ReLU, Sigmoid, Tanh).
- $y$ = neuron output (decision signal).

### Common Activation Functions

| Function | Formula | Range | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sigmoid | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | $(0,1)$ | Binary classification (healthy/diseased) |
| Tanh | $\tanh(z) = \dfrac{e^z - e^{-z}}{e^z + e^{-z}}$ | $(-1,1)$ | Hidden layer (zero-centered) |
| ReLU | $\text{ReLU}(z) = \max(0,z)$ | $[0,\infty)$ | Deep CNNs (default) |
| Softmax | $\sigma(z)_j = \dfrac{e^{z_j}}{\sum_k e^{z_k}}$ | $(0,1)$, sum=1 | Multi-class crop disease |

### Backpropagation Learning Rule

$$
w_{ij}^{(t+1)} = w_{ij}^{(t)} - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial L}{\partial w_{ij}}
$$

Where:
- $\eta$ = learning rate (typically $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-5}$).
- $L$ = loss function (e.g., Cross-Entropy Loss).

## 2.3 Fuzzy Logic Controller (FLC) for Smart Irrigation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fuzzy Logic** is heavily tested in KTU Mechatronics papers because it bridges symbolic reasoning (rules) and numerical control. It is the **go-to AI method** for irrigation, greenhouse climate, and autonomous steering.

A FLC has three stages:

$$
\text{Crisp Input} \xrightarrow{\text{Fuzzification}} \text{Fuzzy Set} \xrightarrow{\text{Rule Engine}} \text{Fuzzy Output} \xrightarrow{\text{Defuzzification}} \text{Crisp Output}
$$

**Example: Smart Irrigation FLC**

- **Inputs (Crisp):** Soil moisture $M$ (%), Temperature $T$ (°C).
- **Fuzzy Sets:** Dry, Moist, Wet (for $M$); Cold, Warm, Hot (for $T$).
- **Output:** Valve opening duration $V$ (seconds).
- **Sample Rule:** `IF Moisture IS Dry AND Temperature IS Hot THEN Valve_Open IS Long.`

**Centroid Defuzzification Formula:**

$$
V^{*} = \frac{\int \mu_V(v) \cdot v \, dv}{\int \mu_V(v) \, dv}
$$

## 2.4 Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for Crop Disease Detection

A CNN processes image inputs through:

$$
\text{Conv} \rightarrow \text{Pooling} \rightarrow \text{Fully Connected} \rightarrow \text{Softmax}
$$

**Convolution Operation (2D):**

$$
S(i,j) = (K * I)(i,j) = \sum_{m} \sum_{n} I(i+m, j+n) \cdot K(m,n)
$$

**Max Pooling (downsampling):**

$$
P(i,j) = \max_{m,n \in \text{window}} I(i+m, j+n)
$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat-Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **high-yield formula table** for AI in Agriculture questions in KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize these.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Neuron Output | $y = \phi\left(\sum w_i x_i + b\right)$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Sigmoid | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | Output in $(0,1)$ |
| 3 | Softmax | $\sigma_j = \dfrac{e^{z_j}}{\sum_k e^{z_k}}$ | Multi-class probability |
| 4 | ReLU | $\max(0,z)$ | Deep learning default |
| 5 | Cross-Entropy Loss | $L = -\sum y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$ | Classification loss |
| 6 | Backprop Update | $w := w - \eta \nabla L$ | Gradient descent |
| 7 | Convolution | $S(i,j) = \sum_m \sum_n I_{i+m,j+n} K_{m,n}$ | Image features |
| 8 | NDVI | $\text{NDVI} = \dfrac{\text{NIR} - \text{Red}}{\text{NIR} + \text{Red}}$ | Range $[-1, 1]$ |
| 9 | IoU | $\dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | Object detection |
| 10 | F1-Score | $2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Imbalanced classes |
| 11 | Centroid Defuzzification | $V^{*} = \dfrac{\int \mu(v) v \, dv}{\int \mu(v) dv}$ | Fuzzy output |
| 12 | Kalman Update (State) | $x_{k \vert k} = x_{k \vert k-1} + K_k(z_k - H x_{k \vert k-1})$ | Sensor fusion |
| 13 | PID Controller | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \dfrac{de}{dt}$ | Actuator control |
| 14 | Yield Prediction (Linear Reg.) | $\hat{Y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \cdots$ | tonnes/hectare |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Yield forecasting** saves 15–25% input cost for farmers.
- **AI-driven weed detection** reduces herbicide use by up to **80%**.
- **Smart irrigation** saves up to **40% water** vs. conventional flooding.
- **Disease early-warning** can prevent **30–50% crop loss** in vegetables.
- **Autonomous tractors** improve field efficiency by **20%** and reduce operator fatigue.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Backpropagation Weight Update for Crop Disease ANN

We will derive the gradient of the **Mean Squared Error (MSE) loss** with respect to a weight $w_{ij}$ in a 2-layer network used to classify a leaf as **Healthy** or **Diseased**.

### Step 1: Define the Network Forward Pass

Let input be $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2]^T$ (e.g., green-channel intensity, lesion area). The hidden layer output:

$$
h_j = \phi\left(\sum_i w_{ij}^{(1)} x_i + b_j^{(1)}\right)
$$

The final output (sigmoid for binary classification):

$$
\hat{y} = \sigma\left(\sum_j w_j^{(2)} h_j + b^{(2)}\right)
$$

**Logic:** The input features propagate through a hidden layer with activation $\phi$, then through a sigmoid output neuron that produces a probability of disease.

### Step 2: Define the Loss Function

Using **Mean Squared Error** for the training set of $N$ samples:

$$
L = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} \left(y_k - \hat{y}_k\right)^2
$$

**Logic:** MSE penalizes the squared difference between true label $y_k$ (0 or 1) and predicted probability $\hat{y}_k$.

### Step 3: Compute the Gradient w.r.t. Output-Layer Weight $w_j^{(2)}$

Apply the **chain rule**:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_j^{(2)}} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z^{(2)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z^{(2)}}{\partial w_j^{(2)}}
$$

Where $z^{(2)} = \sum_j w_j^{(2)} h_j + b^{(2)}$ is the pre-activation.

Compute each partial:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} = -2(y - \hat{y})
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z^{(2)}} = \hat{y}(1 - \hat{y}) \quad \text{(sigmoid derivative)}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial z^{(2)}}{\partial w_j^{(2)}} = h_j
$$

Combine:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_j^{(2)}} = -2(y - \hat{y}) \cdot \hat{y}(1 - \hat{y}) \cdot h_j
$$

**Logic:** The error signal flows backward. A larger error $(y - \hat{y})$ and a more confident wrong prediction (saturated sigmoid) produces a stronger weight update.

### Step 4: Gradient Descent Update Rule

$$
w_j^{(2), \text{new}} = w_j^{(2), \text{old}} - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial L}{\partial w_j^{(2)}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
w_j^{(2), \text{new}} = w_j^{(2), \text{old}} + 2\eta (y - \hat{y}) \hat{y}(1 - \hat{y}) h_j
$$

**Logic:** The weight is nudged in the direction that reduces the error, scaled by the learning rate $\eta$ and the activation $h_j$.

### Step 5: Numerical Worked Example

**Given:**
- True label: $y = 1$ (diseased leaf).
- Predicted: $\hat{y} = 0.3$.
- Hidden activation: $h_1 = 0.7$.
- Old weight: $w_1^{(2)} = 0.5$.
- Learning rate: $\eta = 0.1$.

**Compute error signal:**

$$
\delta = (y - \hat{y}) \hat{y}(1 - \hat{y}) = (1 - 0.3)(0.3)(0.7) = 0.7 \times 0.21 = 0.147
$$

**Compute gradient:**

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_1^{(2)}} = -2(0.147)(0.7) = -0.2058
$$

**Compute new weight:**

$$
w_1^{(2), \text{new}} = 0.5 - 0.1 \times (-0.2058) = 0.5 + 0.02058 = 0.5206
$$

**Logic:** Since the model under-predicted disease (0.3 vs. 1), the weight connecting the active hidden neuron to the output was **increased**, which will raise $\hat{y}$ on the next pass.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: NDVI from Multi-spectral Imagery

The **Normalized Difference Vegetation Index** quantifies plant greenness from satellite/UAV imagery.

### Step 1: Spectral Bands

- **NIR** = reflectance in Near-Infrared band (~840 nm).
- **Red** = reflectance in Red band (~670 nm).

### Step 2: Formula

$$
\text{NDVI} = \frac{\text{NIR} - \text{Red}}{\text{NIR} + \text{Red}}
$$

**Logic:** Healthy vegetation strongly reflects NIR (due to spongy mesophyll) and absorbs Red (chlorophyll absorption). The ratio hence correlates with biomass.

### Step 3: Worked Numerical Example

**Given multispectral UAV pixel data:**
- NIR reflectance = 0.52
- Red reflectance = 0.08

$$
\text{NDVI} = \frac{0.52 - 0.08}{0.52 + 0.08} = \frac{0.44}{0.60} \approx 0.733
$$

**Interpretation:**

| NDVI Range | Vegetation Status |
|---|---|
| $< 0.1$ | Bare soil / water |
| $0.1 - 0.3$ | Sparse vegetation |
| $0.3 - 0.6$ | Moderate / stressed crop |
| $0.6 - 0.9$ | **Healthy, dense canopy** |
| $> 0.9$ | Very dense / tropical forest |

**Logic:** The computed NDVI of **0.733** indicates a **healthy, dense canopy** — consistent with a well-irrigated paddy or wheat field at peak growth.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: AI Method vs. Agricultural Application

| AI Technique | Agricultural Problem | Input Data | Output | KTU Bloom Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CNN (ResNet-50) | Plant disease classification | Leaf RGB image | 38-class label | L3 (Apply) |
| YOLOv8 | Real-time weed detection | Drone video frame | Bounding box + class | L4 (Analyze) |
| Random Forest | Yield prediction | Weather + soil + historical | Yield (t/ha) | L5 (Evaluate) |
| Fuzzy Logic | Smart irrigation control | Moisture + temperature | Valve open time (s) | L4 (Analyze) |
| Reinforcement Learning | Autonomous tractor path | GPS + LiDAR | Steering angle | L6 (Create) |
| SVM | Seed quality sorting | Spectral / morphological | Grade A/B/C | L3 (Apply) |
| LSTM / RNN | Weather forecasting | Time-series sensor | Future rainfall | L4 (Analyze) |
| K-Means Clustering | Soil zone mapping | pH, EC, NPK | Zone labels | L3 (Apply) |
| Expert System | Pest advisory | Symptoms + images | Recommendation text | L5 (Evaluate) |
| ANFIS | Greenhouse climate | Temp, humidity, CO₂ | Fan / heater control | L6 (Create) |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*Define Artificial Intelligence as applied to agricultural systems. List any four AI techniques commonly used in precision farming.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> [!NOTE]
> **AI in Agriculture** is the application of computational intelligence algorithms to autonomously perceive, reason, and act upon agricultural data. Four commonly used AI techniques are:
> 1. **Artificial Neural Networks (ANN)** – for yield and disease prediction.
> 2. **Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN)** – for plant disease image classification.
> 3. **Fuzzy Logic Systems** – for irrigation and greenhouse climate control.
> 4. **Reinforcement Learning** – for autonomous tractor navigation.
>
> *(Each technique: 0.5 mark + 0.5 mark for definition = 3 marks total.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*What is NDVI? State its formula and explain how it is used in precision agriculture.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **NDVI (Normalized Difference Vegetation Index)** is a remote-sensing metric that quantifies vegetation health by comparing the reflectance of **Near-Infrared (NIR)** and **Red** light bands.
>
> **Formula:**
> $$\text{NDVI} = \frac{\text{NIR} - \text{Red}}{\text{NIR} + \text{Red}}$$
>
> **Use:** NDVI maps (from satellite or UAV) help farmers identify **stressed crop zones**, schedule variable-rate irrigation/fertilization, and monitor phenological stages. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for use.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part B questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice. We provide **Question A** and **Question B** as fully independent alternatives.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — AI in Disease Detection + Fuzzy Irrigation**

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]**
*With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a CNN-based automated plant disease detection system. Explain each layer's role. Mention the PlantVillage dataset.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: System Block Diagram (2 marks)**

The CNN-based plant disease detection pipeline:

1. **Image Acquisition** (smartphone or UAV camera, RGB, 256×256 px)
2. **Preprocessing** (resizing, normalization, augmentation: rotation, flip, color jitter)
3. **Convolutional Layers** (multiple stacked layers, e.g., ResNet-50 with 50 layers)
4. **Pooling Layers** (max-pooling for spatial down-sampling)
5. **Fully Connected (Dense) Layers** (flatten + 2 dense layers of 512 neurons)
6. **Softmax Output Layer** (38 neurons for 38 classes of PlantVillage)

**Step 2: Layer-wise Role Explanation (3 marks)**

| Layer | Role | Output Size (example) |
|---|---|---|
| Input | Holds RGB image | $224 \times 224 \times 3$ |
| Conv 1 | Detects edges, color blobs | $224 \times 224 \times 64$ |
| Pool 1 | Downsamples by 2 | $112 \times 112 \times 64$ |
| Conv 2–5 | Detects textures, leaf veins, lesions | $14 \times 14 \times 512$ |
| Pool 5 | Final pooling | $7 \times 7 \times 512$ |
| Flatten | Vectorizes feature map | $1 \times 25088$ |
| Dense | High-level reasoning | $1 \times 512$ |
| Softmax | Class probability | $1 \times 38$ |

**Step 3: PlantVillage Dataset Mention (2 marks)**
The **PlantVillage dataset** contains **54,306 leaf images** spanning **38 disease–crop combinations** across **14 species** (tomato, potato, grape, apple, corn, etc.). It is the standard benchmark, with state-of-the-art CNNs achieving **>99% accuracy**.

**Mathematical note (1 mark):**
The convolution operation is:

$$
S(i,j) = \sum_{m=0}^{M-1} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} I(i+m, j+n) \cdot K(m,n)
$$

where $K$ is the learned kernel, $I$ is the input image.

---

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]**
*Design a Fuzzy Logic Controller (FLC) for a smart irrigation system. The inputs are soil moisture (Dry/Moist/Wet) and temperature (Cold/Warm/Hot). The output is valve opening time (Short/Medium/Long). Define the membership functions, list the rule base, and compute the defuzzified output for: Moisture = 55% (Moist = 0.6, Wet = 0.4) and Temperature = 32 °C (Warm = 0.4, Hot = 0.6).*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Define Linguistic Variables and Membership Functions (2 marks)**

| Variable | Range | Fuzzy Sets |
|---|---|---|
| Moisture $M$ | 0–100 % | Dry (0–40), Moist (30–70), Wet (60–100) |
| Temperature $T$ | 0–50 °C | Cold (0–20), Warm (15–35), Hot (30–50) |
| Valve Time $V$ | 0–60 s | Short (0–20), Medium (15–45), Long (40–60) |

Use triangular membership functions for simplicity.

**Step 2: Rule Base (3 × 3 = 9 rules, 1.5 marks)**

| M \ T | Cold | Warm | Hot |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dry** | Medium | Long | Long |
| **Moist** | Short | Medium | Long |
| **Wet** | Short | Short | Medium |

**Step 3: Firing Strengths for the Given Crisp Inputs (1.5 marks)**

Given: Moist = 0.6, Wet = 0.4; Warm = 0.4, Hot = 0.6.

Active rules (firing strength = min of antecedent memberships):

- Rule 1: Moist ∧ Warm → Medium ; strength = $\min(0.6, 0.4) = 0.4$
- Rule 2: Moist ∧ Hot → Long ; strength = $\min(0.6, 0.6) = 0.6$
- Rule 3: Wet ∧ Warm → Short ; strength = $\min(0.4, 0.4) = 0.4$
- Rule 4: Wet ∧ Hot → Medium ; strength = $\min(0.4, 0.6) = 0.4$

**Step 4: Defuzzification using Centroid Method (2 marks)**

Using representative singletons for output sets: Short = 10 s, Medium = 30 s, Long = 50 s.

$$
V^{*} = \frac{(0.4)(30) + (0.6)(50) + (0.4)(10) + (0.4)(30)}{(0.4) + (0.6) + (0.4) + (0.4)}
$$

$$
V^{*} = \frac{12 + 30 + 4 + 12}{1.8} = \frac{58}{1.8} \approx 32.2 \text{ s}
$$

**Final Answer:** The valve should open for approximately **32 seconds** — a medium-to-long irrigation pulse consistent with moderately moist soil and hot weather.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ANN Theory + Smart Agriculture System**

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]**
*Explain the structure of an Artificial Neural Network (ANN). With a suitable diagram, describe the working of a single artificial neuron. Derive the mathematical model and explain the backpropagation algorithm for a 2-layer network used in crop yield prediction.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: ANN Architecture (2 marks)**

An **Artificial Neural Network** consists of:
- **Input Layer:** receives feature vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ (e.g., rainfall, soil pH, fertilizer dose, temperature).
- **Hidden Layer(s):** one or more layers of neurons that learn non-linear combinations.
- **Output Layer:** produces prediction $\hat{y}$ (e.g., predicted yield in t/ha).

**Step 2: Single Neuron Model (2 marks)**

The mathematical model of an artificial neuron is given by:

$$
y = \phi\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b\right)
$$

Where:
- $x_i$: input feature $i$.
- $w_i$: synaptic weight $i$.
- $b$: bias term.
- $\phi$: non-linear activation function (sigmoid, ReLU, tanh).
- $y$: neuron output.

**Step 3: Backpropagation for 2-Layer Network (3 marks)**

Network: Input → Hidden (sigmoid) → Output (linear, regression).

Forward pass for sample $k$:

$$
h_j = \sigma\left(\sum_i w_{ij}^{(1)} x_i + b_j^{(1)}\right)
$$

$$
\hat{y}_k = \sum_j w_j^{(2)} h_j + b^{(2)}
$$

Loss (MSE):

$$
L = \frac{1}{2}(y_k - \hat{y}_k)^2
$$

Gradient at output layer:

$$
\delta^{(2)} = -(y_k - \hat{y}_k)
$$

Gradient at hidden layer:

$$
\delta_j^{(1)} = \delta^{(2)} \cdot w_j^{(2)} \cdot h_j (1 - h_j)
$$

Weight updates:

$$
w_j^{(2)} \leftarrow w_j^{(2)} - \eta \delta^{(2)} h_j
$$

$$
w_{ij}^{(1)} \leftarrow w_{ij}^{(1)} - \eta \delta_j^{(1)} x_i
$$

**Logic:** Errors are propagated **backward** from output to input, and weights are adjusted to reduce prediction error. Over many epochs, the network learns the mapping from agronomic inputs to yield.

---

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]**
*Draw the block diagram of a mechatronic AI-based smart agriculture system. Explain the function of each block. Discuss two real-world applications: (i) Autonomous Tractor, (ii) UAV-based Crop Spraying.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Block Diagram (3 marks)**

The four-layer mechatronic architecture: **Perception → Data → Cognition → Action** (refer to the diagrams in Section 4.1).

**Step 2: Block Function Description (2 marks)**

- **Perception:** Cameras (RGB, multispectral), soil sensors, weather station, GPS, LiDAR.
- **Data Layer:** ADC, signal conditioning, Kalman filter, edge computing (Jetson Nano / Raspberry Pi).
- **Cognition:** CNN classifier, fuzzy controller, ML yield model, RL path planner.
- **Action:** Drone sprayer, robotic arm, solenoid valve, autonomous tractor.

**Step 3: Application (i) — Autonomous Tractor (1 mark)**

Uses **GPS + RTK (Real-Time Kinematic)**, **LiDAR**, and **stereo cameras** for centimeter-accurate navigation. A **Reinforcement Learning** or **A\* path planner** computes optimal trajectories. Onboard CNN detects weeds in real time, triggering **variable-rate sprayers** — reducing herbicide use by up to **80%**. John Deere's 8R autonomous tractor is a production example.

**Step 4: Application (ii) — UAV-based Crop Spraying (1 mark)**

Multirotor drones (e.g., DJI Agras T50) carry **10–40 L tanks** and fly at **1–3 m altitude**. They use **multispectral cameras** to first scan the field and generate an **NDVI prescription map**. The AI engine then commands **variable-rate nozzles** to spray pesticides only where needed. Coverage rate: **10–15 ha/hour** with 30–50% pesticide savings.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in AI-in-Agriculture Answers:**
>
> 1. **❌ No Block Diagram:** KTU examiners award **2–3 marks** purely for a neat, labeled block diagram of the mechatronic system. Skipping it = guaranteed 0 for that component.
> 2. **❌ Forgetting Units:** Always state units (e.g., "valve time = **32 seconds**", not just "32"). Marks are deducted for unit omission.
> 3. **❌ Skipping the Defuzzification Math:** In FLC questions, you must show the **centroid calculation explicitly** with the integral/sum expanded. Writing only the rule base = partial credit only.
> 4. **❌ Vague Layer Names in CNN:** Don't just say "CNN". Specify: *Conv → ReLU → MaxPool → … → Dense → Softmax* with **output dimensions**.
> 5. **❌ No Dataset Mention:** For image-classification answers, always name the **PlantVillage** dataset (54,306 images, 38 classes). This is a frequently-tested detail.
> 6. **❌ Confusing AI vs. IoT:** AI = decision-making (the brain). IoT = data collection (the nervous system). Examiners deduct marks for conflating the two.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — AI in Agricultural Systems**

### 🔹 Core Concepts
- AI in Agriculture = **Perception + Cognition + Action** mechatronic triad.
- Subsumes: **Precision Agriculture, Smart Irrigation, Disease Detection, Autonomous Robotics, Yield Prediction**.

### 🔹 Key Algorithms
- **ANN** — for tabular agronomic data (yield, soil).
- **CNN** — for leaf disease image classification (PlantVillage benchmark).
- **Fuzzy Logic** — for irrigation, greenhouse climate (rule-based, interpretable).
- **Reinforcement Learning** — for autonomous tractor and robot navigation.
- **LSTM/RNN** — for weather time-series forecasting.

### 🔹 Formula Flashcards
- **Neuron:** $y = \phi(\sum w_i x_i + b)$
- **Sigmoid:** $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$
- **NDVI:** $\dfrac{\text{NIR} - \text{Red}}{\text{NIR} + \text{Red}}$
- **Centroid Defuzzification:** $V^* = \dfrac{\int \mu(v) v\, dv}{\int \mu(v)\, dv}$
- **Backprop Update:** $w := w - \eta \nabla L$
- **Convolution:** $S = K * I$

### 🔹 Standard Datasets
- **PlantVillage** — 54,306 images, 38 classes, 14 crops.
- **WeedMap** — for weed detection.
- **CropNet / AgriNet** — for yield prediction.

### 🔹 Standard Sensors
- Capacitive soil moisture, TDR, multispectral, NDVI, LiDAR, IMU, GPS/RTK.

### 🔹 High-Yield Keywords for Theory Answers
- *Closed-loop control*, *edge computing*, *digital twin*, *site-specific crop management (SSCM)*, *variable-rate technology (VRT)*, *expert system*, *knowledge base*, *inference engine*, *backpropagation*, *gradient descent*, *overfitting*, *data augmentation*.

### 🔹 Numericals to Practice
- NDVI computation from given NIR & Red values.
- Defuzzified output via centroid for 2-input 1-output FLC.
- 2-step backprop weight update for a 2-2-1 ANN.
- Confusion matrix → Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1.

### 🔹 Bloom-Level Distribution (KTU 2024)
- L1–L2 (Remember/Understand) → 20% marks.
- L3–L4 (Apply/Analyze) → 50% marks (numericals + diagrams).
- L5–L6 (Evaluate/Create) → 30% marks (system design, comparison).
